title: MemSlides: A Hierarchical Memory Driven Agent Framework for Personalized Slide Generation with Multi-turn Local Revision
comment: Code, website, project page, and video are linked in the paper
summary: Personalized presentation generation requires more than conditioning on a current prompt or template: agents must preserve stable user preferences across tasks, retain newly introduced preferences and constraints during multi-turn revision, and carry out local edits reliably. We propose MemSlides, a hierarchical memory framework for personalized presentation agents that separates long-term memory from working memory and further divides long-term memory into user profile memory and tool memory. User profile memory stores intent-conditioned profiles for round-0 personalization, working memory carries active preferences and session constraints across revision rounds, and tool memory stores reusable execution experience for reliable localized editing. MemSlides pairs this memory design with scoped slide-local revision, so targeted updates act on the smallest affected region instead of repeatedly regenerating the full deck. In controlled experiments, user profile memory improves persona-alignment judgments on a multi-persona, multi-intent profile bank, tool-memory injection improves closed-loop modify behavior in diagnostic matched-pair settings, and qualitative cases illustrate working memory's ability to carryover preferences. Taken together, these results suggest that effective personalization in presentation authoring depends on separating persistent user profiles, session-level working memory, and reusable execution experience across generation and localized revision.

AI.tldr: MemSlides introduces a hierarchical memory framework for personalized slide-generation agents, separating long-term user profiles, session working memory, and tool experience to improve stable personalization and reliable local edits.
AI.motivation: Existing presentation-generation systems struggle to maintain stable, long-term user preferences, integrate new constraints over multi-turn revisions, and perform precise local edits without regenerating entire decks. The paper aims to design a memory structure that supports persistent personalization, revision carryover, and robust localized modifications in slide authoring agents.
AI.method: The authors design MemSlides, a hierarchical memory framework with three key components: (1) user profile memory, which stores intent-conditioned user profiles for initial (round-0) personalization; (2) working memory, which tracks active preferences and constraints within a session to support multi-turn revision; and (3) tool memory, which logs reusable execution experiences to guide reliable localized edits. They integrate this memory framework with slide-local revision mechanisms so that edits are applied only to the minimal affected slide regions rather than regenerating whole decks. They evaluate the components via controlled experiments on persona-alignment using a multi-persona, multi-intent profile bank, diagnostic matched-pair tests of modify behavior, and qualitative case studies of preference carryover.
AI.result: User profile memory significantly improves persona-alignment judgments in controlled tests, indicating better adherence to user personas across different intents. Tool-memory injection enhances closed-loop modification behavior, leading to more accurate and reliable local edits in matched-pair diagnostic settings. Qualitative analyses show that working memory effectively carries over preferences and constraints across multiple revision rounds within a session.
AI.conclusion: Separating memory into persistent user profiles, session-level working memory, and reusable tool-execution experience leads to more effective personalization and reliable localized revision in presentation authoring agents. The study concludes that hierarchical, functionally specialized memory is crucial for stable preference preservation, constraint carryover, and precise slide-level editing in personalized presentation generation systems.

title: PromptMN: Pseudo Prompting Language
summary: Prompting has become the primary interface between humans and generative AI, yet many natural language prompts remain fragile: roles, goals, constraints, and expected outputs are often buried in prose or left implicit. In agentic and software development workflows, a misread at the first handoff can propagate through every step, since a significant portion of agent failures stem from context ambiguities rather than model limitations. This paper introduces PromptMN, a pseudo-prompting domain-specific language that annotates natural language with compact, %-prefixed typed directives covering roles, goals, requirements, priorities, constraints, plans, inputs, and outputs. Semantic resolution lets authors write in any order while the model interprets directives by function. PromptMN sits between informal prompting and programming-style pseudocode: structured enough to be inspectable and reusable, yet lightweight enough for analysts, managers, developers, and stakeholders across the software development lifecycle (SDLC). PromptMN also pairs with reverse prompt engineering. Asking a model to restate a desired outcome as PromptMN lets users inspect the inferred roles, goals, constraints, and missing assumptions before acting, reducing repair cycles and yielding a reusable artifact for aligning people and AI tools. PromptMN's feasibility is evaluated across several frontier models, including Claude Fable 5, Claude Opus 4.8, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.5. The models correctly resolved PromptMN instructions, including complex structures such as repetition, conditionals, methods, and a prime-checking task, without fine-tuning. The same vocabulary applies across new codebases, maintenance, and redesign in the SDLC scenarios presented. While large-scale validation remains future work, these early results suggest PromptMN is a practical step toward clearer, more reviewable human-to-AI interaction.

AI.tldr: Introduces PromptMN, a lightweight, directive-based pseudo-prompting DSL that structures prompts for generative AI using typed, %-prefixed annotations, aiming to reduce ambiguity and improve reliability in agentic and SDLC workflows.
AI.motivation: Natural language prompts for generative AI are often ambiguous and fragile, especially in multi-step agent workflows and software development, where misinterpretations at the first handoff can propagate errors. There is a need for a representation that is more structured than informal prose but less heavyweight than full programming languages, so that diverse stakeholders can specify roles, goals, constraints, and outputs in a clear, reusable way.
AI.method: Design of PromptMN, a domain-specific pseudo-prompting language using compact %-prefixed directives (for roles, goals, requirements, priorities, constraints, plans, inputs, outputs). The paper defines its semantics via "semantic resolution" so directives can appear in any order. PromptMN is also used in reverse prompt engineering: models are asked to restate desired outcomes in PromptMN form for inspection. Feasibility is evaluated empirically by prompting several frontier LLMs (Claude Fable 5, Claude Opus 4.8, Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.5) with PromptMN-encoded tasks, including complex structures like repetition, conditionals, methods, and a prime-checking task, without any fine-tuning.
AI.result: Across multiple state-of-the-art models, PromptMN directives were correctly interpreted, including more complex control-like structures (repetition, conditionals, methods) and a prime-checking task, demonstrating that existing models can natively work with the proposed DSL. The same directive vocabulary proved usable across different SDLC phases (new codebases, maintenance, redesign), suggesting domain generality. No fine-tuning was required for these successes.
AI.conclusion: PromptMN provides a practical middle ground between freeform prompting and full programming, yielding prompts that are more explicit, inspectable, and reusable. Early feasibility tests across several LLMs show that the language is understandable by current models and usable across software development scenarios. Although broad, large-scale validation is still needed, the approach appears to be a promising step toward clearer and more reviewable human–AI communication, particularly in agentic and SDLC workflows.

title: RepSelect: Robust LLM Unlearning via Representation Selectivity
summary: Making large language models (LLMs) deeply forget specific knowledge and values without sacrificing general capabilities remains a central challenge in unlearning. However, current methods are easily reversed by fine-tuning or few-shot prompting, suggesting their forgetting is only shallow. We identify the root cause. Existing methods target representations shared with both the retain set and the subspace recovered by a fine-tuning attacker, making unlearning both disruptive to general capabilities and easy to reverse. We propose RepSelect (Representation Selectivity), isolates forget-set-specific representations by collapsing top principal components of weight gradients before each update, leaving general capabilities intact while limiting what fine-tuning can recover. We evaluate across two forget categories, biohazardous knowledge and abusive tendencies, and four model families spanning dense and Mixture-of-Experts architectures (Llama 3, Qwen 3.5, Gemma 4 E4B, DeepSeek V2 Lite). Compared to five popular baselines (GradDiff, NPO, SimNPO, RMU, UNDIAL), RepSelect achieves a 4-50x larger reduction in post-relearning answer accuracy than the strongest baseline, and is near-perfectly robust to few-shot prompting attacks. Targeting selective representations is thus an important step towards deep and robust LLM forgetting.

AI.tldr: They propose RepSelect, a method to make large language models forget specific unwanted knowledge/behaviors in a way that is hard to reverse by later fine-tuning or prompting, while preserving general capabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM unlearning methods produce only shallow forgetting: the removed behaviors can often be recovered by simple fine-tuning or clever prompting, and attempts to forget often hurt the model’s general performance. The authors aim to understand why this happens and to design an approach that enables deeper, more robust forgetting of specific knowledge and values without degrading overall capabilities.
AI.method: They analyze current unlearning approaches and argue that these methods act on representation subspaces shared between what should be forgotten and what should be retained, and also overlap with directions that a fine-tuning attacker can easily reinduce. To fix this, they introduce RepSelect, which seeks representations specific to the forget set by collapsing the top principal components of weight gradients before each training update. This targets directions in parameter space uniquely associated with the forget data, thereby preserving shared/general representations while limiting what later fine-tuning can bring back. They apply this across different forget categories and model architectures.
AI.result: Across biohazardous and abusive-content forgetting tasks and for four different LLM families (Llama 3, Qwen 3.5, Gemma 4 E4B, DeepSeek V2 Lite), RepSelect outperforms five existing unlearning baselines (GradDiff, NPO, SimNPO, RMU, UNDIAL). Specifically, after an adversary re-fine-tunes the model, RepSelect yields 4–50x larger reductions in the accuracy of recovered (forbidden) answers compared to the strongest baseline, and shows near-perfect robustness to recovery via few-shot prompting attacks.
AI.conclusion: Focusing unlearning on selectively identified, forget-set-specific representation directions enables deeper and more robust forgetting in LLMs. By collapsing principal components of gradients associated with the forget data, RepSelect preserves general capabilities while making it much harder for standard fine-tuning or prompting to restore the removed knowledge and behaviors. Selective representation targeting appears to be a key ingredient for effective, hard-to-reverse LLM unlearning.

title: From Parasocial Scripts to Dyadic Persistence in Autonomous AI-Agent Communities
comment: Submitted for review in ARR for EMNLP 2026
summary: While parasocial interactions (PSIs) and parasocial relationships (PSRs) have been studied in conventional media settings, we investigate whether PSI- (colloquial) relational cues also exist in online communities where both sides are autonomous AI agents. We analyze 4,434 posts and 50,338 comments from Moltbook through three theory-based textual indicators: attachment/intimacy language, reciprocity bids, and self-identification to original poster (OP). The combined results across methods based on keyword matching, few-shot large language model (LLM) annotation, and grouped-context LLM annotation reveal that PSI colloquial cues prevail and are strongly associated with OP re-engagement and a reciprocal reply structure. These results are robust across negative controls, nullification, clustered-standard-error re-estimation, and multiple-testing correction. A dyadic persistence test further affirms reciprocity bids aligned with sustained OP-involving mutual recurrence, providing empirical evidence for bridging interaction-level PSI scripts with PSR-consistent repeated dyadic patterns. We interpret the evidence as a behavioral structure in discourse by LLM-enabled agents.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether human-like parasocial interaction cues appear in conversations between AI agents in an online community, and finds that such cues are common and predict reciprocal, persistent exchanges.
AI.motivation: Prior work on parasocial interactions and relationships mainly addresses humans engaging with media figures or creators. With the rise of autonomous AI agents interacting with each other in online communities, it is unclear whether similar relational and conversational patterns emerge between non-human agents. The authors aim to examine if colloquial PSI cues—signs of attachment, reciprocity, and identification—can be detected in AI–AI discourse and whether they relate to behaviors consistent with parasocial relationships, such as mutual re-engagement over time.
AI.method: The authors collect 4,434 posts and 50,338 comments from Moltbook, an online community populated by autonomous AI agents. They operationalize three theory-driven textual indicators of PSI: (1) attachment/intimacy language, (2) reciprocity bids, and (3) self-identification with the original poster (OP). To detect these cues, they use a combination of keyword-based matching, few-shot LLM annotation at the comment level, and grouped-context LLM annotation that considers local conversational context. They then statistically test how these cues relate to OP re-engagement and reciprocal reply structures, applying robustness checks including negative controls, nullification tests, clustered standard errors, and multiple-testing corrections. Finally, they conduct a dyadic persistence test to see whether reciprocity bids are associated with sustained, repeated OP–commenter exchanges over time.
AI.result: Across all three detection methods, colloquial PSI cues are found to be prevalent in the AI–AI conversations. The presence of these cues is strongly and positively associated with the likelihood that the original poster re-engages and that the reply structure becomes more reciprocal. These associations remain significant after a range of robustness checks. The dyadic persistence test shows that reciprocity bids, in particular, are linked to ongoing, recurrent interactions between the same pairs of agents, mirroring patterns expected in parasocial relationships.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that autonomous AI agents in an online community exhibit behavioral and conversational structures resembling human parasocial interaction and relationship patterns. PSI-like colloquial cues not only appear frequently in AI–AI discourse but also predict reciprocal and persistent dyadic engagement. This suggests that LLM-enabled agents can generate and sustain relational scripts that echo human social dynamics, providing empirical grounding for bridging interaction-level PSI indicators with longer-term PSR-like patterns in non-human agent communities.

title: Beyond Parallel Sampling: Diverse Query Initialization for Agentic Search
comment: 15 pages, 8 figures; under review at EMNLP 2026
summary: Test-time scaling for agentic search typically increases depth (i.e., more turns and tokens per trajectory) or breadth (i.e., more parallel rollouts). Here we focus on breadth scaling, showing that standard parallel sampling yields diminishing returns, tracing this to query redundancy at the first turn. When models issue similar first queries across rollouts, the threads retrieve overlapping evidence, and subsequent turns are conditioned on this shared retrieval. We address this limitation with DivInit, a training-free intervention at the first turn. Rather than sampling k independent first queries, DivInit draws n candidates from a single call, picks k < n diverse seeds, and runs them as parallel trajectories. Across five open-weight models and eight benchmarks, DivInit consistently improves over standard parallel sampling, with average gains of five to seven points on multi-hop QA at matched compute. Code available at https://github.com/cxcscmu/diverse-query-initialization

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DivInit, a training-free method to increase the diversity of initial queries in parallel agentic search, improving performance over standard parallel sampling without extra compute.
AI.motivation: In test-time scaling for agentic search, increasing parallel rollouts (breadth) typically yields diminishing returns because models often issue redundant, similar first queries. This redundancy causes overlapping retrieval and correlated reasoning trajectories, limiting the benefit of running many parallel threads at the same compute budget.
AI.method: Instead of sampling k independent first queries for k rollouts, the method first generates n > k candidate queries from a single model call. It then selects k diverse initial queries (seeds) from these n candidates using a diversity-based selection procedure, and launches k parallel reasoning/retrieval trajectories from these diverse seeds. The method is training-free and applied only at the first turn of the agentic search process.
AI.result: Across five open-weight language models and eight benchmarks—especially on multi-hop question answering—DivInit consistently outperforms standard parallel sampling under matched compute budgets. The paper reports average gains of about 5–7 points in accuracy on multi-hop QA tasks.
AI.conclusion: Diminishing returns in breadth scaling largely stem from redundancy in initial queries, which causes overlapping evidence retrieval and correlated trajectories. By diversifying only the first-step queries via DivInit, one can obtain significantly better performance from the same number of parallel rollouts, without additional training or compute, making breadth scaling more effective for agentic search systems.

title: Self-Generated Error Training for Token Editing in Diffusion Language Models
summary: Token-to-token (T2T) editing lets LLaDA2.1 revise committed tokens during block-diffusion decoding. The released recipe trains this editor on random vocabulary corruptions, but at inference the editor sees the model's own fluent, high-confidence draft errors instead. We study this training-inference mismatch and propose self-generated T2T, which performs a no-gradient draft pass, fills masked positions with predicted tokens, and supervises recovery in a second pass under these self-generated corruptions. We implement the update as a short LoRA continued-pretraining pass on LLaDA2.1-mini and evaluate on several benchmarks under the official Q-Mode T2T procedure with unchanged inference parameters. The method generally improves accuracy while reducing T2T edit intensity, mitigating failure modes such as final-digit transcription errors after otherwise correct reasoning and excessive self-correction before short factual answers.

AI.tldr: The paper improves token-to-token (T2T) editing for LLaDA2.1 by training on self-generated draft errors rather than random corruptions, leading to better accuracy and fewer, more targeted edits.
AI.motivation: Existing T2T editors for block-diffusion decoding in LLaDA2.1 are trained on synthetic, random vocabulary corruptions. At inference, however, the editor must correct the model’s own fluent, high-confidence draft mistakes, which are structurally different from random noise. This training–inference mismatch can cause suboptimal corrections, unnecessary edits, and failures like small transcription errors after otherwise correct reasoning. The authors aim to close this gap so that the editor is specialized for the kinds of realistic errors it will see at test time.
AI.method: They introduce self-generated T2T training: (1) run a no-gradient draft decoding pass; (2) mask some positions and re-fill them with the model’s own predicted tokens, thereby creating realistic, self-generated corruptions; (3) run a second supervised pass that learns to recover the correct tokens from these corruptions. This is implemented as a short LoRA-based continued-pretraining phase on LLaDA2.1-mini. At evaluation time, they keep the official Q-Mode T2T decoding procedure and all inference parameters unchanged, isolating the effect of the new training recipe.
AI.result: On several benchmarks, self-generated T2T improves task accuracy compared to the baseline recipe. It also reduces T2T edit intensity, meaning the editor changes fewer tokens overall while still improving answers. Qualitatively, it alleviates specific failure modes such as incorrect final digits after correct reasoning chains and excessive self-correction on short factual responses.
AI.conclusion: Training the T2T editor on self-generated, model-like errors rather than random corruptions better aligns training with inference conditions. This alignment yields higher accuracy and more conservative, targeted edits without modifying the decoding procedure, suggesting that realistic corruption schemes are important for effective token-level editing in block-diffusion LMs.

title: When Rules Learn: A Self-Evolving Agent for Legal Case Retrieval
summary: Legal case retrieval remains challenging due to the complexity of legal language and the need for precise lexical alignment between queries and relevant cases. Although dense retrieval models have achieved notable progress, empirical studies show that BM25 continues to serve as a strong baseline in this domain. It motivates us to propose a self-evolving framework for rule-driven query rewriting that enhances BM25 without any parameter training. The framework equips an LLM-based agent with an automatic evaluation environment, enabling it to iteratively create rewriting rules, plan validation experiments over rule combinations, and eliminate ineffective rules based on historical feedbacks. We evaluate our method on the Chinese legal case retrieval benchmark LeCaRD-v2. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework outperforms non-evolutionary baselines, including human-designed rules and greedy rule selection, particularly when powered by a highcapacity core LLM. We also conduct detailed analyses to investigate the mechanisms underlying self-evolution. Our findings reveal that LLM's capabilities to leverage previous experimental results and its intrinsic knowledge of rule elimination play critical roles in refining the rule set via self-evolution.

AI.tldr: They propose a training-free, self-evolving LLM-agent framework that automatically learns query rewriting rules to boost BM25 for Chinese legal case retrieval, outperforming human and simple baselines.
AI.motivation: In legal case retrieval, matching queries with relevant cases is hard because of complex legal language and the need for precise lexical overlap. While dense retrieval has advanced, simple lexical methods like BM25 still perform strongly. This suggests value in improving BM25 via better queries instead of training new models, especially in domains or languages where training data or resources are limited. The authors are motivated to exploit LLMs to automatically design and refine rule-based query rewrites that improve BM25 without additional parameter training.
AI.method: They build a self-evolving framework centered on an LLM-based agent that operates over an automatic evaluation environment. The agent: (1) proposes interpretable, rule-based query rewriting strategies; (2) plans and runs validation experiments on different combinations of rules using BM25 retrieval performance as feedback; and (3) analyzes historical experimental results to prune or modify ineffective rules. This iterative loop lets the rule set evolve over time. The approach is applied to Chinese legal case retrieval on the LeCaRD-v2 benchmark, and compared against non-evolutionary baselines such as manually crafted rules and greedy rule selection. Different core LLMs with varying capacities are tested to understand their impact.
AI.result: On LeCaRD-v2, the self-evolving query rewriting framework consistently improves BM25 and surpasses non-evolutionary baselines, including human-designed rule sets and greedy rule selection strategies. The performance gains are especially pronounced when the core LLM has higher capacity. Analysis shows that the framework effectively converges to a refined set of helpful rules over iterations.
AI.conclusion: A training-free, LLM-driven, self-evolving rule framework can significantly enhance traditional BM25 for legal case retrieval via query rewriting. The key to its success is the LLM’s ability to interpret and exploit past experimental outcomes and to implicitly apply knowledge about which rules to discard. This demonstrates a practical way to combine strong lexical baselines with LLM agents for domain-specific retrieval tasks, without additional model training.

title: Revisiting LLM Adaptation for 3D CT Report Generation: A Study of Scaling and Diagnostic Priors
summary: Recent advances in multimodal learning, including large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs), have demonstrated strong adaptability to natural images. However, extending their use to the medical domain, particularly for volumetric (3D) images, is challenging due to high computational complexity, volumetric dependencies and the semantic gap between visual features and clinical terminology. Naively fine-tuning LLMs on limited medical data often leads to overfitting and clinical hallucination, where linguistic fluency is prioritized over clinical factuality. In this study, we investigate parameter-efficient adaptation strategies for volumetric CT report generation and introduce RAD3D-Prefix, a lightweight diagnostic-prior conditioning framework that minimizes the need for extensive parameter training. This module integrates image embeddings with multi-label diagnostic classification logits, preserving critical clinical details while bridging the semantic gap. By keeping the LLM frozen, our method requires minimal trainable parameters and mitigates the risk of overfitting on small, domain-specific datasets. Through a systematic study spanning LLMs from 96.1M to 1.6B parameters, we find that fine-tuning is most beneficial for smaller LLMs, whereas freezing larger (~1B+ LLMs and training only lightweight projection layers provides a superior trade-off between performance, generalization, and computational efficiency. Across multiple automatic metrics and a clinical reader study, RAD3D-Prefix outperforms comparable parameter-efficient baselines and demonstrates strong out-of-domain generalization while using substantially fewer trainable parameters than fully fine-tuned alternatives.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RAD3D-Prefix, a parameter-efficient method to adapt large language models for generating diagnostic reports from 3D CT scans, achieving better performance and generalization with far fewer trainable parameters than full fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs and VLMs work well on natural 2D images but struggle with medical 3D images due to computational cost, volumetric dependencies, and a semantic gap between visual features and clinical language. Directly fine-tuning LLMs on small medical datasets causes overfitting and clinically incorrect but fluent outputs. There is a need for adaptation methods that preserve clinical accuracy, work well on volumetric CT, and remain computationally efficient and parameter-efficient.
AI.method: They investigate parameter-efficient adaptation strategies for volumetric CT report generation across LLMs of different sizes (96.1M–1.6B parameters). They introduce RAD3D-Prefix, a lightweight conditioning framework that keeps the LLM frozen and only trains small projection/prefix modules. RAD3D-Prefix fuses volumetric CT image embeddings with multi-label diagnostic classification logits, forming a diagnostic-aware prefix that conditions the frozen LLM. This minimizes the number of trainable parameters, preserves clinically relevant information, and bridges the semantic gap between imaging features and report text.
AI.result: Empirically, they find that full fine-tuning mainly benefits small LLMs, while for larger models (~1B+ parameters), freezing the LLM and training only lightweight projections yields a better balance of performance, generalization, and compute/memory cost. RAD3D-Prefix surpasses comparable parameter-efficient baselines on standard automatic report-generation metrics and in a clinical reader study. It also generalizes well to out-of-domain data while using substantially fewer trainable parameters than fully fine-tuned models.
AI.conclusion: Parameter-efficient, diagnostic-prior-based conditioning is an effective way to adapt large LLMs to volumetric CT report generation. For larger LLMs, freezing the backbone and training small prefix/projection modules like RAD3D-Prefix offers superior performance–efficiency trade-offs, reduces overfitting and clinical hallucinations, and improves generalization compared to full fine-tuning and other parameter-efficient baselines.

title: SkillChain-Gym: A Benchmark for Reskilling-Aware Production-Inventory Control under Disruptions
summary: Production planning increasingly has to treat workforce capability as a decision variable: certifications lapse when skills are not maintained, new products require skills the current workforce does not hold, and reskilling competes for the same worker hours needed for production. Existing operations benchmarks usually treat labor as exogenous, while workforce-planning models with skills and learning are rarely released as reusable testbeds. We introduce SkillChain-Gym, a benchmark specification for reskilling-aware production-inventory control: a single-site environment with stylized worker skill-state dynamics, hard threshold certification, forgetting, and capacity-consuming training actions constrained by the same per-worker time budget as production. The benchmark includes seed-controlled disruption scenarios, three feasibility modes with projection diagnostics, deterministic replay, and metrics covering operations, resilience, capability growth, and training-access distribution. We evaluate production-only, reactive adaptive, water-filling adaptive, and static-insurance policies with budget variants over 60-shift horizons with paired statistical tests. The results are regime-dependent rather than a ranking. Training-capable policies dominate the production-only baseline, and maintenance training is necessary under forgetting even without disruptions. Among training-capable classes, adaptive training helps when bottlenecks are visible in the forecast, while a lean static cross-training plan, a deliberately favorable comparator whose structure encodes relevant skill contingencies, acts as strong insurance under surprise shocks and absenteeism. Capacity slack and the forgetting rate govern the boundary between these regimes. No policy class dominates across regimes, motivating forecast-driven controllers that decide when to buy skill insurance and when to react.

AI.tldr: Introduces SkillChain-Gym, a benchmark environment for studying reskilling-aware production-inventory control where worker skills, training, and forgetting interact with production planning.
AI.motivation: Traditional production planning benchmarks assume labor is fixed and fully capable, ignoring that skills lapse, new products need new certifications, and training uses the same time as production. Workforce-planning models with realistic skill and learning dynamics are usually bespoke and not open, leaving researchers without a standard testbed for comparing policies that co-optimize production and reskilling. The authors want to fill this gap with a reusable, disruption-rich benchmark that captures key trade-offs between output, training, resilience, and fairness of training access.
AI.method: They design SkillChain-Gym, a single-site stochastic environment with: (1) stylized worker skill-state dynamics, including certification thresholds and forgetting; (2) training actions that consume the same per-worker capacity as production; (3) disruption scenarios (e.g., shocks, absenteeism) generated by seeds; (4) three feasibility modes with automatic projection diagnostics; (5) deterministic replay for controlled experimentation; and (6) a rich set of metrics on operations, resilience, capability growth, and who gets training. On this benchmark they implement and compare several policy families over 60-shift horizons: a production-only baseline; reactive adaptive policies that train in response to emerging bottlenecks; water-filling adaptive policies that allocate excess capacity to training; and static-insurance policies that commit to a lean, cross-training plan. They vary budget and regime parameters (capacity slack, forgetting rate, disruption profiles) and use paired statistical tests for evaluation.
AI.result: Training-capable policies consistently outperform the production-only baseline. Because of forgetting, maintenance training is required even when no disruptions occur. However, no single policy type dominates: performance is strongly regime-dependent. Adaptive training policies work well when forecasts reveal where the future bottlenecks will be, while a compact static cross-training plan serves as robust insurance under surprise shocks and absenteeism. The interplay between available capacity slack and the forgetting rate largely determines which policy class is preferable in a given regime.
AI.conclusion: SkillChain-Gym provides a standardized, open benchmark for studying joint production-reskilling control under realistic skill dynamics and disruptions. Experiments show that incorporating training is essential but that different training strategies win in different operating regimes—adaptive policies when forecasts are informative and static skill insurance when shocks are unexpected and capacity is tight. Since no policy class dominates across all regimes, future work should focus on forecast-aware controllers that can dynamically choose between buying skill insurance and reacting adaptively to evolving conditions.

title: Speaking in Self-Assessing Tongues: On the Verbalized Confidence of LLMs in Machine Translation
summary: The rapid rise in popularity of large language models (LLMs) for translation calls for a thorough study of the reliability of their confidence in their own outputs. Unlike many generation tasks, translation errors and confidence levels can be useful at different levels of granularity (tokens, words, or spans). Unsupervised approaches based on internal signals like predicted probabilities can be misleading because they reflect certainty among alternatives rather than correctness. In addition, they require access to such internal signals. Here, we devise five verbalized methods of extracting an LLM's per-token confidence without those shortcomings and compare their reliability with that of the model's internal signals of certainty. We evaluate reliability using two forms of alignment: fine-grained error detection and calibration. For both, internal and verbalized methods perform similarly, although results vary by model. Interestingly, we find little to no correlation between internal and verbalized methods.

AI.tldr: Study compares verbalized confidence estimates from LLMs in translation with internal probability-based confidence, at token level, examining reliability for error detection and calibration.
AI.motivation: LLMs are widely used for translation, but we lack a clear understanding of how reliable their own confidence estimates are, especially at fine granularity (token/word/span). Existing unsupervised confidence methods depend on internal probabilities, which both require privileged access and capture only relative certainty among alternatives, not true correctness. There is a need for methods that (1) do not require internal scores, (2) operate at fine granularity, and (3) are empirically evaluated for reliability.
AI.method: The authors design five distinct, explicit ("verbalized") procedures that prompt an LLM to state its confidence for each token in its translation output. These do not rely on internal probabilities. They then compare these verbalized confidence scores against internal model signals (e.g., token probabilities) across LLMs. Reliability is assessed via two alignment notions: (a) fine-grained error detection—how well confidence distinguishes correct from incorrect tokens, and (b) calibration—how closely stated confidence matches empirical accuracy. They perform experiments across models and examine correlations between internal and verbalized measures.
AI.result: Verbalized and internal (probability-based) confidence methods achieve broadly similar reliability for both fine-grained error detection and calibration, but performance depends on the particular LLM. Despite similar aggregate reliability, the two types of signals—internal probabilities and verbalized confidences—appear largely uncorrelated with each other at the token level.
AI.conclusion: LLMs’ explicit, verbalized token-level confidence scores can be as reliable as internal probability-based signals for translation error detection and calibration, even though they do not require access to internal model states. However, the lack of correlation between verbalized and internal signals suggests they capture different aspects of uncertainty, raising questions about how best to combine or interpret them for downstream translation quality estimation and reliability assessment.

title: Skill-Constrained Model Predictive Control for Resilient Manufacturing Supply Chains
summary: In skill-constrained production-inventory systems, the qualified human capacity available tomorrow depends on training decisions made today: production requires certified workers, certifications decay unless maintained, and training consumes the same scarce worker hours that production needs now. We study a closed-loop skill-constrained model predictive controller that, at every shift, solves a finite-horizon mixed-integer program over production, inventory, backlog, and training, with binary predicted certification, hard production eligibility, and an interpretable terminal value that prices certified-capacity gaps at the horizon boundary; only the first-period action is applied before replanning. On synthetic, seed-controlled SkillChain-Gym scenarios - announced and surprise new-skill shocks, demand shocks, absenteeism, forecast- and availability-quality modes, capacity-boundary and training-rate sweeps, and negative controls - we evaluate the controller against production-only and maintenance-only ablations, static cross-training insurance plans, and a strong reactive heuristic, under an ex-ante locked configuration and paired statistics. The result is regime dependence, not superiority: no policy class dominates. Predictive control helps when skill or labor bottlenecks are forecastable early enough for training to complete; lean static insurance remains hard to beat under surprise shocks, near the demand-capacity boundary, and wherever pre-shock slack makes insurance cheap. Attribution ablations separate certification maintenance, re-acquisition of lapsed certifications, and greenfield skill acquisition. Forecastability, not adaptivity per se, decides when predictive control pays.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to jointly plan production and worker training when skills are perishable and both training and production consume the same limited labor hours, using a model predictive control approach and synthetic experiments.
AI.motivation: In many production-inventory systems, workers must be certified to perform certain tasks, certifications expire without maintenance, and training uses the same scarce time needed for production. Managers must decide how to allocate labor between immediate production and training that builds future capacity, especially under uncertainty in demand, skills needed, and worker availability. Existing policies like static cross-training or purely reactive rules may perform poorly when skill requirements change or when labor bottlenecks are forecastable. The paper aims to understand when sophisticated predictive optimization actually outperforms simpler approaches, and how the structure of uncertainty and forecastability affects this tradeoff.
AI.method: The authors formulate a closed-loop, skill-constrained model predictive controller (MPC). At each shift, the MPC solves a finite-horizon mixed-integer optimization problem that simultaneously decides production quantities, inventory and backlog levels, and training allocations. Worker eligibility for production is modeled with binary predicted certifications and hard constraints that only certified workers can produce. Certifications decay unless maintained, and training can acquire, reacquire, or maintain certifications while consuming worker hours. The MPC includes an interpretable terminal value function that prices gaps in certified capacity at the horizon boundary. Only the first-period decision is implemented before the optimization is re-solved in the next shift with updated information. Performance is tested in SkillChain-Gym, a synthetic, seed-controlled environment with a wide variety of regimes: announced vs. surprise new-skill shocks, demand shocks, worker absenteeism, different forecast and availability qualities, sweeps over capacity and training rates, and negative controls. The controller is compared against ablated variants (production-only, maintenance-only), static cross-training insurance plans, and a strong reactive heuristic, using an ex-ante locked configuration and paired statistical evaluations. Attribution-style ablations decompose the effects of certification maintenance, reacquisition, and greenfield skill acquisition.
AI.result: The experiments show strong regime dependence: no single policy class is uniformly best. The MPC-based predictive controller does not dominate the benchmarks across all scenarios. Predictive control tends to perform well when future skill or labor bottlenecks are sufficiently forecastable and there is enough lead time for training to complete before bottlenecks hit. In contrast, simple lean static cross-training (insurance) strategies are difficult to beat in regimes characterized by surprise shocks, operations near the demand-capacity boundary, and settings where pre-shock slack capacity makes it inexpensive to pre-insure with extra certifications. Attribution ablations clarify the distinct contributions of maintaining existing certifications, reacquiring lapsed ones, and acquiring entirely new skills.
AI.conclusion: Predictive, optimization-based control for joint production and training is not universally superior to simpler, static, or reactive policies. Its value is highly contingent on the forecastability of future bottlenecks and the available training lead time. In regimes with good forecasts and sufficient horizon, MPC can leverage anticipatory training to avoid skill or labor bottlenecks; however, in environments dominated by surprise shocks and tight capacity, lean static cross-training remains competitive or superior because pre-insurance is cheap and adaptivity cannot compensate for the lack of foresight. The study emphasizes that forecastability, rather than adaptivity alone, is the key driver of when predictive control is worth the added complexity, and it provides a structured framework to separate and evaluate different components of skill dynamics in such systems.

title: MLLP-VRAIN UPV system for the IWSLT 2026 Simultaneous Speech Translation task
summary: This work describes the participation of the MLLP-VRAIN research group in the shared task of the IWSLT 2026 Simultaneous Speech Translation track. Our submission utilizes the recently released Parakeet and Qwen 3.5 models to create a robust, cascaded solution for long-form SimulST through the use of adaptive "black-box" policies. We explore relaxations of these policies to achieve better quality-latency trade-offs. Compared to last year, we participate on all language directions. In addition to this, for the En$\rightarrow${De, It, Zh} directions we also participate in this year's new context track employing a combination of ASR word-boosting and a RAG mechanism of offline pre-translated exemplars to guide generation and enrich our system with domain-specific context. Finally, we provide a detailed latency analysis of our system. Compared to last year, results on the MCIF En$\rightarrow$De test set shows a substantial quality improvement of +5.82 XCOMET-XL. Our context track processing further improves performance by +1.03.

AI.tldr: The paper presents the MLLP-VRAIN system for the IWSLT 2026 Simultaneous Speech Translation track, using cascaded Parakeet ASR and Qwen 3.5 MT with adaptive black-box policies, enhanced by context-aware mechanisms and achieving significant quality and latency improvements over last year.
AI.motivation: To improve long-form simultaneous speech translation performance, especially the trade-off between translation quality and latency, and to exploit domain/context information in the newly introduced context track across all language directions compared to the group’s more limited participation the previous year.
AI.method: They build a cascaded SimulST system combining Parakeet for ASR and Qwen 3.5 for machine translation, controlled by adaptive black-box read/write policies whose constraints are relaxed to better balance quality and latency. For the new context track (En→De/It/Zh), they augment ASR with word-boosting and use a retrieval-augmented generation mechanism over pre-translated exemplars to inject domain-specific context into the MT model. They then perform detailed latency analysis across conditions.
AI.result: On the MCIF En→De test set, the new system gains +5.82 XCOMET-XL points over last year’s system, with the additional context-track mechanisms contributing a further +1.03 XCOMET-XL, while maintaining competitive latency according to their detailed analysis.
AI.conclusion: A cascaded Parakeet–Qwen 3.5 architecture with adaptive black-box policies is effective for long-form simultaneous speech translation, and incorporating context via ASR word-boosting and RAG over pre-translated exemplars yields further quality gains with manageable latency, demonstrating strong improvements over the previous year’s system in the IWSLT SimulST setting.

title: Nothing from Something: Can a Language Model Discover 0?
summary: AI systems based on artificial neural networks are being developed with aspirations of pushing the boundary of human mathematical knowledge. A key question for these systems is how much they can reach beyond their training data. Mathematical discovery requires a strong form of out of distribution generalization; the ability to hypothesize genuinely new - and potentially logically more powerful - mathematical structures. It has been hypothesized that language abilities support such generalizations in human cognition. In this work, we use simple arithmetic as a case study for examining how modern AI models could expand their mathematical horizons, evaluating whether these models can independently discover the concept of "zero". We show that We show that (1) language models of a GPT-2 size are unable to perform this generalization at test time regardless of language pretraining, but (2) models can improve substantially after training on tens or hundreds of examples of zero. Additionally, we find that language pretraining reduces the number of required examples by approximately $50\%$, showing that language abilities can scaffold mathematical discovery in neural models.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether modern language models can independently discover the mathematical concept of zero using simple arithmetic as a testbed.
AI.motivation: To understand how far neural-network-based AI systems can go beyond their training data in mathematics, particularly in making out-of-distribution generalizations needed for genuine mathematical discovery. The authors focus on whether language abilities, as in humans, can help neural models hypothesize new and more powerful mathematical structures such as the notion of zero.
AI.method: Use simple arithmetic tasks as a controlled environment and evaluate GPT-2–sized language models on their ability to infer and use the concept of zero without explicit training examples. Compare models with and without language pretraining, then progressively provide them with tens or hundreds of supervised examples involving zero to measure how performance and sample efficiency change.
AI.result: (1) GPT-2–scale language models cannot, at test time, independently generalize to the concept of zero in arithmetic if they have not seen explicit training examples involving zero, regardless of whether they were language-pretrained. (2) Their performance improves markedly when given tens to hundreds of training examples featuring zero. (3) Language-pretrained models need about 50% fewer such examples to reach comparable performance, indicating a clear sample-efficiency benefit from language pretraining.
AI.conclusion: Modern GPT-2–sized language models do not spontaneously discover the concept of zero in arithmetic as a form of strong out-of-distribution generalization. However, a relatively modest number of examples allows them to acquire and use the concept effectively, and language pretraining significantly reduces the needed supervision. This supports the idea that language capabilities can scaffold and enhance mathematical discovery in neural networks, though they do not yet replicate the strongest forms of human-like conceptual extrapolation.

title: Are you speaking my languages? On spoken language adherence in multimodal LLMs
comment: 7 pages, 3 tables in the main body
summary: While Large Language Model (LLM) based Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) enables seamless multilingual use, models often misidentify the output language, compromising transcription fidelity and downstream application quality. To preserve flexibility and code-switching capabilities, we propose a soft prompting approach that hints at potential spoken languages without strictly constraining the output. We formally define this challenge as a lack of language adherence, introduce a novel metric to quantify violations, and evaluate three mitigation strategies: (1) zero-shot prompting for robust guidance under uncertainty, (2) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to improve prompt adherence, and (3) Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to enforce adherence during decoding. We present a comparative analysis of these methods across multiple languages, evaluating effectiveness in reducing the language violation while maintaining overall ASR performance. Finally, we discuss trade-offs to guide strategy selection under various compute constraints.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses language misidentification in LLM-based ASR and proposes soft prompting plus other strategies to improve language adherence while preserving multilingual and code-switching capabilities.
AI.motivation: LLM-based ASR systems support multilingual transcription and code-switching, but they frequently produce text in the wrong language, harming transcription quality and downstream applications. Existing approaches that hard-constrain language can reduce flexibility, so there is a need for methods that improve language adherence without sacrificing multilingual behavior.
AI.method: The authors formally define the notion of language adherence and introduce a metric to quantify how often and how severely an ASR model violates the desired output language. They propose and compare three mitigation strategies: (1) zero-shot prompting that provides robust, language-related instructions under uncertainty; (2) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to strengthen the model’s responsiveness to language prompts; and (3) Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning integrated into decoding to guide adherence. A new soft prompting scheme is introduced, which lists possible spoken languages as hints rather than hard constraints, preserving code-switching ability.
AI.result: Experiments are conducted over multiple languages to measure reductions in language violations and the impact on standard ASR metrics. The evaluation shows how each strategy affects language adherence and transcription quality, highlighting which methods best reduce violations while minimizing degradation of ASR performance.
AI.conclusion: Soft language prompting combined with strategies like zero-shot prompting, SFT, and CoT can significantly reduce language adherence violations in LLM-based ASR without eliminating multilingual and code-switching capabilities. The paper provides a trade-off analysis, helping practitioners choose an appropriate mitigation strategy under different computational budgets and performance requirements.

title: Quantifying Consistency in LLM Logical Reasoning via Structural Uncertainty
comment: Published at ICLR 2026 Workshop on Logical Reasoning of Large Language Models. Accepted as best paper
summary: Large language models can arrive at the same answer through reasoning paths that are unstable, contradictory, or difficult to rank consistently -- a failure mode especially prevalent in multi-step deductive reasoning. Existing methods assess reliability primarily through output dispersion -- measuring how much sampled answers differ -- but this discards a complementary signal: whether the model can consistently rank competing reasoning candidates. We propose structural uncertainty, a consistency-aware framework derived from the stability of self-preference-induced rankings over sampled reasoning solutions. Given a query, we generate multiple candidate solutions and ask the model to judge pairwise preferences among its own outputs. We aggregate self-preferences into ranking distributions via Bradley-Terry modeling with PageRank, and decompose the signal into two entropy-based components: across-trial ranking instability and within-trial candidate ambiguity. Across five LLMs and eight benchmarks, structural signals provide information complementary to answer dispersion: on logical and mathematical reasoning tasks, the combination improves identification of unreliable instances, while on factual retrieval the structural signal collapses toward uniformity, diagnosing a regime boundary where reasoning-level consistency evaluation is uninformative. The two components relate differently to accuracy: within-trial ambiguity correlates positively with correctness -- consistent with settings where multiple plausible solution paths remain competitive -- while across-trial instability correlates negatively, signaling unreliable reasoning. Structural uncertainty is best understood not as a universal confidence estimator, but as a regime-sensitive evaluator of logical reasoning consistency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces structural uncertainty, a consistency-aware way to evaluate how reliably large language models reason by examining how stably they rank their own reasoning paths, rather than only how often their final answers vary.
AI.motivation: Existing reliability measures for LLM reasoning mainly look at output dispersion—how much sampled answers differ—but ignore whether the model is consistent in how it prefers one chain of reasoning over another. This is a problem because models can produce the same final answer via unstable, contradictory, or hard-to-rank reasoning paths, especially in multi-step deductive tasks. The authors want a measure that captures this structural consistency of reasoning, not just variance in answers, to better detect unreliable reasoning and understand when such evaluation is informative.
AI.method: For each query, the model generates multiple candidate reasoning solutions. The model is then asked to express pairwise preferences between its own candidate solutions. These pairwise self-preferences are aggregated into a probabilistic ranking over candidates using a Bradley–Terry model combined with a PageRank-style procedure. From these ranking distributions, the authors define structural uncertainty and decompose it into two entropy-based components: (1) across-trial ranking instability, capturing how much the induced rankings change across repeated trials; and (2) within-trial candidate ambiguity, capturing how spread-out or decisive the ranking is within a single trial. They evaluate these signals across five LLMs and eight benchmarks, and compare them to traditional dispersion-based uncertainty measures.
AI.result: Across logical and mathematical reasoning benchmarks, the structural uncertainty signals provide information that is complementary to standard answer-dispersion metrics. Combining structural signals with dispersion improves the identification of instances where the model’s answers are unreliable. In contrast, on factual retrieval tasks the structural signal degenerates toward a uniform pattern, indicating that reasoning-level consistency offers little discriminative power in that regime. The two entropy components behave differently with respect to accuracy: within-trial ambiguity shows a positive correlation with correctness, suggesting that tasks with multiple plausible reasoning paths can remain reliable; across-trial instability shows a negative correlation, marking cases where the model’s reasoning is unreliable.
AI.conclusion: Structural uncertainty is not a one-size-fits-all confidence score but a regime-sensitive tool for evaluating the consistency of LLM reasoning. By focusing on the stability of self-induced rankings over reasoning paths, it exposes aspects of reliability that answer dispersion alone misses. Within-trial ambiguity can signal healthy competition among good solution paths, while across-trial instability flags inconsistent reasoning linked to errors. The framework helps delineate when reasoning-consistency analysis is informative (e.g., multi-step logical/mathematical tasks) and when it collapses and becomes uninformative (e.g., straightforward factual retrieval).

title: Examining the Limits of Word2Vec with Toki Pona
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Accepted to the Society for Computation in Linguistics (SCiL) 2026
summary: Word2Vec's effectiveness at generating semantic embeddings has been widely validated, yet it has been tested almost exclusively on languages with large vocabulary inventories. This study examines whether Word2Vec can successfully capture semantic relationships within an extremely reduced vocabulary using data from Toki Pona, a constructed language with approximately 130 words. We sourced 1.4 million sentences (7.95 million tokens) from the Toki Pona community for training. Approximately 23% of sentences in the corpus contain non-Toki Pona tokens such as named entities, loanwords, and neologisms. To investigate whether this linguistic noise enhances or hinders performance -- a topic rarely addressed in word embedding literature -- we trained two distinct models: one retaining these incidental tokens and another filtering them out completely. Evaluation was conducted using quantitative methods measuring word proximity to semantic category centroids, automated silhouette scores via agglomerative clustering, and qualitative analysis utilizing representational similarity matrices compared against English. The results indicate that while sparse, non-core tokens do not affect the relative structure of the learned embeddings, they actually draw similar words closer together in the vector space. Importantly, Word2Vec's effectiveness depends more on distributional patterns than lexicon size even at this extreme lower bound.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether Word2Vec still learns meaningful semantic structure in an extremely small-vocabulary language (Toki Pona) and whether noisy, non-core tokens help or hurt embeddings.
AI.motivation: Most Word2Vec research focuses on languages with large vocabularies, leaving it unclear whether distributional semantic methods work when the lexicon is tiny and heavily reused. Additionally, the impact of linguistic “noise” such as named entities, loanwords, and neologisms on embeddings is underexplored. The authors want to know if Word2Vec can capture semantic relations at a lower bound of lexicon size and whether sparse, non-core tokens degrade or actually improve the quality of the learned space.
AI.method: They compiled a large corpus of community-produced Toki Pona text (1.4M sentences, 7.95M tokens). They trained two Word2Vec models: one with all tokens included (core Toki Pona words plus noisy non-core tokens) and one where all non-Toki Pona tokens were removed. They then evaluated the embeddings quantitatively by measuring how close words are to semantic category centroids, by computing silhouette scores from agglomerative clustering, and qualitatively via representational similarity matrices, comparing Toki Pona embeddings to English-based expectations.
AI.result: Both models learn a coherent semantic space despite the very small vocabulary. The presence of sparse, non-core tokens does not distort the relative structure of the embedding space. Instead, these tokens make similar core words cluster more tightly, effectively pulling semantically related items closer together in vector space. Word2Vec’s performance remains robust even under extreme lexical sparsity when sufficient distributional data is available.
AI.conclusion: Word2Vec can capture meaningful semantic relationships even in an extremely low-vocabulary language, indicating that its success relies more on the richness of distributional contexts than on lexicon size. Furthermore, incidental non-core tokens like named entities and loanwords, when sparse, do not harm and may even enhance the embedding structure by sharpening semantic clusters. This suggests that aggressive filtering of such “noise” in training corpora is not always necessary and that distributional patterns are the primary driver of embedding quality.

title: MemTrace: Probing What Final Accuracy Misses in Long-Term Memory
summary: LLM agents increasingly maintain long-term memory of user facts across sessions. Yet such memory is usually evaluated by aggregating accuracy over question rows or episodes. Because this approach scores question rows independently, even when several questions probe the same fact, it cannot show how that fact behaves as conditions change. We introduce MemTrace, a benchmark whose unit of measurement is the knowledge point: a single typed fact about the user, rather than an individual question. MemTrace probes each fact along three controlled dimensions: memory age, defined by how many sessions ago the fact appeared in the history; question type, covering current state, earlier state, and trajectory of change; and evidence condition, covering present, missing, and contradicted-by-false-premise settings. Evaluating 13 memory-system configurations across four paradigms, we find that similar pooled accuracy hides different failures: recovering a fact's current and earlier states does not imply tracking how it changed, and safe abstention does not imply correcting a false premise. The dominant bottleneck is evidence use, not retrieval: when systems fail, the evidence was retrievable 10 times more often than it was missing. These results suggest that improving long-term memory requires better use of reachable evidence, not simply more storage or retrieval.

AI.tldr: MemTrace is a benchmark that evaluates long‑term user memory in LLM agents at the level of individual user facts (“knowledge points”), showing that current systems mainly fail to use retrieved evidence rather than to retrieve it.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of long‑term memory in LLM agents usually aggregate accuracy over questions or episodes, treating each question independently. This masks how a single underlying fact behaves under different conditions (e.g., over time or under conflicting evidence). The authors want an evaluation that can reveal whether systems truly track the evolution and robustness of specific user facts, not just answer isolated questions correctly.
AI.method: The authors introduce MemTrace, a benchmark where the atomic unit is a “knowledge point”: one typed fact about a user. For each fact they design multiple probing questions that vary along three controlled dimensions: (1) memory age, measured by how many sessions ago the fact appeared; (2) question type, asking about current state, earlier state, or the trajectory of change of the fact; and (3) evidence condition, where relevant evidence is present, absent, or contradicted via a false premise. They then evaluate 13 different memory-system configurations spanning four paradigms on this benchmark and compare their behavior across these dimensions.
AI.result: Across the 13 memory configurations, pooled accuracy scores appear similar but conceal qualitatively different failure modes. Systems that can recall both current and previous states of a fact often still fail on questions about how that fact has changed over time. Likewise, systems that abstain safely when evidence is missing often still fail to challenge or correct questions that contain false premises. An analysis of errors shows that when answers are wrong, relevant evidence is typically still retrievable; in fact, evidence is found around ten times more often than it is actually missing, indicating that retrieval is rarely the main obstacle.
AI.conclusion: Evaluations based on independent questions understate important weaknesses in LLM long‑term memory. By focusing on knowledge points and systematically varying memory age, question type, and evidence conditions, MemTrace reveals that the core bottleneck is not storage or retrieval capacity but how systems use available evidence—especially for reasoning about changes over time and handling false premises. Improving long‑term memory behavior will therefore require better evidence utilization and reasoning mechanisms rather than just scaling memory size or retrieval methods.

title: Do Large Language Models Always Tell The Same Stories?
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled the generation of high-quality prose, yet the question of whether these models are capable of generating diverse outputs remains contested. In this work, we investigate the diversity of LLM-generated stories through the framework of narrative similarity. Using a contrastive framework and a dataset of human-written stories and prompts from r/WritingPrompts, we collect narrative similarity judgments across 10 representative LLMs, utilizing both human evaluations and three different automatic annotation methods. Our findings reveal a consistent trend: LLM-generated narratives are consistently more similar to each other than human-written stories are. We demonstrate that frontier models in particular converge on a ``mean'' generic narrative that approximates individual human stories but lacks the collective diversity of human authors. Finally, we show that common mitigation strategies, including negative prompting and temperature scaling, fail to meaningfully address this homogeneity.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how diverse or similar stories generated by large language models are, showing that models tend to produce more homogeneous narratives than humans and that simple prompting tweaks do not solve this.
AI.motivation: While LLMs can generate fluent, high-quality text, it is unclear whether they can match the breadth and variety of human creativity. Understanding narrative diversity is important for applications in creative writing, personalization, and avoiding model collapse toward generic outputs. The authors aim to rigorously measure and compare the diversity of LLM-generated stories against human-written ones.
AI.method: The authors use a contrastive narrative similarity framework on a dataset of human-written stories and prompts from r/WritingPrompts. They generate stories from 10 representative LLMs for the same prompts, then collect narrative similarity judgments via human annotators and three automatic annotation methods. They compare within-model similarity, across-model similarity, and human–human similarity to assess how clustered or diverse the narratives are.
AI.result: Across all evaluation methods, LLM-generated narratives are more similar to each other than human-written stories for the same prompts. Frontier, state-of-the-art models especially tend to converge on a generic ‘mean’ narrative pattern that resembles a plausible human story but shows less collective variety across outputs. Attempts to increase diversity using negative prompts (explicitly asking for non-generic stories) and temperature scaling do not substantially reduce this homogeneity.
AI.conclusion: LLMs—particularly frontier models—exhibit narrative convergence, generating stories that are stylistically and structurally similar to each other, lacking the diversity found in human authorship. Conventional strategies like negative prompting and higher sampling temperature are insufficient to restore human-level diversity, suggesting that improving narrative variety will require deeper algorithmic or training changes rather than simple decoding or prompting tweaks.

title: SpeechDx: A Multi-Task Benchmark for Clinical Speech AI
summary: Speech offers a uniquely informative window into health by simultaneously engaging neurological, motor, respiratory, and vocal systems. Current clinical speech AI methods have largely progressed through isolated condition-specific studies, making results difficult to compare and generalization difficult to assess. We introduce SpeechDx, a large-scale benchmark for clinical speech AI spanning 12 datasets and 27 tasks across diverse health conditions. To enable evaluation across shared clinical mechanisms, SpeechDx structures tasks by the stage of speech production they disrupt: conceptualization, formulation, and articulation. The benchmark tests generalization by including tasks with limited labeled data and evaluating the same health condition across multiple datasets, distinguishing clinically meaningful patterns from dataset artefacts. We systematically evaluate 12 state-of-the-art audio encoders across all tasks and under zero-shot cross-condition transfer. Results show that large-scale speech models represent the strongest overall baselines, domain-specific models improve performance only on closely matched tasks, and no current representation generalizes reliably across the clinical speech landscape. SpeechDx establishes a shared evaluation framework for tracking progress toward general-purpose clinical speech representations

AI.tldr: SpeechDx is a large-scale benchmark to systematically evaluate and compare clinical speech AI models across many health conditions and tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing clinical speech AI research is fragmented into isolated, condition-specific studies, making it hard to compare methods, measure true generalization, and distinguish meaningful clinical signals from dataset-specific artifacts.
AI.method: The authors build SpeechDx, a benchmark integrating 12 datasets and 27 tasks, categorized by which stage of speech production is affected (conceptualization, formulation, articulation). They design tasks to test generalization with limited labels and repeated conditions across datasets, and then systematically evaluate 12 state-of-the-art audio encoders, including large general speech models and domain-specific ones, including zero-shot cross-condition transfer settings.
AI.result: Large-scale general speech models achieve the best overall performance; domain-specific models only help when the evaluation task closely matches their training domain; no existing representation provides robust, reliable generalization across all clinical speech tasks and conditions examined.
AI.conclusion: SpeechDx provides a unified evaluation framework and shows that current models are still far from general-purpose clinical speech representations, setting a benchmark for future work to develop more broadly generalizable clinical speech AI models.

title: Translating the Untranslatable: An Operationalizable Ontology for Untranslatability
summary: Untranslatability, cases where meaning cannot be directly preserved across languages, is well-studied in linguistics but underexplored in NLP. As machine translation (MT) systems improve on standard benchmarks, their limitations increasingly concentrate in such cases, where translation cannot be reduced to one-to-one equivalence. We introduce a structured ontology of untranslatability along with a taxonomy of compensation strategies, which are specific techniques to convey meaning under these untranslatable circumstances. We operationalize this framework into a multilingual dataset of untranslatable sentences paired with strategy-based translations, enabling controlled analysis of translation behavior. Initial human preference studies suggest that translation quality depends on the strategy used, with consistent preferences for outputs that include explanatory context, known as the Annotation compensation strategy. Our framework and dataset provide a foundation for studying and modeling strategy-informed machine translation.

AI.tldr: The paper builds an ontology and dataset for studying untranslatability and compensation strategies in machine translation, showing that certain strategies, especially adding explanatory context, yield better human-rated translations.
AI.motivation: While untranslatability is well-theorized in linguistics, NLP and MT evaluations focus mostly on segments where near one-to-one meaning preservation is possible. As MT systems saturate standard benchmarks, their remaining weaknesses cluster around linguistically tricky, culturally embedded, or structurally mismatched content where direct equivalence fails. There is a lack of structured resources and frameworks that characterize these untranslatable phenomena and the practical strategies translators use to compensate for them, making it difficult to systematically study and improve MT behavior in these hard cases.
AI.method: The authors (1) design a structured ontology of untranslatability types and a taxonomy of compensation strategies (e.g., addition of explanatory context, rephrasing, etc.), (2) instantiate this framework as a multilingual dataset containing untranslatable source sentences paired with multiple translations annotated by the strategy employed, and (3) conduct human preference studies to evaluate how different strategies affect perceived translation quality, with special attention to strategies that provide extra context (Annotation).
AI.result: They successfully create a multilingual dataset of carefully selected untranslatable sentences with aligned translations labeled by compensation strategy. Human evaluation shows that translation quality is not uniform across strategies: annotative strategies that add explicit explanatory context are consistently preferred over alternatives, indicating that how information is compensated matters greatly for user satisfaction.
AI.conclusion: Untranslatability should be treated as a structured, analyzable phenomenon in MT rather than as noise or rare edge cases. The proposed ontology, strategy taxonomy, and dataset offer a practical foundation for controlled experimentation on strategy-informed MT. Moreover, the findings that annotation-style compensations are reliably preferred suggest promising directions for designing MT systems that can dynamically select and apply suitable translation strategies when faced with untranslatable content.

title: Distributed General-Purpose Agent Networks: Architecture, Key Mechanisms, and Prototypes
summary: Large language models have accelerated the transition from passive conversational assistants to autonomous agents that can understand goals, plan actions, invoke tools, and execute multi-step tasks. Yet the capability of a single agent remains constrained by its local data, tool permissions, runtime environment, and governance boundary. This paper studies distributed general-purpose agent networks: open peer-to-peer networks in which heterogeneous agents deployed on personal devices, edge nodes, or autonomous computing environments can discover one another, establish trust, negotiate cooperation rules, and execute open-ended tasks. We argue that such networks cannot be obtained by simply combining existing peer-to-peer overlays with conventional multi-agent systems. Unlike traditional P2P networks, agent networks must propagate semantic declarations about intentions, capabilities, states, and cooperation constraints. We therefore propose a layered architecture centered on a protocol adaptation layer that connects upper-level task semantics with lower-level network operations. Based on this architecture, the paper identifies three core mechanism problems: semantic announcement propagation for collaborator discovery, verifiable identity and multi-topic reputation for cooperation governance, and semantic-gradient mechanism design for open task execution. For each problem, we present a technical route, including bodyless gossip with sequential logs, BAID-based identity binding with MG-EigenTrust reputation, and a Stackelberg-style mechanism-generation loop driven by semantic attribution feedback. We further report prototype overhead results for BAID-style tiered verification and mechanism-level simulations of MG-EigenTrust under cross-topic disguise-collusion attacks. The resulting framework provides a system-level foundation for open, trustworthy, and scalable agent collaboration.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a system-level architecture and mechanisms for open, peer-to-peer networks of heterogeneous AI agents to collaborate on complex tasks in a trustworthy and scalable way.
AI.motivation: Single autonomous agents are limited by their local resources, permissions, and environments.
