Existing peer-to-peer overlays and traditional multi-agent systems do not adequately support open, heterogeneous networks of agents that must share rich semantic information about their intentions, capabilities, and constraints while remaining secure and trustworthy. There is a need for a generalized framework that enables such agents to discover each other, establish trust, govern cooperation, and execute open-ended tasks across organizational and technical boundaries.
AI.method: The authors introduce a layered architecture for distributed general-purpose agent networks, emphasizing a protocol adaptation layer that maps high-level task semantics to low-level network operations. Within this architecture, they formalize three core mechanism problems: (1) semantic announcement propagation for discovering collaborators, (2) verifiable identity and multi-topic reputation for governing cooperation, and (3) semantic-gradient-based mechanism design for open task execution. They propose concrete technical approaches: bodyless gossip with sequential logs for propagation, BAID-based identity binding with MG-EigenTrust for identity and reputation, and a Stackelberg-style mechanism-generation loop guided by semantic attribution feedback for task execution. They also build prototypes and perform simulations to evaluate overhead and robustness.
AI.result: The paper delivers: (a) a conceptual layered architecture for semantic agent networks; (b) specific protocol and mechanism designs—bodyless gossip with sequential logs, BAID-style identity binding, MG-EigenTrust reputation, and a semantic feedback-driven mechanism-generation loop; and (c) empirical evidence from prototype experiments showing the overhead characteristics of BAID’s tiered verification and from simulations indicating how MG-EigenTrust behaves under cross-topic disguise-collusion attack scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture and mechanisms together form a foundational framework for constructing open, trustworthy, and scalable networks of autonomous agents. By integrating semantic announcement propagation, verifiable identities with multi-topic reputations, and adaptive mechanism design, the system aims to enable heterogeneous agents across diverse environments to reliably discover each other, coordinate, and execute complex open-ended tasks in a decentralized, peer-to-peer fashion.

title: Implicit vs. Explicit Prompting Strategies for LVLMs in Referential Communication
summary: Two recent studies (Jones et al. (2026); Zeng et al. (2026)) reach apparently contradictory conclusions about whether LVLMs can coordinate on efficient referring expressions. We control for task differences between the studies while directly comparing their prompting styles. We replicate the finding that models can coordinate efficient referring expressions when explicitly prompted to do so, suggesting that other task differences are not responsible for divergent results. However, we also find that the same models fail to infer the need for communicative efficiency from a more implicit prompt, highlighting critical differences between how humans and AI systems communicate.

AI.tldr: Studies compare LVLMs’ ability to use efficient referring expressions under different prompts, finding success only with explicit instructions.
AI.motivation: Clarify why two prior studies report conflicting results about LVLMs’ communicative efficiency and understand whether prompting style or task differences drive the discrepancy.
AI.method: Control for task differences between the two prior works while systematically varying and directly comparing their prompting styles, using the same LVLMs on referring expression coordination tasks under explicit vs implicit prompts.
AI.result: LVLMs successfully coordinate on efficient referring expressions when given explicit instructions but fail to infer and apply communicative efficiency under more implicit prompts; task differences across the prior studies do not explain their divergent findings.
AI.conclusion: Prompting style is crucial: LVLMs do not reliably infer communicative efficiency needs from implicit context, revealing an important gap between human and AI communication despite apparent success under explicit guidance.

title: Treatment Response Optimized Clinical Decision Support AI System via Digital Twin Simulation
comment: Accepted for presentation at the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Conference (EMBC) 2026
summary: Clinical decision support AI systems (CDSASs) must adapt to evolving patient conditions in real-time while adhering to strict safety constraints. We present an online adaptive framework that integrates Treatment Effect (TE) estimation to quantify clinical benefits, a patient Digital Twin (DT) to simulate treatment trajectories, and Reinforcement Learning (RL) for sequential decision-making. The AI system is initially trained on historical medical records and operates in a continuous learning loop. To ensure safety, a rule-based module monitors vital signs and blocks contraindicated treatments. Cases with strong internal model disagreement are flagged for clinician review, simulated in our experiments via a pre-trained outcome model. We validate our framework using both a synthetic clinical simulator and a real-world ovarian cancer dataset from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA). In both simulated and clinical settings, our method demonstrated superior effectiveness and stability in recommending treatments compared to standard computational baselines. Furthermore, the AI system maintains low latency and requires expert consultation for only a minority of cases in our experimental validation, demonstrating its potential as a safe, clinician-supervised tool for personalized medicine that continuously improves through practical use.

AI.tldr: An online adaptive clinical decision support AI that combines treatment effect estimation, digital twins, and reinforcement learning to recommend safe, personalized treatments in real time while involving clinicians when uncertainty is high.
AI.motivation: Clinical decision support systems need to make sequential, real-time treatment decisions that adapt to changing patient states, but must remain safe, interpretable, and efficient. Existing models often rely on static data, lack mechanisms to quantify treatment benefit, and may be unsafe or unstable when deployed online. There is a need for a framework that can learn continuously from real-world data, personalize treatment, respect clinical safety constraints, and know when to defer to human experts.
AI.method: The authors design an online adaptive framework comprising three main components: (1) Treatment Effect (TE) estimation modules to quantify the expected benefit of different treatments for a specific patient; (2) a patient Digital Twin (DT) to simulate plausible future clinical trajectories under candidate treatments; and (3) a Reinforcement Learning (RL) agent that uses TE estimates and DT simulations to select treatments in a sequential decision-making setup. The system is pre-trained on historical medical records, then updated in a continuous learning loop during deployment. A rule-based safety module monitors vital signs and blocks treatments that violate pre-specified clinical safety rules. Additionally, a disagreement-detection mechanism flags cases with strong internal model disagreement (e.g., between outcome models or policy variants) for clinician review, mimicked in experiments using a pre-trained outcome model. The framework is evaluated on a synthetic clinical simulator and a real-world ovarian cancer dataset from TCGA, and compared against standard computational baselines.
AI.result: Across both a synthetic simulator and the TCGA ovarian cancer dataset, the proposed framework shows higher effectiveness and stability in treatment recommendations than standard baselines, indicating better clinical outcomes or surrogate metrics and reduced variability. The system runs with low latency suitable for real-time use and triggers expert review only for a minority of high-uncertainty cases. Safety constraints enforced by the rule-based module prevent contraindicated actions without excessively limiting performance.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that integrating treatment effect estimation, digital twins, and reinforcement learning into an online adaptive framework can produce a clinically viable CDS AI that is both effective and safety-aware. The framework offers personalized, sequential treatment recommendations, maintains computational efficiency, and includes explicit mechanisms for safety monitoring and human oversight. This positions the system as a promising clinician-supervised tool that can continuously improve through real-world deployment while managing risk.

title: NarrativeWorldBench: A Frontier-Saturated Benchmark and a Latent World Model for Long-Horizon Co-Creative Audio Drama
comment: 10 pages. Accepted to the ICML 2026 Workshops on High-dimensional Learning Dynamics (HiLD) and Culture x AI
summary: Long-form serialized audio drama, with arcs that run for 200 to 800 episodes, is a major creative medium and a setting where frontier large language models (LLMs) fail. We benchmark 21 models, spanning classical, fine-tuned, open-frontier, closed-frontier, and reasoning tiers, on a uniform set of structural narrative metrics. All closed-frontier systems saturate at a plot-beat F1 in the band [0.78, 0.81] and collapse by about -0.20 F1 at horizon h=200. We introduce NarrativeWorldBench, an open benchmark of nine narrative-structure metrics evaluated across horizons h in {10, 20, 50, 100, 200}, with cross-lingual evaluation across four Indic languages (Hindi, Tamil, Telugu, Marathi). We introduce N-VSSM, a Narrative Variational State-Space Model that maintains a structured 256-dimensional latent world state over more than 200 episodes via a Mamba-2 backbone with an event-conditioned posterior and an 8B decoder. N-VSSM holds plot-beat F1 >= 0.84 across all horizons at 4x lower compute than the closed-frontier band. A learned Cultural Transfer Function lifts cross-language fidelity by +0.20 to +0.23 Likert points. In a within-subjects writer study (n = 12 professional authors, 240 trials), N-VSSM is preferred over Claude Opus 4.5 on long-arc consistency 71% of the time and rated +1.3 Likert points higher on controllability.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces NarrativeWorldBench to evaluate long-form serialized audio drama understanding and proposes N-VSSM, a narrative variational state-space model that significantly outperforms frontier LLMs on long-horizon narrative structure tracking, including cross-lingual settings.
AI.motivation: Frontier LLMs struggle with long-form serialized audio dramas that span hundreds of episodes, failing to maintain coherent narrative structure and world state over long horizons, especially across multiple languages. There is also a lack of standardized, open benchmarks focusing specifically on narrative-structure tracking at long horizons and in cross-lingual conditions.
AI.method: The authors build NarrativeWorldBench, an open benchmark with nine narrative-structure metrics evaluated at multiple horizons (10, 20, 50, 100, 200 episodes) and across four Indic languages. They evaluate 21 models from different tiers on this benchmark. They then propose N-VSSM, a Narrative Variational State-Space Model that keeps a 256-dimensional latent representation of the world state over 200+ episodes, implemented with a Mamba-2 backbone, event-conditioned posterior, and an 8B decoder. They also introduce a Cultural Transfer Function to improve cross-language narrative fidelity. A within-subjects user study with professional writers evaluates preference and perceived controllability versus Claude Opus 4.5.
AI.result: All tested closed-frontier models plateau in performance with plot-beat F1 around 0.78–0.81 and suffer about a 0.20 F1 drop by horizon 200. N-VSSM achieves plot-beat F1 ≥ 0.84 across all horizons while using 4x less compute than the closed-frontier models. The Cultural Transfer Function improves cross-language fidelity by 0.20–0.23 Likert points. In a user study with 12 professional authors and 240 trials, N-VSSM is preferred over Claude Opus 4.5 for long-arc consistency 71% of the time and is rated 1.3 Likert points higher on controllability.
AI.conclusion: NarrativeWorldBench exposes the limitations of current frontier LLMs on maintaining coherent, long-horizon narrative structures in serialized audio dramas and across multiple languages. The proposed N-VSSM architecture, with its variational state-space world model and cultural transfer mechanisms, substantially improves long-arc plot tracking, cross-lingual fidelity, and controllability, outperforming strong closed models while being more compute-efficient and better liked by professional writers.

title: Incumbent Advantage: Brand Bias and Cognitive Manipulation Dynamics in LLM Recommendation Systems
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are becoming a major way for consumers to find products, but we do not yet understand how brands compete in this new channel. We study brand dynamics in LLM recommendations using skincare products -- a category where consumers cannot easily judge quality before buying and must rely on brand reputation -- across three commercial LLMs (GPT-4o-mini, Claude Sonnet, Gemini 3 Flash), with a robustness check on search goods. In three experiments, we find: (1) a Conditional Monopoly where well-known brands get recommended 100% of the time (IAI = 10.0) when all products have the same specifications, but this dominance disappears with less than a +0.1-star rating advantage for a competitor; (2) authority-style marketing language, including fabricated clinical-evidence claims, breaks this monopoly at a Bias Surplus Value equal to +0.17 rating points, with each model responding differently; and (3) a social dilemma in multi-brand GEO competition: when all brands adopt the same optimization strategy, individual payoff falls from +0.802 to +0.007 in our payoff proxy, and non-participating brands receive zero recommendations in our tests. Our results suggest that generative engine optimization (GEO) should be studied not only as a security risk, but also as an emerging marketing practice that shapes market competition.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how brands compete for visibility in large language model (LLM) product recommendations, using skincare as the main setting, and shows how reputation, small rating differences, and marketing language affect which brands are recommended, as well as the collective consequences when all brands try to optimize for LLMs.
AI.motivation: As consumers increasingly use LLMs to discover products, LLM recommendations become a new competitive channel for brands. However, we lack empirical understanding of how brand reputation, product attributes, and marketing tactics shape which brands LLMs recommend, and what happens when multiple brands simultaneously optimize for these systems. The paper aims to fill this gap and to frame generative engine optimization (GEO) as a marketing and competition issue, not just a security concern.
AI.method: The authors run three experiments using skincare products, a credence-type category where quality is hard to assess before purchase and reputation matters. They query three commercial LLMs (GPT-4o-mini, Claude Sonnet, Gemini 3 Flash) for product recommendations under systematically varied conditions: equal product specifications with different brand familiarity, small differences in star ratings, and different marketing language styles including authority-style copy and fabricated clinical claims. They measure recommendation frequencies, compute an index of algorithmic inequality (IAI), estimate a Bias Surplus Value in rating points that compensates for language-induced bias, and then simulate or test multi-brand competition scenarios where all brands adopt GEO strategies, using a payoff proxy to quantify outcomes. They also conduct a robustness check with search goods.
AI.result: (1) In a baseline with identical product specifications, LLMs exhibit a “Conditional Monopoly”: well-known brands capture 100% of recommendations (IAI = 10.0). This dominance is fragile: even a small competitor advantage of less than +0.1 stars in ratings is enough to eliminate the monopoly. (2) Authority-style marketing language, including fabricated clinical-evidence claims, can overturn the baseline brand advantage, quantified as a Bias Surplus Value around +0.17 rating points, and different LLMs respond differently to this language. (3) In a multi-brand GEO competition setting, there is a social dilemma: when all brands adopt similar optimization tactics, the individual payoff proxy drops sharply from +0.802 to +0.007, while brands that do not participate receive no recommendations at all. These patterns also hold, to some extent, in a robustness check with search goods.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based recommendation is a distinct and powerful channel that can strongly concentrate visibility on leading brands under some conditions, but also be manipulated via subtle rating differences and persuasive or dubious marketing language. Generative engine optimization (GEO) behaves like an arms race: individually rational for brands, but collectively inefficient and potentially harmful to competition and information quality. Policymakers, platforms, and marketers should treat GEO not only as a security or integrity problem but as an emerging marketing practice that reshapes market structure and brand dynamics.

title: MODE-RAG: Manifold Outlier Diagnosis and Energy-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation Evaluation
summary: While Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (M-RAG) enhances Large Vision-Language Models, it remains highly susceptible to cross-modal hallucinations, causal fabrications, and sycophancy. Furthermore, existing mitigation pipelines often face an intervention paradox: static rules tend to unnecessarily disrupt accurate generations, whereas leaving the multi-modal reasoning completely unguided allows existing mismatches to cascade into severe logical fabrications. To quantify and mitigate these hallucinations, we propose a Multi-Agent system, MODE-RAG, driven by Variational Free Energy (VFE) and internal attention states to dynamically gate interventions. High-risk queries are routed to five stage-specific agents, integrating Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for rigorous causal derivation and logit perturbations to penalize sycophancy. Dedicated Correction and Overseer agents ensure formatting stability and perform post-hoc factual verification. To objectively evaluate our approach, we introduce ModeVent, a challenging subset derived from the MultiVent dataset. Extensive experiments indicate that our system effectively reduces hallucination rates and logical fabrication, significantly improving the robustness of M-RAG systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MODE-RAG, a multi-agent, variational-free-energy-driven control system for Multimodal RAG that dynamically intervenes to reduce hallucinations, logical fabrications, and sycophancy, evaluated on a new benchmark subset ModeVent.
AI.motivation: Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation significantly boosts the capabilities of Large Vision-Language Models but suffers from serious issues such as cross-modal hallucinations, causal fabrications, and sycophancy. Existing mitigation approaches use static rules that either over-intervene and degrade correct outputs or under-intervene and allow small mismatches to snowball into large logical errors. There is a need for a principled, dynamic, and fine-grained control mechanism that can identify when and how to intervene during multi-stage multimodal reasoning to improve reliability without harming good generations.
AI.method: The authors design MODE-RAG, a multi-agent architecture that monitors and controls a multimodal RAG pipeline. The system uses Variational Free Energy and internal attention states as risk signals to decide whether a query is high-risk and requires intervention. High-risk queries are routed through five specialized agents, each aligned with a particular stage of the reasoning process. These agents apply techniques such as Monte Carlo Tree Search for explicit causal reasoning and derivation of answer chains, and logit perturbation to penalize sycophantic behavior in the base model. Additionally, there are Correction and Overseer agents: the Correction agent enforces output formatting and consistency, while the Overseer conducts post-hoc factual verification of the generated answers. The approach is evaluated on a new benchmark, ModeVent, a challenging subset of the MultiVent dataset tailored to stress-test hallucination and reasoning robustness.
AI.result: Experiments on the ModeVent benchmark show that MODE-RAG reduces hallucination rates and the prevalence of logically fabricated reasoning chains compared to baseline M-RAG systems and static rule-based mitigation pipelines. The system maintains or improves answer quality while decreasing cross-modal mismatches and sycophantic responses, leading to more robust and trustworthy multimodal outputs.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that a variational-free-energy-guided, multi-agent control framework is an effective way to dynamically manage interventions in multimodal RAG systems. By combining stage-specific agents, causal search (MCTS), and sycophancy-aware logit adjustments, MODE-RAG can substantially curb hallucinations and logical fabrications without over-disrupting correct generations. The authors position MODE-RAG and the ModeVent benchmark as practical tools and evaluation grounds for building more reliable Large Vision-Language Models in multimodal retrieval-augmented settings.

title: A Machine-Learned Comorbidity Index
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026), Seoul, South Korea. 35 pages
summary: Traditional comorbidity scores (e.g., Charlson and Elixhauser) are widely used for risk adjustment and patient stratification, but they have two key limitations: (i) they are largely mortality-centric and do not align well with other clinical outcomes, and (ii) their linear, rule-based structure cannot capture nonlinear, outcome-specific risk relationships. We propose a Machine-Learned Comorbidity Index (MLCI) that maps diagnosis codes to a single scalar by maximizing the normalized Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion (nHSIC) between the learned score and multiple clinical outcomes. MLCI captures nonlinear risk-outcome dependence and is supported by a theory that characterizes when a unified, informative admission-level ordering can be achieved across outcomes. Empirical results on multiple benchmark electronic health record (EHR) datasets show that MLCI outperforms strong baselines across multiple evaluation metrics.

AI.tldr: They introduce a new machine-learned comorbidity index that uses nHSIC to learn a single nonlinear risk score from diagnosis codes aligned with multiple outcomes, outperforming traditional indices on EHR benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Traditional comorbidity indices like Charlson and Elixhauser are designed mainly around mortality and use linear, rule-based weights, which do not fit well when the goal is to predict or adjust for diverse, possibly nonlinear clinical outcomes. There is a need for a unified comorbidity score that better aligns with a range of outcomes and can capture complex dependence patterns between comorbidities and risk.
AI.method: They construct a Machine-Learned Comorbidity Index (MLCI) that maps diagnosis codes to a single scalar score by optimizing the normalized Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion (nHSIC) between this scalar and multiple clinical outcomes. This kernel-based dependence measure allows the method to explicitly maximize nonlinear statistical dependence across outcomes. They also provide theoretical analysis establishing when such a single admission-level ordering over patients can be informative across multiple outcomes.
AI.result: On several benchmark EHR datasets, the MLCI is empirically evaluated against strong baselines, including traditional comorbidity scores and likely other machine learning approaches. Across multiple metrics and outcomes, MLCI consistently yields better performance, demonstrating improved risk adjustment and patient stratification capabilities.
AI.conclusion: A unified, machine-learned comorbidity score based on maximizing nHSIC with respect to multiple outcomes can more effectively capture nonlinear risk relationships than traditional rule-based indices. The proposed MLCI offers a theoretically supported and empirically validated alternative for outcome-agnostic comorbidity adjustment and stratification in EHR-based studies.

title: AIPatient Arena: EHR-grounded evaluation of large language models in end-to-end clinical consultation workflows
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly considered for use in clinical consultation tasks, yet most medical evaluations remain static, single-turn, or narrowly outcome-based, limiting their ability to reflect the sequential, uncertain, and interactive nature of real-world care. Here, we propose AIPatient Arena, an EHRs-grounded evaluation framework for assessing the clinical utility of LLMs across eight dimensions of clinical competence. The framework integrates EHR data into patient-specific knowledge graphs, enabling multi-turn physician-patient interactions. We applied AIPatient Arena on a primary cohort of 437 patients and two out-of-distribution validation cohorts of 119 and 67 patients. We observe that LLMs performed well in medical interview questioning skills (QS; mean scores, 4.43-4.99/5), ethical and professional conduct (ET; 4.38-4.93/5), and clarity and transparency of clinical explanations (EX; 3.80-4.72/5). Performance was moderate in information integration (II; 3.19-4.21/5) and medication safety and justification (MS; 3.13-3.78/5), but persistent weaknesses were observed in handling of ambiguous patient responses (HR; 2.57-3.32/5), information coverage (IC; 2.08-3.02/5), and diagnostic accuracy and reasoning (Dx; 2.63-3.55/5). Process-based evaluation revealed recurrent interaction failures, including repetitive questioning, omission of past medical history, and inadequate handling of uncertainty. Richer conversational context improved diagnostic reasoning but yielded limited gains in treatment planning. These findings indicate that final-answer accuracy alone is insufficient for evaluating clinical readiness and highlight the importance of assessing how models gather, interpret, and communicate information throughout a consultation. AIPatient Arena provides an EHR-grounded framework for workflow-oriented pre-deployment evaluation of medical LLMs.

AI.tldr: Proposes AIPatient Arena, an EHR-grounded, multi-turn evaluation framework showing LLMs do well on interviewing and communication but struggle with coverage, ambiguity, and diagnostic reasoning, proving final-answer accuracy is insufficient for clinical readiness assessment.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM medical benchmarks are static, single-turn, and outcome-focused, failing to capture the sequential, uncertain, and interactive nature of real-world clinical consultations. There is a need for an evaluation framework that reflects full clinical workflows, including how models gather information, reason over EHRs, and communicate with patients across multiple turns.
AI.method: The authors build AIPatient Arena, which converts EHR data into patient-specific knowledge graphs and uses them to simulate multi-turn physician–patient interactions. They define eight dimensions of clinical competence—questioning skills, information coverage, information integration, handling of ambiguous responses, diagnostic accuracy and reasoning, medication safety and justification, ethical/professional conduct, and explanation clarity. They then run several LLMs on consultations for 437 primary-cohort patients plus two smaller out-of-distribution cohorts (119 and 67 patients), and have performance scored process-wise along these dimensions.
AI.result: Across three cohorts, LLMs scored highly on questioning skills (4.43–4.99/5), ethical/professional conduct (4.38–4.93/5), and explanation clarity (3.80–4.72/5). They showed moderate performance in information integration (3.19–4.21/5) and medication safety/justification (3.13–3.78/5), but substantial weaknesses in handling ambiguous patient responses (2.57–3.32/5), information coverage (2.08–3.02/5), and diagnostic accuracy/reasoning (2.63–3.55/5). Process analysis uncovered recurrent interaction issues such as repetitive questions, missing past medical history, and poor management of uncertainty. Providing richer conversational context improved diagnostic reasoning but had little effect on treatment planning quality.
AI.conclusion: Final-answer or single-metric accuracy is not sufficient to judge whether LLMs are ready for clinical deployment. Instead, evaluation must examine the whole consultation process—how models elicit, synthesize, and communicate information in realistic, multi-turn settings. AIPatient Arena offers an EHR-grounded, workflow-oriented framework to perform such pre-deployment evaluations and exposes current strengths (interviewing, communication, professionalism) and critical weaknesses (coverage, ambiguity handling, diagnostic reasoning) that must be addressed before safe clinical use.

title: MapSatisfyBench: Benchmarking Satisfaction-Aware Map Agents through Behavior-Grounded Implicit Decision Factors
summary: Large language model agents are increasingly integrated into map services. Since map services are embedded in everyday-life scenarios rather than professional task settings, users often express their needs informally, resulting in underspecified queries with many unspoken needs, namely, implicit decision factors that are critical for user satisfaction. Although clarification is an effective way to mitigate this issue, it increases user burden in daily interaction, and a capable agent should first proactively recover such factors from available information sources. However, evaluating this ability is challenging. The first challenge is to determine which implicit decision factors are suitable for evaluation. A factor is evaluable only if it affects user acceptance and can be recovered from information available to the agent before it responds. Second, user satisfaction cannot be reliably represented by a single reference answer, requiring a benchmark that converts satisfaction-relevant factors into objective and quantifiable evaluation targets. To address these challenges, we propose a restore-identify-filter framework that reconstructs complete user needs from behavior-chain evidence, identifies implicit decision factors, and retains only those supported by pre-query evidence. Building on this methodology, we construct MapSatisfyBench from large-scale, real-world anonymized user data and annotate ground truth from five dimensions and enables full-chain evaluation of satisfaction-aware map agents. Experiments show that current agents generally perform well on explicit task completion, but remain limited in satisfying implicit decision factors and proactively acquiring the evidence needed for satisfaction-aware decisions. These findings establish MapSatisfyBench as a benchmark for shifting map-agent evaluation from task completion toward satisfaction-aware spatial decision making.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MapSatisfyBench, a benchmark for evaluating large language model map agents on user satisfaction, especially regarding implicit decision factors in underspecified, everyday map queries.
AI.motivation: In everyday map use, people state their needs informally and incompletely, leaving many important decision factors (e.g., preferences, constraints) implicit. While agents can ask clarification questions, doing so frequently increases interaction burden and hurts usability. To design better map agents, we need to measure how well they can infer and satisfy these implicit needs from available evidence, but there is no clear, systematic benchmark for this, and user satisfaction is hard to quantify with a single reference answer.
AI.method: The authors propose a restore-identify-filter framework. First, they reconstruct complete user needs from behavior-chain evidence (e.g., sequences of user actions and choices). Second, they identify decision factors that were implicit in the original query but evident from the reconstructed needs. Third, they filter these factors to keep only those that can, in principle, be inferred from information available to the agent before responding. Using this pipeline on large-scale, anonymized real-world map usage data, they build MapSatisfyBench and annotate ground-truth labels along five satisfaction-related dimensions to enable full-chain evaluation of map agents.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation of current large language model map agents on MapSatisfyBench shows that, while they handle explicit task completion reasonably well, they struggle with inferring and satisfying implicit decision factors and with proactively seeking or using evidence necessary for satisfaction-aware decisions.
AI.conclusion: MapSatisfyBench offers a systematic way to evaluate map agents not just on whether they complete explicit tasks, but on whether they make satisfaction-aware spatial decisions that account for users' implicit needs. The benchmark reveals significant gaps in current agents and is positioned as a foundation for future research on more proactive, user-satisfaction-focused map services.

title: Decoding Hidden Deception in Reasoning LLMs: Activation Explainers for Deception Auditing
summary: As LLMs acquire stronger reasoning capabilities, deceptive behavior becomes an increasingly serious safety concern. Existing deception monitors either score visible transcripts or derive scalar probe scores from representation vectors, leaving little inspectable evidence about why a response is suspicious. We introduce STATEWITNESS, an activation explainer for deception auditing. A separate decoder reads a target model's hidden states, then answers natural-language queries or emits structured reports about them. We evaluate STATEWITNESS on two target reasoning LLMs across seven deception datasets. STATEWITNESS reaches 0.916 mean AUROC, a relative gain of 11.6% over the best black-box text monitor and 25.0% over the best activation-probe baseline under the same evaluation protocol. When combined with existing monitors, STATEWITNESS reduces missed deceptive examples in simple threshold ensembles. Beyond scalar detection, the decoder returns query-level answers, schema reports, and token- or sentence-level evidence traces for human inspection. We view this interface as a potential building block for broader interpretability and alignment tools.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces STATEWITNESS, an activation-based explainer that decodes an LLM’s hidden states to detect and explain deceptive behavior, outperforming existing text-only and probe-based monitors and providing human-inspectable evidence.
AI.motivation: As LLMs get better at reasoning, they can also become better at strategically deceiving users, which is a major safety risk. Existing deception monitors either look only at the output text or compress internal activations into single scalar scores, offering limited transparency and weak, hard‑to‑interpret signals. There is a need for monitoring tools that both improve detection performance and provide rich, interpretable evidence about why a model’s behavior is suspicious.
AI.method: The authors propose STATEWITNESS, a separate decoder model trained to read the hidden states (activations) of a target reasoning LLM. Given these internal representations, STATEWITNESS answers natural-language queries or outputs structured reports about whether and how the target model is being deceptive. It is evaluated on two reasoning LLMs and seven deception datasets, and compared against black-box text monitors and scalar activation probes using AUROC and ensemble detection metrics. The system is also designed to return fine-grained evidence, such as token- or sentence-level traces, and schema-based reports for human auditors.
AI.result: Across seven deception datasets and two target reasoning LLMs, STATEWITNESS achieves a mean AUROC of 0.916. This corresponds to an 11.6% relative improvement over the strongest black-box text-based deception monitor and a 25.0% relative improvement over the best activation-probe baseline under the same protocol. When combined with existing monitors via simple threshold ensembles, STATEWITNESS reduces the number of missed deceptive examples, indicating complementary strengths. It also successfully produces query-level answers, structured schema reports, and localized evidence traces that can be inspected by humans.
AI.conclusion: STATEWITNESS demonstrates that decoding hidden states into natural-language and structured explanations is an effective and more interpretable approach to deception auditing in LLMs. It not only improves detection performance over existing text-based and scalar-probe methods but also yields richer, human-legible evidence about suspicious behavior. The authors suggest that this style of activation explainer can serve as a foundation for more general interpretability and alignment tools that monitor and explain internal model reasoning, rather than just scoring its outward behavior.

title: Dissecting model behavior through agent trajectories
summary: AI agent performance is not just a modeling problem, it is fundamentally a systems problem. The advanced capabilities of models are realized through agent harnesses. Therefore, a gap between model assumptions and harness behavior can easily prevent the model's full capabilities from translating into agent performance. We formalize this as the `intent-execution' gap: the mismatch between what the model intends and what the harness executes, and vice versa. We argue that minimizing this intent-execution gap is as important as other aspects of harness design such as tools and execution loops. To illustrate the impact of this harness-model alignment, we develop a simple and customizable harness called `Simple Strands Agent' (SSA). SSA aims to find the bulk of common patterns which generalize across different model families (such as Claude, Gemini, GPT, Grok, Qwen), as well as a small number of model-specific preferences. We make two contributions: (i) we $\textbf{reproduce or improve on the pass@1}$ performance reported by diverse model-provider families on popular agentic benchmarks (SWE-Pro, SWE-Verified and Terminal-Bench-2), and (ii) building on an $\textbf{analysis of 138k trajectories generated by SSA}$, we look beyond the $\texttt{pass@1}$ numbers which tend to be relatively even across frontier models. By representing agent trajectories in code state-spaces, we observe model-level differences in problem-solving behavior. Finer-grained metrics such as edit frequency, testing activity, and phase-transitions reveal how individual models allocate effort across different stages of autonomous problem solving.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the concept of an intent-execution gap between AI models and their agent harnesses, proposes a simple, customizable harness (Simple Strands Agent) to better align them, reproduces or improves benchmark results across multiple model families, and analyzes 138k trajectories to reveal model-specific problem-solving behaviors beyond pass@1 metrics.
AI.motivation: Although large AI models are powerful, their real-world performance as agents depends heavily on the harnesses that wrap them. Mismatches between what the model is trying to do and what the harness actually executes can silently limit performance. Existing work focuses more on model quality, tools, and loop design, with less attention to this alignment. The authors are motivated to formalize this gap, demonstrate its practical impact, and provide a harness that works robustly across different model families while enabling deeper behavioral analysis.
AI.method: The authors formalize the intent-execution gap as the mismatch between model intentions and harness execution. They design a simple, customizable agent harness called Simple Strands Agent (SSA) that captures common interaction patterns across multiple LLM families, plus a few model-specific adaptations. They then run SSA on popular agentic benchmarks (SWE-Pro, SWE-Verified, Terminal-Bench-2) across diverse models (Claude, Gemini, GPT, Grok, Qwen), generating 138k trajectories. They represent these trajectories in code state-spaces and compute fine-grained metrics like edit frequency, testing activity, and phase transitions to characterize how different models solve problems over time.
AI.result: Using SSA, the authors reproduce or improve on pass@1 scores reported by various model providers on standard agentic benchmarks, showing that a well-aligned harness can unlock model capabilities without highly specialized engineering. Their large-scale trajectory analysis reveals that, despite similar pass@1 scores among frontier models, there are systematic differences in problem-solving strategies, such as how often they edit code, how they use tests, and how they move between phases of the solution process.
AI.conclusion: Agent performance should be viewed as a systems problem where harness design and model behavior must be tightly aligned. The intent-execution gap—misalignment between model intent and harness execution—is a key factor limiting performance, on par with tools and loop design. A simple, well-aligned harness like SSA can both match or exceed reported benchmark performance across model families and expose meaningful behavioral differences between models that are invisible to coarse metrics like pass@1. Future work on AI agents should therefore focus not only on better models but also on systematic reduction of the intent-execution gap and richer behavioral evaluation.

title: Evaluating Second-Order Bias of LLMs Through Epistemic Entitlement
summary: Evaluations of social bias in LLMs largely focus on whether models generate or imply biased content. However, as LLMs are increasingly used as judges of bias, they may exhibit social biases in subtler ways in how they evaluate biased content, which current methods do not systematically capture. We call this second-order bias: social bias in an LLM's judgment about social bias, which we evaluate through a novel, philosophically grounded reasoning task. Drawing on entitlement epistemology, we conceptualize bias as misplaced foundational knowledge that shapes an agent's rational inquiry, and derive a logical reasoning task for LLMs to judge to whom a biased text is acceptable or non-acceptable. We develop two simple metrics to measure how biased LLM judges are in inferring demographics for acceptability without sufficient support, and how these inferences vary across groups targeted by biased texts. Evaluating open and closed models, we find that our task evades safety guardrails by surfacing bias in model judgment. It varies systematically across target groups, reflects implicit social maps, and shows how models are still triggered by demographic labels. Our work points to the need for LLM bias evaluation in judgment tasks and broadly, for more theoretically grounded approaches to bias evaluation in NLP. We release our code and model responses at https://github.com/uofthcdslab/second-order-bias.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and measures “second-order bias”: social biases in how LLMs judge whether texts are biased and for whom they are acceptable.
AI.motivation: Existing bias evaluations focus on whether LLMs directly produce biased content, but LLMs are increasingly used as evaluators or judges of bias. Current methods overlook whether models themselves show social bias in their judgments about who would accept biased content. This gap is important because biased evaluation behavior can silently affect moderation, fairness auditing, and downstream decision-making that relies on LLM judgments.
AI.method: Grounded in entitlement epistemology, the authors treat bias as misplaced foundational knowledge that structures rational inquiry. From this, they design a logical reasoning task where an LLM must infer for which demographic groups a given biased text would be acceptable or unacceptable. They operationalize this task into prompts and create two metrics: (1) how often models infer demographic groups as finding a biased text acceptable without adequate support in the text, and (2) how such inferences differ across demographic groups that are the explicit targets of biased texts. They apply this evaluation to multiple open-source and closed-source LLMs.
AI.result: The evaluation shows that models’ safety guardrails do not prevent these subtler judgment patterns from surfacing: LLMs still make unsupported demographic inferences, and these inferences vary systematically depending on which group is targeted in the biased text. The patterns reveal that LLMs encode implicit social maps and remain sensitive to demographic labels, even when positioned as neutral judges. Different models exhibit different degrees and structures of second-order bias.
AI.conclusion: Bias evaluation for LLMs should extend beyond generation to include their role as judges, with attention to second-order bias in evaluative reasoning. The findings suggest current safety mechanisms are insufficient for such judgment tasks and highlight the value of philosophically grounded, theory-driven frameworks like entitlement epistemology for designing richer bias evaluations in NLP. The authors release code and model outputs to facilitate further research on second-order bias.

title: Can LLMs Be CEOs? Benchmarking Strategic Resource Reallocation with Multi-Role Agent Simulation
summary: Evaluating the decision-making capabilities of large language models (LLMs) is a growing research priority, yet existing benchmarks focus on isolated cognitive tasks such as reasoning, knowledge retrieval, and economic rationality in stylized settings. These evaluations overlook the defining challenge of real executive decision-making: integrating conflicting recommendations from specialized stakeholders under information asymmetry, organizational constraints, and temporal dependencies. We introduce \textsc{CEO-Bench}, a multi-agent benchmark that evaluates LLMs on CEO-level strategic resource reallocation -- the process of redirecting capital across business units in a multi-round, constraint-rich organizational environment. In \textsc{CEO-Bench}, LLM agents receive conflicting advice from four role-conditioned C-suite advisors (CFO, CTO, COO, CMO), each with private signals and distinct priorities, and must synthesize these into a concrete allocation plan evaluated along four dimensions: role integration, conditional boldness, history-sensitive judgment, and plan validity. Experiments across five frontier models on 13 scenarios reveal that all models achieve high structural validity but diverge sharply on strategic calibration -- the hardest capability layer. We identify systematic failure modes including single-advisor capture, conservative default under ambiguity, and historical amnesia, and uncover a structural integration-boldness tradeoff: models that engage more deeply with conflicting perspectives tend to produce less decisive action. These findings delineate the current capability boundary of LLMs as organizational decision-makers and inform the design of future AI-assisted executive systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CEO-Bench, a benchmark for testing LLMs on complex, CEO-level multi-round resource allocation decisions that require integrating conflicting advice from multiple specialized agents.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks test narrow abilities like reasoning or economic rationality in simplified settings, but real executive decision-making involves integrating conflicting stakeholder perspectives under constraints, uncertainty, and over time. There is a need to evaluate whether LLMs can perform such integrated, strategic decision-making tasks.
AI.method: The authors design CEO-Bench, a multi-agent simulation in which an LLM, acting as CEO, reallocates resources across business units over multiple rounds. The CEO model receives inputs from four advisor agents (CFO, CTO, COO, CMO), each with private information and distinct objectives, producing conflicting recommendations. The CEO’s decisions are scored along four axes: how well it integrates roles, its conditional boldness in taking decisive action under uncertainty, its use of historical context across rounds, and the structural validity/consistency of its plans. They test five frontier LLMs on 13 different strategic scenarios within this framework.
AI.result: All evaluated models generate plans that are structurally valid and coherent, but they differ markedly in their higher-level strategy. Performance is weakest on strategic calibration: choosing when to be bold vs. cautious given the advisors’ conflicting signals and evolving information. Recurrent failure modes are observed, including over-weighting a single advisor, defaulting to conservative choices when uncertain, and failing to properly use historical information across rounds. A tradeoff emerges: models that more thoroughly integrate multiple advisor views tend to be less decisive and more cautious in their final allocations.
AI.conclusion: CEO-Bench reveals that current LLMs are competent at producing well-structured, formally valid decision plans, but struggle with calibrated, history-aware strategic judgment in complex organizational settings. There is an inherent tension between integration of diverse inputs and decisiveness. These insights clarify current limits of LLMs as potential organizational decision-makers and suggest design directions for AI systems that support, rather than replace, human executives in strategic resource allocation.

title: Scaling Enterprise Agent Routing: Degradation, Diagnosis, and Recovery
summary: Production LLM assistants route user requests to growing libraries of specialized tools, but how does routing accuracy degrade as the catalog scales? We study single-step routing on a 110-agent, 584-tool catalog from a deployed enterprise productivity assistant, evaluating three frontier models from 10 to 110 agents. Routing F1 on under-specified requests drops 16--23 percentage points across models. An oracle analysis decomposes the degradation into a \emph{retrieval} gap (the model cannot surface the right tool) and a \emph{confusion} gap (even with perfect retrieval, the oracle ceiling drops 10pp). Embedding-based shortlisting recovers +10--11pp F1 at full scale across all three models and two providers. A production annotation study (1,435 human-labeled utterances, three annotators) confirms the recovery on real traffic at +10--17pp despite 10--15pp lower absolute performance.

AI.tldr: Study of how tool-routing accuracy in LLM assistants degrades as the number of tools/agents scales, and how embedding-based shortlisting mitigates this.
AI.motivation: Production LLM assistants increasingly depend on routing user requests to large catalogs of specialized tools/agents. As these catalogs grow, it is unclear how routing performance scales, especially for under-specified user requests where multiple tools might plausibly apply. Understanding this degradation and ways to mitigate it is crucial for reliable enterprise deployments.
AI.method: The authors analyze single-step routing behavior on a real-world enterprise productivity assistant with 110 agents and 584 tools. They evaluate routing performance (F1) for three frontier LLMs as the catalog scales from 10 to 110 agents, and they separate performance degradation into a retrieval gap (failing to surface the correct tool) and a confusion gap (making the wrong choice even when the correct tool is surfaced) via an oracle analysis. They then introduce embedding-based shortlisting of candidate tools prior to model routing and validate improvements in both controlled experiments and a production annotation study with 1,435 human-labeled utterances annotated by three annotators.
AI.result: As the catalog scales to 110 agents, routing F1 on under-specified user requests drops by 16–23 percentage points across all tested models. Oracle analysis reveals that even assuming perfect retrieval, there is still a ~10 percentage point drop due to confusion among surfaced tools. Applying embedding-based shortlisting recovers 10–11 F1 points at full scale across all three models and two providers. A production annotation study on real user traffic corroborates these gains, showing 10–17 point F1 improvement, though absolute performance is 10–15 points lower than in controlled settings.
AI.conclusion: Routing quality for LLM-based assistants degrades significantly as the tool catalog grows, driven by both retrieval and confusion errors, particularly for under-specified requests. Embedding-based shortlisting is an effective, model- and provider-agnostic strategy that substantially mitigates this degradation in both experimental and real production settings, though absolute performance remains lower on real traffic, indicating remaining challenges for large-scale, tool-rich assistants.

title: LLM-as-Judge in Education: A Curriculum-Grounded Marking Pipeline
summary: Generative AI and large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to question generation and automated assessment. However, deploying LLMs in preparation for high-stakes exams requires more than prompt engineering; it demands software pipelines that systematically ground model outputs in authorised curriculum artefacts and marking guidelines issued by education authorities. This paper presents a curriculum-grounded, configurable LLM-as-Judge pipeline for question-level marking, co-developed with an industrial partner, to support exam preparation for university admission. The pipeline identifies the relevant topics, subtopics, and cognitive demand of a question, and assembles verifiable and authorised context to support LLM judgement. Curriculum intent is operationalised through concrete syllabus artefacts, including prescribed verbs and outcomes, performance band descriptors, glossary definitions, and marking-guideline principles. A staged LLM workflow is employed to first generate question-specific rubrics, capturing structured expectations of performance, and then derive and evaluate marking criteria used to allocate marks to student responses. This design improves consistency, transparency, and alignment with official marking practices. Preliminary evaluation shows that the proposed LLM-as-Judge pipeline delivers marking outcomes comparable to human tutors, while yielding justifications that are more traceable to authorised curriculum artefacts and marking standards. The pipeline has also been integrated into an online study platform, where early deployment data provide initial insights into operational usage and manual overrides.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a curriculum-grounded, configurable LLM-as-Judge pipeline for automated, question-level marking aligned with official exam standards, showing human-comparable performance and better traceability to authorised curriculum artefacts.
AI.motivation: Existing uses of LLMs for question generation and assessment rely largely on prompt engineering and are not sufficiently aligned with authorised curricula and marking guidelines, which is critical for high-stakes exam preparation. There is a need for systematic, auditable software pipelines that make LLM-based marking consistent, transparent, and defensible against official standards.
AI.method: The authors design a staged, configurable LLM-as-Judge workflow that: (1) automatically identifies the topics, subtopics, and cognitive demand of each exam question; (2) assembles verifiable, authorised context from curriculum artefacts such as syllabus verbs and outcomes, performance band descriptors, glossary definitions, and marking-guideline principles; (3) uses an LLM to generate question-specific rubrics encoding structured expectations of performance; and (4) derives and applies marking criteria to allocate marks to student responses, while logging justifications and links to the underlying artefacts. The pipeline is co-developed with an industrial partner and integrated into an online study platform.
AI.result: Preliminary evaluation indicates that the pipeline’s automated marks are comparable to those assigned by human tutors, and that its explanations for marks are more explicitly traceable to official curriculum documents and marking standards. Early deployment in a real study platform has produced operational data, including patterns of usage and instances of manual overrides, though detailed metrics are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: A curriculum-grounded LLM-as-Judge pipeline can deliver automated question-level marking that is both aligned with authorised educational standards and practically deployable in an online exam-preparation platform. The staged rubric-generation and marking-criteria workflow enhances consistency, transparency, and traceability compared to ad hoc LLM use, and initial results suggest that such systems can realistically support large-scale exam preparation with human-comparable marking quality.

title: An expressivity analysis of hierarchical modelling in deep transformers via bounded-depth grammars
summary: Deep neural networks are widely believed to derive their expressive power from their ability to form \textbf{hierarchical representations}, capturing progressively more abstract and compositional features across layers. In language modeling, \textbf{transformers} have emerged as the dominant architecture, with early layers capturing local syntactic patterns and later layers encoding more complex clause-level dependencies. While this intuition has shaped model design, there remains a lack of rigorous theoretical work demonstrating \textbf{how} deep transformers represent such hierarchical structures. In this work, we analyze the expressiveness of deep transformer models through the formal lens of bounded-depth, non-recursive context-free grammars. For this class of grammars, we explicitly construct transformers with positional attention whose depth grows linearly with grammar depth, while the neuron count scales with the number of derivation-tree shapes and quadratically with the number of production rules. Our theoretical results support the linear representation hypothesis by demonstrating that these architectures possess the structural capacity to encode abstract grammatical states into low-dimensional, linearly separable subspaces within the residual stream.

AI.tldr: The paper theoretically analyzes how deep transformers can represent hierarchical structures by constructing transformer architectures that simulate bounded-depth context-free grammars, supporting the idea that abstract grammatical states are linearly encoded in the residual stream.
AI.motivation: Although it is widely believed that deep transformers learn hierarchical, increasingly abstract linguistic representations across layers, there is little rigorous theory explaining how they can represent such structures. The authors aim to bridge this gap by providing a formal analysis of transformer expressiveness with respect to hierarchical grammars.
AI.method: The authors study transformers through the formal framework of bounded-depth, non-recursive context-free grammars. They explicitly construct transformer models with positional attention that can realize these grammars. They analyze how the required transformer depth scales with grammar depth, and how the number of neurons scales with the number of derivation-tree shapes and production rules, then connect this to linear separability of grammatical states in the residual stream.
AI.result: They show that for the considered class of grammars, one can construct deep transformer architectures whose depth increases only linearly with the grammar depth. The neuron count required grows with the number of derivation-tree shapes and quadratically with the number of production rules. These constructions ensure that grammatical states correspond to low-dimensional, linearly separable subspaces in the residual stream.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a constructive theoretical demonstration that deep transformers have sufficient structural capacity to encode hierarchical grammatical information in a linearly organized way across layers. This supports the linear representation hypothesis and offers a formal foundation for the common intuition that deeper layers capture more abstract syntactic structure.

title: SEAGym: An Evaluation Environment for Self-Evolving LLM Agents
summary: Self-evolving LLM-based agents improve mainly by changing their agent harness: the structured execution layer around a base model, including prompts, memory, tools, middleware, runtime state, and the model-tool interaction loop. Existing evaluations often reduce this process to isolated task scores or a single sequential curve, obscuring whether an update produces reusable improvement, overfits recent tasks, increases cost, or harms older behavior. We introduce SEAGym, an evaluation environment for measuring agent harness updates across training, validation, test, replay, and cost records. SEAGym turns Harbor-compatible benchmarks into dynamic self-evolution task sources with train batches, frozen update-validation, held-out ID and OOD transfer views, replay diagnostics, and saved snapshot and metric records. Instantiating SEAGym on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and HLE, we compare ACE, TF-GRPO, and AHE under a shared epoch/batch protocol. The results show that these evaluation views provide complementary signals about the evolution process: frequent updates may fail to improve held-out performance, useful intermediate snapshots may collapse later, and source diversity and model backend can affect harness reliability.

AI.tldr: SEAGym is an evaluation environment to rigorously measure how self-evolving LLM agents’ harness updates impact performance, generalization, stability, and cost over time.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of self-evolving LLM agents mostly look at isolated task scores or a single learning curve. This hides whether harness updates genuinely generalize, overfit to recent tasks, degrade past abilities, or increase computational cost. There is a need for a structured, multi-view evaluation framework to inspect these aspects across time and data splits.
AI.method: The authors build SEAGym, an evaluation environment that wraps Harbor-compatible benchmarks and exposes them as dynamic self-evolution task sources. SEAGym organizes data into training batches, frozen update-validation sets, held-out in-distribution (ID) and out-of-distribution (OOD) transfer splits, replay diagnostics, and cost logs. It records snapshots of agent harnesses and associated metrics. They instantiate SEAGym on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and HLE, and run multiple self-evolving methods—ACE, TF-GRPO, and AHE—under the same epoch/batch training protocol to compare their evolution behavior across the different evaluation views.
AI.result: Using SEAGym on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and HLE, the authors find that the various SEAGym evaluation views reveal different and complementary aspects of agent evolution. They observe that frequent harness updates do not necessarily improve held-out performance, intermediate harness snapshots that perform well can later collapse, and both the diversity of the source tasks and the choice of model backend influence how reliable harness improvements are over time.
AI.conclusion: SEAGym enables more nuanced and reliable assessment of self-evolving LLM agents by providing multi-faceted evaluation across training, validation, transfer, replay, and cost dimensions. This richer view reveals phenomena like overfitting, instability of improvements, and dependence on task diversity and backbone models, which are obscured by simpler, single-curve evaluations, and suggests that careful evaluation design is crucial for advancing harness-level self-evolution methods.

title: Evaluating Large Language Models Abilities for Addressee, Turn-change, and Next Speaker Prediction in Meetings
summary: We investigate turn-taking in multimodal multi-party conversations using large language models (LLMs). We construct an evaluation framework for three tasks: addressee detection, turn-change prediction, and next speaker prediction. We compare supervised models trained for these tasks, text-based LLMs, multimodal LLMs (MM-LLMs), and human subjects. Experiments on the AMI corpus showed that LLMs outperformed supervised models and humans in next speaker prediction, despite not being trained on the target domain and without access to audio or visual information. An MM-LLM performed better than text-based LLMs on addressee detection and turn-change prediction but remained below human performance, indicating difficulty leveraging raw audio-visual signals. Ablation analyses revealed that conversational context was critical, particularly for next speaker prediction. We observed that human and LLM prediction patterns were similar, and intervals with frequent turn changes were difficult for both.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well large language models handle turn-taking in multi-party conversations, comparing them to supervised models, multimodal LLMs, and humans.
AI.motivation: Understanding and predicting conversational turn-taking is key for building natural, interactive AI agents that can participate in multi-party dialogues. Existing turn-taking models are often task-specific or rely heavily on audio-visual cues and supervised training, and it is unclear how general-purpose LLMs and multimodal LLMs compare to humans on this ability.
AI.method: The authors build an evaluation framework on the AMI corpus for three tasks: addressee detection, turn-change prediction, and next speaker prediction. They test (1) supervised task-specific models, (2) text-only LLMs, (3) multimodal LLMs with access to raw audio-visual signals, and (4) human subjects. They conduct ablation studies to assess the impact of conversational context and analyze error patterns across models and humans.
AI.result: Text-based LLMs, even without domain-specific training or access to audio/visual data, outperform supervised models and humans on next speaker prediction. A multimodal LLM does better than text-only LLMs for addressee detection and turn-change prediction, but still falls short of human performance on these tasks, indicating challenges in effectively using raw multimodal signals. Ablation shows that rich conversational context is particularly important for accurate next speaker prediction. Both humans and LLMs struggle similarly in intervals with high rates of turn changes.
AI.conclusion: LLMs exhibit strong capabilities in modeling conversational structure, especially for predicting the next speaker from text alone, sometimes surpassing humans. However, effectively exploiting raw multimodal audio-visual information for turn-taking remains challenging, as MM-LLMs still lag behind humans in addressee detection and turn-change prediction. Conversational context plays a crucial role, and difficulty patterns shared by humans and LLMs suggest they rely on similar cues and are sensitive to rapid turn dynamics.

title: DeepInsight: A Unified Evaluation Infrastructure Across the Physical AI Stack
summary: Evaluating a Physical AI stack spans operators that differ by more than three orders of magnitude -- from a single foundation-model decoding step to thousands of physics ticks of whole-body control -- varying orthogonally in modality, reward semantics, and resource profile. No existing framework spans this range, so the stack is evaluated today by stitching together separate harnesses that share neither runtime nor scoring, preserving each segment's local validity but losing the shared identity needed to diagnose cross-layer regressions. We present DeepInsight, an evaluation infrastructure that serves this full spectrum on a single runtime. Rather than homogenize the regimes, it preserves their heterogeneity behind three narrow abstractions -- task, resource, and result -- each realized as one invariant shared by every subsystem: one episode driver, one resource-handle protocol implemented by every expensive backend (LLM inference and sandboxed runtimes alike), and one trace identity scheme under which every event is written. Deployed in production across all three layers of an embodied humanoid stack, this single set of invariants onboards new benchmarks largely by configuration. Where mature peer orchestrators exist -- at the foundation-model end -- it reproduces published references and peer-framework readings within their own spread, runs the same suites faster on a single node, and scales near-linearly across nodes. Its distinctive return is diagnostic: because every layer writes into one shared trace, a regression that begins in one layer and surfaces in another stays localizable on that trace -- a cross-layer payoff no federation of per-segment harnesses can reproduce.

AI.tldr: DeepInsight is a unified evaluation infrastructure for the entire physical AI stack, from foundation-model steps to whole-body control physics ticks, enabling consistent runtime, resource handling, and tracing across layers.
AI.motivation: Current physical AI stacks are evaluated using multiple separate harnesses for different layers (e.g., LLM reasoning vs. low-level control), which do not share runtime or scoring. This fragmentation prevents unified diagnostics and makes it hard to track and localize regressions that propagate across layers of the stack.
AI.method: Define three narrow, shared abstractions—task, resource, and result—implemented uniformly across all subsystems. Concretely: a single episode driver for all tasks; one resource-handle protocol used by every expensive backend (LLMs, simulators, sandboxes, etc.); and a unified trace-identity scheme so that all events from all layers of the system write into a common trace. Implement this within a single runtime capable of serving evaluations from foundation-model inference to whole-body control loops.
AI.result: In production on an embodied humanoid stack spanning three layers, DeepInsight can onboard new benchmarks mainly via configuration. At the foundation-model level, it matches published reference results and readings from mature orchestrators within their variance, runs equivalent suites faster on one node, and scales nearly linearly across multiple nodes. The unified tracing allows direct end-to-end visibility of behaviors across layers.
AI.conclusion: A single-runtime evaluation infrastructure with shared abstractions for tasks, resources, and results can span the heterogeneity of a full physical AI stack without flattening it. DeepInsight preserves local validity of specialized benchmarks while adding cross-layer diagnostics: regressions originating in one layer and manifesting in another remain localizable via a common trace, offering capabilities that federated, per-segment harnesses cannot match.

title: The Benchmark Illusion: Pruned LLMs Can Pass Multiple Choice but Fail to Answer
summary:
Compressing large language models reduces memory use and inference cost, but it can also create failures that standard benchmarks miss. A pruned model may still perform well on multiple-choice evaluations, yet fail to answer the same question in open generation. We ask what pruning changes: does it erase the correct answer, or does it make the answer harder to produce as the top output?
  We study this question with multilingual question answering, tracking the same questions before and after pruning. We find a benchmark illusion. Under high-sparsity pruning, especially Wanda, models often fail in greedy open generation while still selecting the correct answer under multiple-choice scoring. In these recognition-only errors, the answer is usually not gone, but demoted: it often reappears with beam search, sampling, or one in-context example. Overall, multiple-choice benchmarks can overstate the usability of compressed LLMs, creating an evaluation blind spot. Compressed models should be tested on what they can produce, not only on what they can recognize.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that pruning-based compression can make language models appear strong on multiple-choice benchmarks while failing at open-ended generation, because the correct answers often remain in the model but are demoted in its output ranking.
AI.motivation: Large language models are expensive to run, so pruning and other compression methods are widely used to lower memory and inference costs. However, standard evaluations mainly use multiple-choice benchmarks, which might not reflect how models behave in realistic open-ended generation. The authors want to understand what pruning actually changes in the model’s knowledge and output behavior, and whether current benchmarks give a misleading picture of compressed models’ practical capabilities.
AI.method: The authors perform multilingual question answering experiments, evaluating the same set of questions on models before and after high-sparsity pruning (with a focus on methods like Wanda). They compare model performance under multiple-choice scoring versus greedy open-ended generation, and then test alternative decoding strategies (beam search, sampling) and adding in-context examples to see whether the correct answers still exist in the model’s distribution but are no longer top-1 under greedy decoding. They categorize errors to distinguish between erased knowledge and demoted answers (recognition-only errors).
AI.result: After aggressive pruning, models commonly fail to produce correct answers in greedy open generation, even when they still correctly identify the right option in multiple-choice settings. Many errors are recognition-only: the pruned model’s probability distribution still assigns non-trivial mass to the correct answer, which can be recovered via beam search, sampling, or a single in-context example. This pattern is particularly strong for high-sparsity pruning like Wanda and appears across multilingual QA tasks.
AI.conclusion: Compression by pruning often does not eliminate the model’s underlying knowledge but makes it harder for that knowledge to surface as the top output. As a result, multiple-choice benchmarks can significantly overestimate the real-world usability of compressed LLMs, creating an evaluation blind spot. The authors argue that compressed models must be evaluated with open-ended generation-oriented tests—focusing on what they can produce, not just what they can recognize—to obtain realistic assessments of their capabilities.

title: Surrogate Assisted Pedestrian Protection Design via a Foundation Model Orchestrated Workflow
summary:
AI-driven engineering workflows face particular challenges in crash safety design: unlike aerodynamics, crash events involve highly nonlinear contact dynamics, material nonlinearity, and discrete state transitions that are difficult to capture with data-driven surrogate models. To the best of our knowledge, we present the first foundation model--orchestrated workflow for crash safety design that enables surrogate-assisted exploration for pedestrian protection, reducing evaluation time from hours per CAE simulation to seconds.
  The workflow integrates four components: (1) a surrogate trained on CAE crash simulations to predict pedestrian leg injury metrics from design parameters, achieving an average $R^2=0.87$ and providing distribution-free conformal prediction intervals; (2) multiobjective evolutionary search (NSGA-II) to discover diverse feasible parameter sets under user-specified constraints; (3) a morphing-based geometry generator that maps parameters to topology-preserving 3D shapes; and (4) a natural-language interface in which an LLM orchestrates the workflow and a vision--language model supports semantic comparison of generated designs.
  In an automotive front-bumper case study, the workflow produces 35 distinct safety-compliant alternatives from a single exploration, a process that would require weeks with conventional CAE iteration. These results suggest that foundation models can serve as integration layers between ML surrogates and physics-based simulation, helping bring AI capabilities to safety-critical engineering domains.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an AI-orchestrated workflow, driven by foundation models, to accelerate and broaden exploration in crash safety design, specifically for pedestrian protection, by replacing many expensive crash simulations with a surrogate-assisted, multimodule pipeline.
AI.motivation: Crash safety design is computationally expensive and difficult for standard data-driven surrogates due to highly nonlinear contact dynamics, material nonlinearity, and discrete state transitions. Unlike smoother physics domains such as aerodynamics, this complexity hinders rapid design-space exploration. The authors aim to enable fast, interactive, yet reliable exploration of crash-safe vehicle designs while maintaining safety-critical fidelity.
AI.method: They build a four-part workflow: (1) a surrogate model trained on CAE crash simulations that predicts pedestrian leg injury metrics from design parameters, augmented with distribution-free conformal prediction intervals and achieving R^2≈0.87; (2) a multiobjective NSGA-II evolutionary optimizer to search for diverse parameter sets that satisfy user constraints; (3) a morphing-based geometry generator that converts parameter vectors into topology-preserving 3D bumper shapes; and (4) a foundation-model-based natural-language interface where an LLM orchestrates the entire pipeline and a vision–language model enables semantic comparison and discussion of candidate designs.
AI.result: In an automotive front-bumper case study, the system generates 35 distinct design alternatives that meet pedestrian safety constraints in a single exploration run. This replaces what would normally be weeks of manual CAE-driven iteration with a seconds-level surrogate-driven process, without sacrificing diversity or constraint satisfaction in the candidate designs.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that foundation models can effectively act as orchestration and integration layers for linking ML surrogates, optimization algorithms, geometry generators, and physics-based simulations in safety-critical engineering workflows. This architecture enables rapid, surrogate-assisted design exploration in complex crash safety problems, indicating a viable path for deploying AI in other high-consequence engineering domains.

title: OPD-Evolver: Cultivating Holistic Agent Evolver via On-Policy Distillation
summary: Memory has become a standard substrate for self-evolving agents, yet retaining experience is not the same as learning how to evolve through it. Existing memory agents can store trajectories, retrieve reflections, or accumulate skills, but often lack the holistic competence to select useful experience, act on it, write reusable knowledge, and maintain a growing repository.
