We introduce OPD-Evolver, a slow-fast co-evolution framework that cultivates such an agent evolver through on-policy self-distillation. In the fast loop, OPD-Evolver interacts with a four-level memory hierarchy to read, use, write, and maintain experience for rapid test-time evolution. In the slow loop, outcome-calibrated memory attribution and privileged hindsight distill these four abilities into the deployable policy. Across multi-domain benchmarks, OPD-Evolver surpasses memory systems such as ReasoningBank by up to 11.5%, and training-based methods such as Skill0 by ~5.8%. Further analysis shows that OPD-Evolver internalizes high-value experience and memory management, enabling OPD-Evolver-9B to challenge giant counterparts such as Qwen3.5-397B-A17B and Step-3.5-Flash, pointing beyond memory-augmented agents toward genuinely qualified agent evolvers.

AI.tldr: OPD-Evolver is a slow-fast co-evolution framework that turns a memory-augmented agent into a competent “agent evolver,” capable of selecting, using, writing, and maintaining experience for continual improvement, achieving superior performance over prior memory and training-based systems.
AI.motivation: Many current memory-augmented agents can store and retrieve past trajectories or skills, but they struggle to truly learn how to evolve from experience. They often lack coherent mechanisms to (1) decide what experience is valuable, (2) use it effectively at test time, (3) write it back as reusable knowledge, and (4) maintain a scalable, high-quality memory repository. This gap limits their ability to self-improve and to compete with much larger models. The paper is motivated by the need for a principled framework that unifies memory usage and memory management into a learnable competency, yielding agents that can genuinely evolve over time rather than just recall past data.
AI.method: The authors propose OPD-Evolver, a slow-fast co-evolution framework using on-policy self-distillation. The system operates with a four-level memory hierarchy. In the fast loop (test-time evolution), the agent interacts with this hierarchy to read from memory, leverage it for decision-making, write new experiences or abstractions, and maintain or curate the memory store. In the slow loop (training), outcome-calibrated memory attribution identifies which memory interactions were valuable, and privileged hindsight is used to refine how the agent should have read, written, or maintained memory. These signals are distilled back into the deployable policy, so that over time the policy internalizes both effective task behavior and good memory management strategies.
AI.result: On multi-domain benchmarks, OPD-Evolver significantly outperforms prior systems. It surpasses memory-based agents like ReasoningBank by up to 11.5%, indicating more effective use of stored experience, and it exceeds training-based methods like Skill0 by about 5.8%, suggesting that dynamic evolution via memory and self-distillation can beat static skill accumulation. Further, analysis shows that OPD-Evolver learns to internalize high-value experiences and good memory maintenance behaviors. A 9B-parameter instance, OPD-Evolver-9B, can challenge much larger models such as Qwen3.5-397B-A17B and Step-3.5-Flash, demonstrating strong scaling efficiency and validating the proposed evolver paradigm.
AI.conclusion: OPD-Evolver moves beyond simple memory-augmented agents to create an “agent evolver” that learns how to select, exploit, and manage experience. Through a slow-fast co-evolution process and on-policy self-distillation from outcomes and hindsight, the system turns external memory operations into internalized competencies. This yields tangible performance gains over existing memory and skill-based baselines and enables relatively small models to compete with much larger counterparts. The work suggests that future progress in autonomous agents will come not just from adding more memory or parameters, but from learning principled mechanisms for continual self-evolution driven by experience.

title: Closing the Feedback Loop: From Experience Extraction to Insight Governance in Verbal Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted to the ICML 2026 RLxF: Reinforcement Learning from World Feedback Workshop, RLxF@ICML 2026, Seoul, South Korea
summary: Training-free verbal reinforcement learning enables LLM agents to learn from world feedback -- objective signals such as dynamic task outcomes, market returns, or demand forecasts -- by extracting verbal rules from experience and injecting them as context, updating the agent's behavior without parameter changes. However, in non-stationary environments these agents face a retention-forgetting dilemma: retaining stale insights causes negative transfer, while discarding them causes catastrophic forgetting when conditions recur. We identify four requirements for navigating this dilemma -- outcome-driven evaluation, persistent structured evidence, non-monotonic knowledge lifecycle, and compositional governance -- and show that existing methods invest heavily in experience extraction while underinvesting in insight governance. We propose a three-layer architecture -- rules, evidence, and skills -- connected by a feedback-driven curation loop that closes the governance gap. Rules capture distilled experience from world outcomes; evidence logs track each rule's reliability across episodes; skills govern which rules to apply, how to resolve conflicts, and when to abstain. On financial forecasting as a case study, where world feedback is naturally abundant, noisy, and non-stationary, we show that the same accumulated experience either degrades performance below the zero-shot baseline or dramatically improves accuracy and risk-adjusted returns, depending on whether the curation loop is present.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a governance-focused framework for training-free verbal RL in LLM agents to handle non-stationary environments, using a three-layer rules–evidence–skills architecture with a feedback-driven curation loop.
AI.motivation: Training-free verbal RL lets LLM agents adapt using world feedback without updating parameters, but in non-stationary environments, previously learned rules can become harmful or later become useful again. Existing work focuses on extracting rules from experience but not on how to manage, update, or discard them over time. The authors aim to address this retention-forgetting dilemma and close the gap in insight governance so that accumulated experience consistently helps rather than sometimes hurting performance.
AI.method: The authors formalize four requirements for resolving the retention-forgetting dilemma: outcome-driven evaluation of rules, persistent structured evidence about rule performance, a non-monotonic lifecycle where rules can be revived or deprecated, and compositional governance that decides which rules to apply jointly. They design a three-layer architecture: (1) Rules that encode distilled verbal insights from world outcomes; (2) Evidence logs that maintain historical performance and reliability statistics for each rule across episodes; (3) Skills that implement meta-policies over the rule set—selecting, combining, suppressing rules, and deciding when to abstain. A feedback-driven curation loop updates evidence and adjusts rules and skills over time based on new outcomes. They evaluate this architecture on a financial forecasting task with naturally noisy, non-stationary feedback.
AI.result: In financial forecasting experiments, the authors find that simply accumulating rules from experience without governance often leads to negative transfer, degrading performance below a zero-shot LLM baseline. When they enable the full three-layer architecture and feedback-driven curation loop, the same pool of accumulated experience instead yields substantial gains in predictive accuracy and risk-adjusted returns, demonstrating the importance of structured insight governance in training-free verbal RL.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the main bottleneck in training-free verbal RL for non-stationary environments is not extracting more experience but governing the resulting insights over time. Implementing a rules–evidence–skills architecture with a feedback-driven curation loop allows LLM agents to adapt to changing conditions, avoid harm from stale rules, and recover useful knowledge when conditions recur. This governance-focused approach turns noisy, abundant world feedback into a reliable source of long-term performance improvement.

title: Prompt Perturbation for Reliable LLM Evaluation over Comparison Graphs
summary: Evaluating large language models (LLMs) is important for understanding their capabilities, comparing competing systems, and supporting the deployment of reliable models in practice. For open-ended tasks, pairwise evaluation has become a popular paradigm, in which two responses to the same prompt are compared and the resulting judgments are aggregated into an overall ranking. A central challenge of this paradigm is intransitivity: the induced comparison outcomes may fail to support any coherent global ranking. For example, one may observe cyclic preferences such as $A \succ B \succ C \succ A$, or inconsistencies involving ties such as $A \equiv B\equiv C\neq A$. Such contradictions make the resulting leaderboard unstable and challenging to interpret. In this paper, we propose a prompt perturbation framework for improving the consistency of pairwise LLM evaluation. Our approach generates perturbed variants of each prompt, uses the resulting comparison graphs to identify and filter out structurally inconsistent comparison patterns, and then applies standard ranking methods to the filtered comparisons. A key feature of the proposed framework is that graph-level structural consistency is incorporated explicitly into the evaluation pipeline before ranking aggregation. This provides a simple and principled way to reduce cyclic inconsistencies and improve the reliability of LLM rankings.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses inconsistencies in pairwise evaluation of LLMs by using prompt perturbations and graph-based filtering to obtain more stable, reliable rankings.
AI.motivation: Pairwise comparisons are widely used to evaluate and rank large language models on open-ended tasks, but they often exhibit intransitive and cyclic preferences (e.g., A > B > C > A) or contradictions involving ties, which undermine the coherence and stability of leaderboards. The authors are motivated to develop a method that makes these evaluations more consistent and interpretable, so that model capabilities can be compared and deployed with greater confidence.
AI.method: The authors propose a prompt perturbation framework. For each original prompt, they generate multiple perturbed variants and run pairwise comparisons on responses to these prompts. They then represent the outcomes as comparison graphs and analyze these graphs to detect and filter out structurally inconsistent comparison patterns (e.g., cycles or contradictions). After removing these problematic edges, they apply standard ranking/aggregation methods on the remaining, more consistent comparison data to derive a global ranking of LLMs.
AI.result: The framework reduces cyclic inconsistencies in the pairwise comparison graphs and yields more stable and coherent global rankings of LLMs. By enforcing graph-level structural consistency before aggregation, the resulting leaderboards are less sensitive to intransitivity and better reflect an underlying ordering of model quality.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating graph-level structural consistency into LLM evaluation through prompt perturbations and comparison-graph filtering offers a simple, principled way to mitigate intransitivity in pairwise judgments. This improves the reliability, stability, and interpretability of LLM rankings, making them more suitable for comparing systems and informing practical deployment decisions.

title: Brick-DICL: Dynamic In-Context Learning for Automated Brick Schema Classification
summary: Building Management Systems (BMS) are essential for optimizing energy efficiency and operational performance in modern buildings. However, the lack of standardization across BMS points from different manufacturers creates significant barriers to integration and data utilization. While the Brick schema offers a standardized ontology for building systems, mapping BMS points to appropriate Brick classes presents three critical challenges: (i) the extensive number of Brick classes (936 in the latest version), (ii) limited domain-specific knowledge in large language models (LLMs), and (iii) substantial manual effort required for verification. To address these challenges, we propose Brick-DICL, a two-stage dynamic in-context learning framework for automated Brick schema classification. Brick-DICL consists of two primary components: metadata-RAG, which retrieves relevant examples to enhance LLMs' domain knowledge, and class-RAG, which narrows down potential Brick classes to address the large classification space. Additionally, we implement a multi-LLM filtering mechanism that compares predictions across multiple models, flagging low-confidence classifications for human review. As a result: (i) General: Brick-DICL is applicable to any building management system regardless of manufacturer or metadata format; (ii) Novel and Powerful: as the first dynamic in-context learning approach for Brick schema classification, Brick-DICL achieves significant classification accuracy improvements on building datasets, outperforming existing methods; (iii) Efficient: our multi-LLM filtering strategy reduces manual verification effort, enabling rapid digital building onboarding. Extensive experiments demonstrate Brick-DICL's effectiveness across diverse building datasets, accelerating the path toward standardized, interoperable building management systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Brick-DICL, a two-stage dynamic in-context learning framework that uses retrieval-augmented generation and multi-LLM filtering to automatically map heterogeneous BMS points to Brick schema classes with higher accuracy and reduced manual effort.
AI.motivation: Building Management Systems from different vendors have non-standard point metadata, making integration and use of building data difficult. Although the Brick schema provides a standard ontology, automatically mapping raw BMS points to the correct Brick classes is hard due to the large number of classes, limited domain knowledge in generic LLMs, and the heavy manual verification currently required. There is a need for a scalable, accurate, and less labor-intensive method to generate Brick-compliant models from arbitrary BMS data.
AI.method: The authors design Brick-DICL, a two-stage dynamic in-context learning framework. Stage one (metadata-RAG) retrieves relevant example mappings and metadata to enrich the LLM’s domain knowledge and provide tailored in-context examples. Stage two (class-RAG) retrieves and narrows down a small, relevant subset of candidate Brick classes from the full ontology, reducing the effective label space for the LLM’s final classification. On top of this, they employ a multi-LLM filtering mechanism that compares predictions from multiple language models; disagreements or low-consensus predictions are flagged for human review, while high-consensus predictions are accepted automatically.
AI.result: Brick-DICL is shown experimentally to be general across BMS from different manufacturers and metadata formats. It achieves substantially higher classification accuracy on multiple building datasets than prior approaches to Brick schema mapping. The multi-LLM filtering also reduces the amount of manual verification needed, thereby speeding up the creation of Brick models for new buildings.
AI.conclusion: Brick-DICL successfully addresses key obstacles in automated Brick schema classification by combining retrieval-augmented in-context learning with candidate class narrowing and multi-model consensus filtering. The framework improves accuracy, reduces manual labor, and is broadly applicable across heterogeneous BMS sources, thus helping to accelerate standardized and interoperable building management systems.

title: From Trainee to Trainer: LLM-Designed Training Environment for RL with Multi-Agent Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning pipelines for Large Language Model (LLM) training often rely on manually redesigned environments between stages, requiring practitioners to heuristically infer which configuration will best improve the current policy. To automate this process, we propose the LLM-as-Environment-Engineer framework in which the current policy model analyzes failure trajectories together with contextual information and proposes modifications to the next-stage training environment configuration. We also introduce MAPF-FrozenLake, a controllable testbed whose generator exposes multi-dimensional environment configurations, making it suitable for studying and benchmarking environment redesign. On this testbed, we condition the environment engineer on structured summaries of policy behavior, failure cases, and environment statistics, from which it produces the configuration for the next training stage. With Qwen3-4B as the backbone, our framework achieves the strongest aggregate performance on our benchmarks, outperforming larger proprietary LLMs (e.g., GPT, Gemini) and fixed-environment training baselines. We further analyze which forms of context are most effective, finding that successful environment updates rely on failure evidence and preserve configurations that already work. Interestingly, the current RL checkpoint serves as a better environment engineer than the original base model, suggesting that policy learning improves the model's ability to diagnose its remaining weaknesses.

AI.tldr: They propose a framework where an LLM automatically redesigns RL training environments between stages, using failure trajectories and context, and show it can outperform both fixed-environment baselines and larger proprietary LLMs on a new configurable benchmark, MAPF-FrozenLake.
AI.motivation: Current RL training for LLM-based agents often needs humans to manually tweak or redesign environments between training stages, guessing which changes will best help the policy improve. This manual loop is labor-intensive, heuristic-driven, and not systematically benchmarked. The authors want to automate environment redesign, turn the policy itself into an environment engineer, and study this process in a controlled setting with a configurable environment suite.
AI.method: 1) Introduce the LLM-as-Environment-Engineer framework: the current policy LLM inspects failure trajectories plus contextual signals (structured summaries of policy behavior, failure cases, and environment statistics) and outputs an updated environment configuration for the next training stage. 2) Design MAPF-FrozenLake, a multi-agent pathfinding variant of FrozenLake whose environment generator exposes multiple controllable configuration dimensions, enabling systematic environment redesign experiments. 3) Use Qwen3-4B as the backbone policy/environment engineer model and compare different context types fed to the engineer (e.g., with/without explicit failure evidence or environment stats). 4) Benchmark against larger proprietary LLMs and standard RL training with fixed environments, measuring aggregate performance over tasks and stages. 5) Analyze ablations to see which contextual inputs and which model checkpoints serve best as environment engineers.
AI.result: On MAPF-FrozenLake benchmarks, the proposed framework with Qwen3-4B as both policy and environment engineer achieves the best aggregate performance, surpassing fixed-environment RL baselines and configurations driven by larger proprietary LLMs like GPT and Gemini. Experiments show that including explicit failure evidence in the engineer’s context significantly improves environment redesign quality. The framework tends to keep environment aspects that already work and adjust only problematic parts. They also find that using the current RL-updated checkpoint as the environment engineer yields better redesigns than using the initial base model.
AI.conclusion: Automating environment redesign in RL pipelines with an LLM-based environment engineer is effective and can outperform both manual/fixed setups and larger external LLMs on a configurable benchmark. The MAPF-FrozenLake suite provides a controlled testbed for this problem. Successful environment updates hinge on conditioning the engineer on concrete failure evidence and on keeping successful configurations stable. Moreover, as the policy learns, it becomes better at analyzing its own failures and proposing beneficial environment changes, indicating a virtuous cycle between policy improvement and environment engineering capability.

title: FinAcumen: Financial Multimodal Reasoning via Self-Evolving Experience Memory Harness
summary: Financial multimodal reasoning requires agents to coordinate numerical computation, retrieval, visual interpretation, and temporal grounding across heterogeneous evidence sources. Existing tool-augmented agents improve execution fidelity, yet remain largely stateless across episodes, repeatedly rediscovering reasoning strategies and failure patterns. In high-stakes financial settings, this leads to unreliable tool routing, noisy retrieval, and hallucination-prone reasoning. We present FinAcumen, a financial reasoning agent framework centered on selective experience memory for tool-augmented multimodal reasoning. FinAcumen accumulates financially grounded reasoning experience from prior trajectories, distilling successful strategies and failure-derived cautionary rules into a persistent memory bank. During inference, retrieved experiences condition reasoning only when semantic relevance exceeds a calibrated threshold, while irrelevant memory is explicitly suppressed through a fallback mechanism. A deterministic financial tool environment further grounds numerical computation, retrieval, visual decoding, and answer verification.Across four financial multimodal reasoning benchmarks, FinAcumen consistently improves a frozen 8B vision-language model over finance-specialized models and approaches leading proprietary general-purpose models. Further analysis shows that selective experience activation improves reasoning reliability under retrieval uncertainty. Our code is anonymously available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/FinAcumen

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FinAcumen, a financial multimodal reasoning agent that uses selective experience memory to improve tool-augmented reasoning reliability over multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Financial reasoning tasks are high-stakes and require coordinated use of tools for numerical calculation, information retrieval, visual interpretation of financial documents, and temporal reasoning. Existing tool-augmented agents treat each query independently, lacking persistent memory of past successes and failures. This statelessness leads to repeated mistakes, unreliable selection of tools, noisy retrieval, and hallucinations, which are especially problematic in finance. The authors are motivated to design an agent that can systematically accumulate and selectively reuse prior financial reasoning experiences to make tool use more robust and reliable.
AI.method: The authors propose FinAcumen, a framework that augments a vision-language model with a selective experience memory and a deterministic financial tool environment. The system stores prior reasoning trajectories, including both successful strategies and failure-induced cautionary rules, in a persistent memory bank grounded in financial context. At inference time, FinAcumen retrieves experiences based on semantic similarity, but only conditions current reasoning on them if a calibrated relevance threshold is met; otherwise, a fallback suppresses irrelevant memories to avoid pollution. The deterministic tool environment provides controlled interfaces for numerical computation, retrieval from financial sources, visual decoding of financial documents, and answer verification, enabling grounded multimodal tool usage.
AI.result: On four financial multimodal reasoning benchmarks, FinAcumen consistently improves the performance of a frozen 8B vision-language model beyond that of finance-specialized models, and its performance approaches that of leading proprietary general-purpose models. Additional analysis demonstrates that the selective activation of experience memory increases the reliability of reasoning, particularly when retrieval results are uncertain or noisy.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that selectively leveraging past financial reasoning experiences in a persistent memory, combined with a deterministic financial tool ecosystem, substantially enhances the robustness and accuracy of multimodal financial reasoning agents. The selective experience mechanism mitigates hallucinations and unreliable tool routing, allowing a relatively small frozen model to rival or approach much larger proprietary systems on financial benchmarks. The approach suggests a promising direction for building more trustworthy tool-augmented agents in high-stakes domains like finance.

title: Bridging Functional Correctness and Runtime Efficiency Gaps in LLM-Based Code Translation
summary: While large language models (LLMs) have greatly advanced the functional correctness of automated code translation systems, the runtime efficiency of translated programs has received comparatively little attention. With the waning of Moore's law, runtime efficiency has become increasingly important for program quality, alongside functional correctness. Our preliminary study reveals that LLM-translated programs often run slower than human-written ones, and this issue cannot be remedied through prompt engineering alone. Therefore, our work proposes SwiftTrans, a code translation framework comprising two key stages: (1) Multi-Perspective Exploration, where MpTranslator leverages parallel in-context learning (ICL) to generate diverse translation candidates; and (2) Difference-Aware Selection, where DiffSelector identifies the optimal candidate by explicitly comparing differences between translations. We further introduce Hierarchical Guidance for MpTranslator and Ordinal Guidance for DiffSelector, enabling LLMs to better adapt to these two core components. To support the evaluation of runtime efficiency in translated programs, we extend existing benchmarks, CodeNet and F2SBench, and introduce a new benchmark, SwiftBench. Experimental results across all three benchmarks show that SwiftTrans achieves consistent improvements in both correctness and runtime efficiency.

AI.tldr: SwiftTrans is a two-stage LLM-based code translation framework that improves both correctness and runtime efficiency of translated programs via multi-candidate generation and difference-aware selection, validated on extended and new benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based code translation focuses mainly on functional correctness, neglecting runtime efficiency. With Moore's law slowing down, performance matters more, and preliminary results show LLM-translated code is often slower than human-written code and not fixable by prompt tweaks alone. This motivates a new approach that explicitly targets runtime efficiency in addition to correctness.
AI.method: SwiftTrans has two stages. (1) Multi-Perspective Exploration: MpTranslator uses parallel in-context learning to generate multiple diverse translation candidates for a source program. (2) Difference-Aware Selection: DiffSelector compares the candidate translations explicitly based on their differences to select the best one. The framework adds Hierarchical Guidance prompts to better steer MpTranslator and Ordinal Guidance prompts to better steer DiffSelector toward efficient and correct outputs. The authors also prepare evaluation support by extending CodeNet and F2SBench benchmarks and constructing a new SwiftBench benchmark focused on runtime efficiency of translated code.
AI.result: Across the three benchmarks—extended CodeNet, extended F2SBench, and the new SwiftBench—SwiftTrans consistently improves code translation quality along two dimensions: functional correctness of the translated programs and their runtime efficiency compared to baselines and standard LLM translation approaches.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating multi-candidate generation with explicit difference-aware selection, guided by hierarchical and ordinal prompting strategies, can effectively address the runtime inefficiency of LLM-based code translation without sacrificing (and even improving) correctness. Benchmarks tailored for runtime evaluation confirm that SwiftTrans yields translated programs that run faster and remain correct.

title: Beyond Domains: Reusing Web Skills via Transferable Interaction Patterns
summary:
Large language model (LLM) web agents are usually deployed as tool callers: each turn, the model reads a fresh page observation and emits one structured tool action. When every action is a low-level primitive, horizons grow quickly and so do policy-facing LLM completions, dominating latency and cost on benchmarks such as Mind2Web and WebArena. Recent systems therefore wrap repeated interaction fragments as web skills: callable tools built from successful trajectories or induced programs, so one call can replace several primitives. However, prior skill libraries are still triggered mainly by instruction similarity or coarse site metadata, which yields low skill reuse on held-out sites and leaves much of the potential step and token reduction on the table.
  We present SkillMigrator, an agent that learns reusable web skills and transfers them across sites by matching layout structure rather than specific element references. Each induced skill is stored as a transferable interaction pattern (TIP): the skill paired with a structural sketch of the snapshot at induction time. At test time, SkillMigrator retrieves TIPs by layout similarity and grounds their references on the live page. The rest of the stack is standard: accessibility-snapshot observations with stable references, and fixed tool calling over primitives plus skill invocations. Compared with the state-of-the-art approaches, SkillMigrator reduces the average LLM-action count on successful trajectories by 8-10% across both WebArena and Mind2Web at matched success rate.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SkillMigrator, a system that reduces the number of low-level actions LLM web agents must take by learning and transferring reusable, layout-based web skills across sites, improving efficiency on web navigation benchmarks without hurting success rate.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM web agents operate at a low level, issuing one primitive tool action per step based on a fresh page read, which leads to long horizons, many LLM calls, high latency, and high cost. Recent work tries to mitigate this by wrapping frequent action sequences into reusable web skills, but these are mostly triggered via textual instruction similarity or coarse site metadata, limiting their applicability on new, unseen sites and wasting potential reductions in steps and tokens. The authors aim to create a method that generalizes these skills across different websites by exploiting structural similarities in page layouts, thus achieving better skill reuse and more efficient web navigation.
AI.method: The authors propose SkillMigrator, an LLM-based web agent that induces and reuses web skills based on page layout structure. During training/induction, whenever a useful interaction sequence is found, it is encapsulated as a skill along with a structural sketch of the web page snapshot where it was learned, forming a transferable interaction pattern (TIP). This sketch captures layout information rather than concrete DOM element IDs or text. At test time, when the agent faces a new page, it retrieves relevant TIPs by measuring layout similarity between the current page’s structure and stored sketches, then grounds the abstract references from the TIP onto concrete elements of the live page. The execution framework otherwise remains standard: the agent observes accessibility-based snapshots with stable references and chooses between primitive tool actions or invoking learned skills.
AI.result: On two standard web-agent benchmarks, WebArena and Mind2Web, SkillMigrator achieves an 8–10% reduction in the average number of LLM actions required on successful trajectories compared with state-of-the-art baselines, while maintaining a matched task success rate. This indicates that the system can substantially reduce interaction length and, consequently, latency and cost, without degrading task performance.
AI.conclusion: SkillMigrator shows that encoding web skills as layout-based transferable interaction patterns, and retrieving them via structural similarity rather than textual or site-level cues, leads to better reuse of learned behaviors across websites. This structural transfer improves the efficiency of LLM web agents by reducing action counts while preserving success rates, suggesting a promising direction for building more scalable, cost-effective web interaction systems that generalize across heterogeneous sites.

title: SuCo: Sufficiency-guided Continuous Adaptive Reasoning
summary: Despite remarkable performance on complex tasks, Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) often generate excessively long Chain-of-Thoughts (CoT), inflating computational costs even for simple queries. Existing efforts to mitigate this inefficiency typically rely on discrete reasoning modes or fixed budget tiers, lacking a principled criterion of when reasoning is sufficient. In this work, we introduce Minimal Sufficient CoT (MSC), defined as the shortest prefix of a CoT trajectory which is adequate for producing the correct answer. We empirically show that MSC not only reduces reasoning tokens, but also improves accuracy across difficulty levels. Building on MSC, we propose Sufficiency-guided Continuous Adaptive Reasoning (SuCo), a two-stage training framework for autonomous reasoning control along a continuous spectrum. In stage 1, MSC-Aligned Fine-Tuning (MFT) constructs MSC data using problem-adaptive sufficiency thresholds that naturally scale with question difficulty, then fine-tunes the model to internalize concise yet sufficient reasoning patterns. In stage 2, Sufficiency-Aware Policy Optimization (SAPO) further optimizes the model through reinforcement learning with dynamic complexity tracking and sufficiency-aware rewards that penalize both over- and under-thinking. Extensive experiments across mathematics, code, and science benchmarks show that SuCo consistently achieves improvements in both accuracy and reasoning efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Minimal Sufficient CoT (MSC) and a training framework, SuCo, to make large reasoning models generate only as much chain-of-thought as needed, improving both accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Large Reasoning Models often produce unnecessarily long chains-of-thought, which increases computation cost and latency, especially for simple queries. Existing methods control reasoning via coarse modes or fixed budgets, without a principled way to decide when the model has reasoned enough. The authors want a data-driven, principled notion of sufficiency to guide adaptive, token-efficient reasoning without sacrificing—and ideally improving—accuracy.
AI.method:
1) Define Minimal Sufficient CoT (MSC) as the shortest prefix of a chain-of-thought that still leads to the correct answer.
2) Empirically estimate MSC for training data and analyze its properties across difficulty levels.
3) Propose SuCo, a two-stage framework:
   - Stage 1: MSC-Aligned Fine-Tuning (MFT) builds an MSC-style dataset using problem-adaptive sufficiency thresholds that scale with difficulty, and fine-tunes the model on these concise, sufficient trajectories.
   - Stage 2: Sufficiency-Aware Policy Optimization (SAPO) applies reinforcement learning with dynamic complexity tracking and a reward that simultaneously penalizes under-thinking (insufficient reasoning) and over-thinking (unnecessarily long CoT), learning a continuous control policy over reasoning depth.
4) Evaluate on math, code, and science benchmarks, measuring both accuracy and token usage.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks (mathematics, coding, science), models trained with SuCo use fewer reasoning tokens while achieving higher or at least comparable accuracy relative to baselines. The MSC notion reveals that many problems can be solved correctly with much shorter CoT prefixes than full trajectories, and aligning training to these minimal sufficient prefixes improves the model’s reasoning quality and efficiency. SuCo consistently outperforms discrete-mode or fixed-budget baselines in the accuracy–efficiency trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Minimal Sufficient CoT is a useful, principled concept for defining when reasoning is enough, and exploiting it in training enables fine-grained, continuous control over reasoning length. The proposed SuCo framework (MFT + SAPO) leads to models that autonomously adjust how much they think based on problem difficulty, reducing computation while improving or preserving accuracy. This suggests that more nuanced, sufficiency-aware control of chain-of-thought is a promising direction for making large reasoning models both smarter and more efficient.

title: From Brewing to Resolution: Tracing the Internal Lifecycle of Code Reasoning in LLMs
summary: Standard accuracy metrics cannot explain why LLMs handle variable tracking but fail on semantically equivalent loops. We study an internal lifecycle of code reasoning in which models first brew the answer, making it linearly recoverable many layers before it becomes self-decodable, and then diverge into one of four resolution outcomes: Resolved, Overprocessed, Misresolved, or Unresolved. Understanding this lifecycle matters because similar task accuracies can mask fundamentally different failure modes that surface-level evaluation cannot detect. We introduce a dual diagnostic framework pairing layer-wise linear probing with Context-Stripped Decoding (CSD) and apply it to six code-reasoning task families across 16 models spanning Qwen, Llama, and DeepSeek architectures. All four outcomes carry substantial mass in every task family: overall Resolved is only 41.5%, with multiple tasks below 30%. Controlled sweeps over structure, depth, and operators expose task-specific failure bottlenecks: Function Call Resolved plunges from 61.1% to 2.5% as call depth increases from one to three. Across architectures and scales, the brewing scaffold remains stable, with normalized brewing duration 24-42% across all 16 models, while resolution success varies with capability. This indicates that the scaffold is a stable empirical regularity across the tested decoder-only Transformer families, whereas resolution success covaries with capability, scale, and training. Code: https://github.com/euyis1019/llm-brewing

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how large language models internally process code reasoning, revealing a stable ‘brewing’ phase where answers are linearly recoverable before the model can explicitly decode them, followed by four distinct resolution outcomes that explain why similar accuracies can hide very different failure modes.
AI.motivation: Standard accuracy metrics cannot explain puzzling behaviors in LLM code reasoning—for instance, models that can track variables but fail on semantically equivalent loop formulations. The authors aim to uncover the internal dynamics of reasoning that lead to these successes and failures, and to identify systematic failure modes that are invisible to surface-level accuracy scores.
AI.method: They introduce a dual diagnostic framework combining (1) layer-wise linear probing to test when an answer becomes linearly recoverable inside the network, with (2) Context-Stripped Decoding (CSD), which decodes from intermediate states without the original input context. Applying this to six code-reasoning task families and 16 decoder-only Transformer models (Qwen, Llama, DeepSeek), they categorize trajectories into four outcomes—Resolved, Overprocessed, Misresolved, and Unresolved—and analyze how these vary with task structure, depth, and operators.
AI.result: They find that model reasoning passes through a ‘brewing’ phase: the correct answer becomes linearly decodable in middle layers long before the full model can self-decode it. Across all tasks and models, only about 41.5% of cases end in Resolved outcomes, with some tasks under 30%. All four outcomes carry significant mass. In function-call reasoning, the Resolved rate drops sharply from 61.1% to 2.5% when call depth increases from one to three. The brewing-phase duration is remarkably stable across 16 models (normalized 24–42% of the network depth), whereas whether a trajectory ends Resolved varies strongly with model capability and training.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that code reasoning in decoder-only Transformers exhibits a robust internal scaffold: a stable brewing phase where answers are internally available but not yet properly decoded. Failures arise mainly in the subsequent resolution phase, whose success depends on model capability, scale, and training. Therefore, similar task-level accuracies can conceal very different internal failure modes, and diagnosing LLM reasoning requires tools like their dual framework rather than relying on external accuracy alone.

title: LLMs Infer Cultural Context but Fail to Apply It When Responding
comment: 9 pages, 7 figures, 2 tables (24 pages, 12 figures, 8 tables including references and appendices)
summary: Recent work has shown that LLMs overrepresent dominant cultures, particularly Western ones, while marginalizing others. We investigate whether this affects models' ability to generate culturally adapted responses by evaluating their use of local measurement units based on the user's perceived cultural background. We introduce Cultural and Pragmatic Response Inference (CAPRI), a dataset of conversations with varying levels of cultural cues. Experiments with state-of-the-art LLMs show that models can infer cultural background and recall relevant conventions, but often fail to utilize the information to adapt their answers to the relevant cultural conventions, unless explicitly prompted to perform the tasks sequentially. We further evaluate adaptation to the interpretation of time and quantity expressions, two subjective language grounding dimensions that are affected by culture. We find that models increasingly adapt their answers as cultural cues accumulate, but their priors are not culture-neutral, sometimes aligning with the model's country of origin. Overall, CAPRI provides a resource for future research aimed at narrowing the gap between cultural knowledge and culturally adaptive language generation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CAPRI, a dataset to test whether LLMs detect and adapt to users’ cultural context (e.g., units, time and quantity expressions), finding that models infer culture but often fail to adapt responses unless explicitly guided, and that their defaults reflect non‑neutral cultural priors.
AI.motivation: LLMs are known to overrepresent dominant (especially Western) cultures, which may harm their usefulness and fairness for users from other cultures. However, it is unclear whether models merely store cultural facts or can actually generate responses that are pragmatically and culturally appropriate to a user’s background. The authors aim to systematically measure this gap between cultural knowledge and culturally adaptive generation.
AI.method: They construct CAPRI, a conversation dataset containing prompts with different strengths of cultural cues (from none to explicit) and several tasks that require using local measurement units and interpreting culturally dependent expressions of time and quantity. They then evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs on two axes: (1) their ability to infer the user’s cultural background and recall relevant conventions, and (2) their ability to adapt their responses accordingly. They additionally test prompting setups where the model is explicitly asked to perform inference and adaptation as separate steps.
AI.result: Models can generally infer the cultural background from cues and recall appropriate local conventions, and their adaptation improves as more explicit cultural information is given. However, they often fail to spontaneously incorporate this inferred information into their answers unless the prompt enforces a sequential procedure (first infer, then adapt). The models’ default behaviors reflect non‑neutral cultural priors that sometimes align with the model’s origin country rather than with the user’s presumed culture.
AI.conclusion: CAPRI is introduced as a benchmark to study and improve cultural adaptivity in LLMs. The findings reveal a systematic gap between cultural inference and culturally adaptive generation: current LLMs know about multiple cultural conventions but do not reliably use that knowledge to tailor responses. This gap, as well as the presence of non‑neutral cultural priors, highlights the need for methods and training strategies that better align LLM behavior with users’ diverse cultural contexts.

title: Using Cognitive Models to Improve Language Model Simulation of Human Persuasion Games
summary: People make decisions differently in strategic interactions. Some update beliefs like a Bayesian; others exhibit biases like motivated reasoning. Although creators of large language models use simulated humans for safety evaluations and training, they often fail to cover this breadth of human behavior. We argue that cognitive science and economics provide a convenient tool for doing so, making use of mathematical models of human decision-making. We propose an approach that we call Equation-to-Behavior Prompting for guiding large language models to match cognitive models, and evaluate this approach on persuasion games based on legal decision-making. We find that large models can approximate equation-based specifications -- Bayesian updating, affine distortion, motivated updating, and Grether's $α$-$β$ model -- using prompting, but small models fail to do so. However, training small models with reinforcement learning to adhere to mathematical rules, Equation-to-Behavior RL, reduces belief error by 26.5% in out-of-distribution parameterizations. We show that these simulations can help create diverse training environments; training small models to consider different kinds of decision-makers improves average belief change by 2.5%--12% over Bayesian-only training, even when persuading GPT-5-mini. Our work could improve human simulations for training and evaluation in increasingly realistic settings, and could also enable novel research into more complicated mathematical models of human decision-making.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces methods to align large language model behavior with formal cognitive decision-making models, and shows this improves both their fidelity as human simulators and their usefulness for training other models in persuasion tasks.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based “simulated humans” used for safety testing and training rarely capture the diversity of real human decision-making, which ranges from normative Bayesian updating to biased processes like motivated reasoning. Cognitive science and economics already provide precise mathematical models of such behaviors, but LLMs are not systematically guided to match these models. The authors want a principled way to make LLMs instantiate specific cognitive models so they can serve as richer, more realistic agents in simulations and experiments.
AI.method: They propose Equation-to-Behavior Prompting, where formal mathematical models of belief updating (e.g., Bayesian updating, affine distortion, motivated updating, Grether’s α-β model) are translated into prompts instructing LLM agents how to update beliefs in persuasion games based on legal decision-making scenarios. They test whether different-sized models can follow these equation-based behavioral rules via prompting alone. For smaller models that struggle, they introduce Equation-to-Behavior RL: reinforcement learning that trains models directly to conform to these mathematical updating rules and then evaluate generalization to out-of-distribution parameter settings. Finally, they use these cognitively diverse simulated agents as training environments for smaller persuader models, comparing multi-model training to Bayesian-only training when persuading various targets (including a GPT-5-mini).
AI.result: Large LLMs can approximate the target mathematical decision rules via prompting and thus simulate different cognitive types (Bayesian, affine distortion, motivated updating, Grether’s α-β). Smaller models, however, fail to reliably implement these specifications through prompting alone. When small models are trained via Equation-to-Behavior RL, their belief-updating accuracy improves substantially, reducing belief error by 26.5% on out-of-distribution parameterizations. Using these cognitively heterogeneous simulated agents as training partners for persuader models improves persuasion performance: models trained against a mixture of decision-maker types achieve 2.5%–12% higher average belief change than those trained only against Bayesian agents, even when the target is a more advanced model (GPT-5-mini).
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that formal cognitive and economic models of decision-making can be effectively instantiated in LLMs, especially large ones via prompting and smaller ones via targeted RL. This enables more realistic, diverse simulations of human decision-making for training and evaluation. Moreover, training models in environments populated by multiple cognitive types yields better persuaders than training only on Bayesian agents. The approach opens up opportunities for using LLMs as testbeds for richer mathematical theories of human cognition and for constructing safer, more representative human simulations in applied settings.

title: The Slop Paradox: How Synthetic Standardization Erodes Clinical Uncertainty and Cross-Modal Alignment in AI-Rewritten Radiology Reports
summary: AI-assisted clinical documentation tools increasingly summarize, standardize, and reformat radiology reports using large language models (LLMs). We present a controlled measurement of the resulting information degradation. Using 450 chest X-ray reports from the Indiana University dataset, we generate synthetic versions via three realistic LLM rewriting tasks: EHR summarization, standardized rewriting, and teaching case preparation. We measure entity erosion (via medical NER), hedging collapse (loss of clinical uncertainty language), and cross-modal alignment degradation (via BiomedCLIP image-text similarity). Our central finding is a dissociation between information loss and cross-modal fidelity. EHR summarization is the most destructive at the content level, eroding 51.4% of clinical entities and 43.7% of hedging language, yet it preserves image-text alignment almost entirely (a 2.5% drop). The two tasks meant to produce cleaner training data, standardized rewriting and teaching case preparation, do the reverse: they preserve more entities (26.8% and 29.3% eroded) but cause 14.9-16.5% alignment drops, six to seven times those of EHR summarization. We term this the slop paradox: rewriting that makes clinical text look cleaner for multimodal training is precisely what pulls it away from the image. Contrary to our pre-specified hypothesis, rare pathologies were not preferentially degraded: across nine rare-versus-common comparisons, no difference survived multiple-comparison correction, and nominal differences ran in the opposite direction (common > rare), so contamination is invisible to condition-specific monitoring. The dominant determinant of degradation is the type of AI rewriting task, not the clinical content. These findings bear on multimodal medical AI dataset construction and the governance of AI-assisted clinical documentation.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how AI-assisted rewriting of radiology reports using large language models degrades clinical information and image-text alignment, revealing a paradox where cleaner-looking text can be less faithful to the original imaging data.
AI.motivation: AI tools are increasingly used to summarize, standardize, and reformat clinical radiology reports, including for electronic health records and multimodal model training. However, it is unclear how much clinically relevant information is lost and how the rewritten text aligns with the original medical images, which is crucial for safe documentation and reliable multimodal AI systems.
AI.method: Using 450 chest X-ray reports from the Indiana University dataset, the authors apply large language model rewriting under three scenarios: EHR summarization, standardized report rewriting, and teaching case preparation. They quantify three forms of degradation: (1) entity erosion using medical named entity recognition to detect loss of clinical entities, (2) hedging collapse by measuring reduction in language expressing clinical uncertainty, and (3) cross-modal alignment degradation using BiomedCLIP image-text similarity scores between X-ray images and original vs. rewritten reports. They also test whether rare pathologies are preferentially degraded compared to common ones.
AI.result: EHR summarization produced the greatest content loss, eroding 51.4% of clinical entities and 43.7% of hedging/uncertainty language, but preserved image-text alignment with only a 2.5% drop in BiomedCLIP similarity. In contrast, standardized rewriting and teaching-case preparation degraded fewer entities (26.8% and 29.3% eroded, respectively) yet caused substantially larger drops in image-text alignment (14.9–16.5%), roughly six to seven times worse than summarization. Analyses of rare versus common pathologies showed no statistically reliable preferential degradation of rare conditions; nominally, common findings were more eroded. The main factor governing degradation patterns was the rewriting task type itself, not the underlying clinical content.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals a "slop paradox": LLM-based rewriting that makes radiology reports look cleaner and more standardized for multimodal training can significantly misalign the text from the associated images, whereas heavily lossy summarization can remain image-faithful. Since degradation patterns depend primarily on the rewriting use case rather than clinical content and do not preferentially affect rare pathologies, condition-specific monitoring may fail to detect contamination. These results have important implications for constructing multimodal medical AI datasets and for designing and governing AI-assisted clinical documentation workflows.

title: FllumaOne: A Code-Native Multimodal CAD Dataset with Executable Programs and Kernel-Validated Feature Histories
summary:
Parametric computer-aided design records both final geometry and the ordered construction history that determines how a part can be edited. Datasets for editable CAD research should therefore expose modeling operations, parameters, and feature dependencies together with validated geometry. We introduce FllumaOne, a code-native multimodal CAD dataset whose models are generated by executable Python programs in Flluma, a Qt/C++ OpenCASCADE-based CAD system. Each sample aligns its program with a structured feature tree, a training-oriented intermediate representation, STEP geometry, a surface point cloud, natural-language descriptions, metadata, and eight canonical visible-edge renderings.
  The primary release, FllumaOne-100K, contains 100,000 accepted samples across four template-level complexity regimes. Programs are executed and retained only after kernel geometry, solid validity, and export checks; release reports also record modality completeness and split-level duplicate tests. A Qwen2.5-Coder-1.5B LoRA baseline trained on 80,000 samples achieves 99.98% Python syntax validity, 99.97% Flluma build success, and 99.14% STEP-export validity on the held-out 10,000-sample test split. For the 9,909 predictions converted to surface point clouds, the mean normalized Chamfer Distance is 0.002124. The dataset supports conditioned CAD reconstruction, executable program synthesis, feature-tree prediction, B-Rep analysis, retrieval, design completion, and editable reverse engineering.

AI.tldr: FllumaOne is a large, code-native, multimodal dataset for parametric CAD that links executable Python modeling programs to geometry, feature trees, and multiple downstream representations, enabling research on editable CAD reconstruction and program synthesis.
AI.motivation: Existing CAD datasets often provide only final geometry (e.g., meshes or B-Reps) without the full parametric construction history—operations, parameters, and feature dependencies—that engineers actually use to edit designs. This lack of executable, fine-grained modeling data limits progress on learning systems that can generate or modify real-world, editable CAD models. The authors aim to fill this gap with a validated, large-scale dataset where each sample is an executable program with rich aligned modalities.
AI.method: They build FllumaOne on top of Flluma, a Qt/C++ CAD system using OpenCASCADE. For each model, they generate an executable Python program that constructs the part; from this they automatically extract a structured feature tree as an intermediate representation. They run strict validation (geometry creation, solid validity, and export checks) and, for accepted models, export multiple aligned modalities: the program text, feature tree, STEP geometry, a surface point cloud, eight canonical visible-edge renderings, natural-language descriptions, and metadata. They organize 100,000 accepted samples into four complexity regimes and report dataset health statistics like modality completeness and duplicate tests. As a usage baseline, they train a LoRA-finetuned Qwen2.5-Coder-1.5B model on 80k samples and evaluate it on 10k held-out samples across syntax, build, export validity, and geometric Chamfer distance.
AI.result: They release FllumaOne-100K with 100,000 validated, multimodal CAD samples spanning four template-level complexities. Their Qwen2.5-Coder-1.5B LoRA baseline, trained for executable program synthesis, achieves 99.98% Python syntax validity, 99.97% build success in Flluma, and 99.14% STEP-export validity on the 10k-sample test set. For the 9,909 generated samples that can be converted to point clouds, the mean normalized Chamfer Distance between predicted and ground-truth surfaces is 0.002124, indicating close geometric agreement. These results demonstrate that models can learn to reliably generate executable CAD programs from the dataset.
AI.conclusion: FllumaOne provides a large-scale, code-native, and multimodal dataset specifically designed for editable CAD research by exposing parametric programs, feature trees, and validated geometry in a unified framework. The strong baseline results show that modern code models can be trained to produce highly valid, executable CAD programs with accurate geometry. The dataset enables a wide range of downstream tasks—such as conditioned CAD reconstruction, program synthesis, feature-tree prediction, B-Rep analysis, retrieval, design completion, and editable reverse engineering—and is positioned as a foundation for future work on learning-based parametric CAD systems.

title: Improving low-resource ASR using bilingual fine-tuning with language identification: a cross-linguistic evaluation
summary: This study explores how bilingual fine-tuning affects automatic speech recognition (ASR) in low-resource languages. We evaluate this method across nine linguistically and geographically diverse language pairs, covering a range of language families and writing systems. To distinguish the two languages, during training, we pre-pend each input text with a language identification token. At inference, the model jointly predicts both the language and transcription from the speech input alone. As texts for which the language is incorrectly determined show low ASR performance, we also conduct a follow-up experiment in which the language identification token is provided both during training and inference. Our results show that bilingual fine-tuning can be beneficial when language identification accuracy is high, and that in cases where language identification performance is low, including the language identification token at inference helps to improve ASR performance.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how bilingual fine-tuning with language ID tokens affects ASR performance for low-resource language pairs, and when it helps or hurts.
AI.motivation: Low-resource languages lack sufficient training data for robust ASR. Multilingual or bilingual fine-tuning is a promising way to leverage data across languages, but it introduces a language identification (LID) problem: the model must know which language it is transcribing. The authors want to systematically understand how bilingual fine-tuning interacts with LID accuracy across diverse languages and whether explicitly providing language ID at inference can mitigate errors.
AI.method: Train ASR models in a bilingual fine-tuning setup on nine diverse language pairs. During training, prepend a language identification token to each text so the model conditions on language. At inference, first setting: the model receives only speech and must jointly infer language and transcription; second setting: the language ID token is also given at inference (oracle LID). Compare ASR performance across language pairs and conditions, relating it to LID accuracy.
AI.result: Bilingual fine-tuning can improve ASR for low-resource languages when the model’s implicit language identification is accurate. However, when LID accuracy is low, ASR performance drops significantly, particularly on segments where the language is misidentified. Supplying the correct language identification token at inference time substantially improves ASR in those harder cases.
AI.conclusion: Bilingual fine-tuning is beneficial for low-resource ASR only if language identification is reliable. The practical effectiveness of bilingual ASR systems depends critically on LID quality. When LID is weak, explicitly providing language labels at inference (or otherwise ensuring accurate LID) can recover or enhance ASR performance, suggesting that future systems should tightly integrate or improve LID in multilingual ASR pipelines.

title: EComAgentBench: Benchmarking Shopping Agents on Long-Horizon Tasks with Distributed Hidden Intent
summary: As LLM-based shopping agents enter production, existing benchmarks fail to capture how a shopper's requirements arrive: stated implicitly in the query, recorded in a profile, or revealed only when the right question is asked. Benchmarks that expose full intent upfront and grade only the final choice can neither pose this long-horizon challenge nor explain which requirement an agent missed. To address this gap, we introduce EComAgentBench, a benchmark of 662 tasks grounded in real Amazon products and reviews. Each task scatters these requirements across a visible query, a tool-gated profile, and scripted clarification; an agent must uncover hidden intent, verify candidates against attributes and review evidence, and commit to a single product within 100 tool calls. Moreover, typed, source-tagged rubrics grade every task, attributing each failure to a requirement and its source. Construction is automated yet reliable, with every answer fixed in code before any text is generated and every sample validated. Our evaluation of seven models reveals that even the strongest attains only 57.1% overall accuracy, and rubric satisfaction degrades from visible to hidden sources. Overall, we believe EComAgentBench will serve as a reproducible foundation for moving shopping agents from single-query search toward dependable assistance over long horizons.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EComAgentBench, a benchmark for evaluating LLM-based shopping agents on long-horizon, multi-step product recommendation tasks with partially hidden user intent.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for shopping and recommendation agents assume that the user’s full intent is explicitly stated and evaluate only the final product choice. This ignores how real shoppers’ requirements are split across queries, profiles, and interactive clarifications, and it cannot diagnose which specific requirement an agent failed to satisfy. There is a need for a realistic, diagnostic, and reproducible benchmark that stresses agents’ ability to uncover and use hidden requirements over many tool calls.
AI.method: The authors construct EComAgentBench with 662 tasks based on real Amazon products and reviews. For each task, they distribute user requirements across three sources: an explicit query, a tool-restricted user profile, and scripted clarification dialogues. Agents must interact via tools to uncover hidden requirements, check candidate products against structured attributes and review evidence, and pick one final product within 100 tool calls. The benchmark includes typed, source-tagged rubrics that specify which requirements come from which source and allow grading of both overall correctness and per-requirement satisfaction. The construction is automated and code-first (answers fixed in code before text generation), and each sample is validated for reliability. Seven different LLM-based agents are then evaluated on this benchmark.
AI.result: Across seven evaluated models, the best-performing model achieves only 57.1% overall task accuracy. Detailed rubric-based evaluation shows that models are better at satisfying requirements that are explicitly visible in the initial query, and performance progressively degrades for requirements that are hidden in the profile or only revealed via clarification. This demonstrates that current agents struggle with uncovering and consistently honoring latent user requirements in long-horizon shopping scenarios.
AI.conclusion: EComAgentBench provides a realistic, diagnostic, and reproducible benchmark for long-horizon e-commerce assistant behavior, capturing implicit and distributed user intent. The relatively low accuracies of strong models and the observed drop-off from visible to hidden requirements indicate that current LLM-based shopping agents are far from dependable in realistic settings. The authors argue that this benchmark can serve as a foundation for developing and comparing future shopping agents that move beyond single-turn search toward robust multi-step assistance that uncovers and respects user intent over time.

title: When Multiple Scripts Matter: Evaluating ASR in Clinical Settings
summary: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) in non-English clinical settings is challenged by multiscript variability, where the same term may appear in multiple valid orthographic forms. Conventional string-matching evaluation metrics often underestimate ASR performance by treating orthographic variants as errors. To address this issue, we introduce MultiClin, a clinical ASR benchmark designed to evaluate robustness to multiscript variability. Experiments across diverse ASR models show that multiscript-aware evaluation provides a fairer assessment of recognition quality than conventional single-reference evaluation. We further investigate the impact of script consistency during training and find that inconsistent script mappings increase orthographic uncertainty and hinder model convergence, with a balanced 50% mapping ratio producing the highest entropy. In contrast, script unification consistently yields the best ASR performance. Our dataset and code are publicly available at: https://github.com/aitrics-ronaldo/Interspeech_MultiClin.

AI.tldr: They build a benchmark and evaluation strategy for clinical ASR in non-English, multiscript settings, showing standard metrics underestimate performance and that script unification helps models most.
AI.motivation: In many non-English clinical contexts, the same medical term can be written in multiple scripts or spellings (e.g., native script vs. Latin transliteration). Standard ASR evaluation treats these orthographic variants as completely different strings, unfairly penalizing models and obscuring their true ability to recognize content. There is a need for a benchmark and evaluation protocol that reflects multiscript realities and for understanding how script consistency in training data affects ASR learning and convergence.
AI.method: They construct MultiClin, an ASR benchmark for clinical speech in multiscript environments, and define a multiscript-aware evaluation scheme that recognizes multiple valid orthographic forms of the same term instead of relying on a single reference transcript. They then run experiments across diverse ASR architectures, comparing conventional single-reference string-matching metrics with their multiscript-aware evaluation. Additionally, they systematically vary the consistency of script mappings in the training data (from unified script to mixed ratios) to study how different levels of script inconsistency affect orthographic uncertainty (entropy) and model convergence.
AI.result: Multiscript-aware evaluation reveals higher, more realistic recognition performance compared to conventional single-reference metrics, demonstrating that standard string-matching underestimates ASR quality in multiscript clinical settings. Experiments show that increasing script inconsistency in the training data raises orthographic uncertainty and harms model convergence, with a 50/50 mapping between scripts yielding the highest entropy. Models trained with unified script mappings achieve the best ASR performance across setups.
AI.conclusion: Evaluating clinical ASR in non-English, multiscript settings requires accounting for orthographic variability; benchmarks and metrics must be multiscript-aware to fairly measure recognition quality. Script inconsistency in training data introduces harmful uncertainty, while script unification leads to more stable training and superior ASR performance. MultiClin offers a practical benchmark and codebase for studying and improving ASR robustness under realistic multiscript conditions.

title: LongWebBench: Evaluating Structural and Functional Webpage Generation in Long-Horizon Settings
summary: Recent vision-language models (VLMs) have shown promising progress in generating webpages from visual inputs, yet existing evaluations mainly focus on short, single-screen, and largely static webpages. We introduce LongWebBench, a benchmark for evaluating long-horizon webpage generation from both structural and functional perspectives. LongWebBench contains 490 real-world long webpages for structural fidelity evaluation and 507 goal-oriented interaction tasks over 129 webpages for functional evaluation. It employs two complementary protocols: a multi-dimensional VLM-based metric for assessing long-range structural coherence, and a DOM-augmented agent-based pipeline for end-to-end functional verification. We further examine the automatic evaluation protocols through human agreement analysis. Experiments with state-of-the-art open-source and proprietary VLMs under single-image and multi-image settings reveal that structural fidelity degrades as webpage length increases, while visually plausible generations often fail to support executable multi-step interactions. These results highlight the need to evaluate long webpage generation beyond visual similarity, with executable interaction as a core criterion. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/zheny2751-dotcom/LongWebBench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LongWebBench, a benchmark to evaluate how well vision-language models can generate long, interactive webpages, focusing on both structure and functionality rather than just visual similarity.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of webpage generation by VLMs mostly address short, simple, single-screen and static pages, which do not reflect real-world long webpages with complex layouts and interactive, multi-step user flows. There is a need for a benchmark and metrics that capture long-range structural coherence and executable interactions.
AI.method: The authors build LongWebBench, consisting of 490 real long webpages for structural assessment and 507 interaction tasks over 129 webpages for functional assessment. They design two evaluation protocols: (1) a multi-dimensional VLM-based metric to measure long-range structural fidelity, and (2) a DOM-augmented agent pipeline that interacts with generated webpages to verify whether specified tasks can be completed end-to-end. They also perform human agreement analysis to validate the automatic metrics.
AI.result: Experiments on state-of-the-art open and proprietary VLMs, under both single-image and multi-image input settings, show that as webpage length increases, the structural fidelity of generated pages decreases. Furthermore, generations that look visually plausible often cannot support the required multi-step interactions, indicating a gap between visual resemblance and functional correctness.
AI.conclusion: LongWebBench exposes limitations of current VLMs on long-horizon webpage generation and demonstrates that evaluation must go beyond visual similarity to include executable interaction as a key criterion. The benchmark and code are released publicly to foster future research on structurally coherent and functionally reliable webpage generation.

title: Perceptual compensation for tonal context in self-supervised speech models
summary: This study examines the extent to which the wav2vec2.0 architecture exhibits evidence of compensation for phonological context. We conducted a pseudo-replication of a perceptional compensation experiment on Mandarin Chinese tones, and compared the embedding similarities and probing classifier outputs between a purely self-supervised pre-trained model and a model fine-tuned for Mandarin ASR. No evidence of compensation was found in the embedding similarities of the purely pre-trained model. Probing classifiers showed some evidence of compensation in addition to the expected layer-wise improvements in categorization, but failed to replicate human performance on isolated test syllables. Our findings contrast with previous reports of sensitivity to phonological structure emerging through pre-training alone, and suggest that supervised objectives may be necessary to encourage the abstraction of at least some types of phonological regularities.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether wav2vec2.0 representations show human‑like compensation for phonological context in Mandarin tone perception, finding little such evidence from self-supervised pre-training alone and suggesting supervised fine-tuning is needed for some phonological abstractions.
AI.motivation: To understand whether powerful self-supervised speech models like wav2vec2.0 spontaneously learn abstract, human-like phonological processes—specifically contextual compensation in tone perception—or whether explicit supervision is required to induce these linguistic regularities.
AI.method: They pseudo-replicate a human perception experiment on phonological compensation for Mandarin tones using wav2vec2.0. They compare two versions of the model: (1) a purely self-supervised, pre-trained model and (2) the same architecture further fine-tuned for Mandarin ASR. They analyze (a) embedding similarity patterns across phonological contexts and (b) performance of probing classifiers trained on layer-wise representations to detect compensation effects and categorization accuracy, and they compare these to human behavioral results, including on isolated test syllables.
AI.result: The purely self-supervised wav2vec2.0 model shows no evidence of phonological compensation in embedding similarities. Probing classifiers do show some compensation effects and the expected improvement in phonological categorization across layers, but they still fall short of replicating human behavior, especially for isolated syllables. The observed behavior differs from earlier claims that phonological sensitivity emerges from pre-training alone.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised pre-training in wav2vec2.0 does not automatically yield robust, human-like compensation for phonological context in Mandarin tone perception. While probing can reveal partial phonological structure, full abstraction of certain phonological regularities appears to require supervised training objectives, such as ASR fine-tuning, challenging the view that pre-training alone is sufficient for all aspects of phonological knowledge.

title: Shattering the Autoregressive Curse: Dynamic Epistemic Entropy Orchestrated Erasable Reinforcement Learning for LLMs
summary: Although reinforcement learning (RL) has expanded the cognitive boundaries of large language models (LLMs), it often remains vulnerable to the autoregressive curse in long-horizon logical reasoning: small epistemic perturbations introduced early in generation can propagate irreversibly along the Markov decision process flow, triggering cascading failures that drive the reasoning trajectory toward collapse. To overcome this autoregressive cascade, in which a single early mistake can compromise all subsequent reasoning steps, we propose dynamic epistemic entropy orchestrated erasable reinforcement learning ($\text{E}^3\text{RL}$). $\text{E}^3\text{RL}$ eliminates reliance on external signals by grounding the model's endogenous local autoregressive cross-entropy as an intrinsic coordinate of epistemic uncertainty. By introducing segment-level adaptive dynamic thresholds and advantage allocation, $\text{E}^3\text{RL}$ enables the model to precisely excise localized logical defects while reusing historical key-value (KV) cache streams, thereby endowing the reasoning process with a self-healing capability. We train $\text{E}^3\text{RL}$ on the DeepMath-103k dataset. Experimental results show that $\text{E}^3\text{RL}$ reshapes the exploration efficiency of long-sequence reasoning and improves sample efficiency while maintaining linear memory overhead. On mathematical reasoning benchmarks such as AIME, $\text{E}^3\text{RL}$ achieves substantial performance gains, with the 4B and 8B parameter models surpassing previous state-of-the-art (SOTA) results by 5.349\% and 6.514\%, respectively. These findings suggest that $\text{E}^3\text{RL}$ shatters the autoregressive curse in long-sequence reasoning and establishes a theoretical and systems-level foundation for the next generation of self-healing artificial general intelligence (AGI).

AI.tldr: They propose E^3RL, a reinforcement learning framework that detects and erases local reasoning errors in long LLM generations using the model’s own cross-entropy as an uncertainty signal, achieving SOTA gains on math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard RL for LLMs suffers from the “autoregressive curse” in long-horizon reasoning: a small early mistake propagates through the sequence and irreversibly degrades all later steps. Existing methods depend heavily on external signals and cannot selectively repair local reasoning faults without discarding useful past computation.
