The authors want a self-contained, self-correcting RL method that can dynamically detect and fix errors during generation while preserving efficiency.
AI.method: They introduce E^3RL (dynamic epistemic entropy orchestrated erasable RL). The key idea is to treat the model’s own local autoregressive cross-entropy as an intrinsic measure of epistemic uncertainty (epistemic entropy). During training, they apply segment-level adaptive dynamic thresholds on this entropy to detect segments with likely logical defects. An advantage allocation mechanism then guides RL updates to “erase” or correct these defective segments while preserving and reusing historical key–value cache streams from correct segments. This gives the model a ‘self-healing’ capability over long sequences without external supervisory signals, and maintains linear memory overhead.
AI.result: Trained on the DeepMath-103k dataset, E^3RL improves exploration efficiency for long-sequence reasoning and yields better sample efficiency in RL training. On mathematical reasoning benchmarks such as AIME, 4B and 8B parameter models trained with E^3RL surpass previous SOTA performance by 5.349% and 6.514%, respectively, demonstrating strong gains in long-horizon logical reasoning.
AI.conclusion: E^3RL effectively mitigates the autoregressive cascade problem in long-horizon LLM reasoning by enabling localized detection and correction of logical errors based on internal epistemic uncertainty. This provides both a theoretical framework and a systems-level mechanism for ‘self-healing’ reasoning in LLMs, which the authors position as a foundation for more robust, AGI-like capabilities in future models.

title: Environment-Grounded Automated Prompt Optimization for LLM Game Agents
summary: LLM agents in interactive environments are highly sensitive to their prompts, yet prompt engineering remains a manual, task-specific process. We introduce an automated prompt optimization framework for LLM agents that decomposes the observation-to-action pipeline into a goal-conditioned descriptor agent and an action selection agent, and iteratively refines each module's prompt through an LLM-driven evolutionary loop guided by environment returns. We propose a behavior analyzer to attribute episode outcomes to specific prompt components, and a mutator to propose targeted revisions to the prompt, before validating them through environment rollouts. We evaluate on all five BabyAI tasks in the BALROG benchmark, comparing our pipeline against BALROG's RobustCoTAgent under both plain and guided prompt initializations. Optimization improves performance consistently across tasks and conditions, without requiring updates to the model weights. On PutNext, a multi-step coordination task where the RobustCoTAgent achieves 0% success, our framework reaches up to 72.5% success rate using the same underlying LLM with optimized prompts. These results suggest that a multi-agent framework, combined with automatic prompt optimization, enhances LLMs without the need for fine-tuning or extensive human supervision.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an automated framework to optimize prompts for LLM-based agents in interactive environments, significantly boosting performance without changing model weights.
AI.motivation: Prompt design critically affects how LLM agents act in interactive environments, but current prompt engineering is largely manual, brittle, and task-specific. There is a need for a systematic, automated way to improve prompts based on actual performance feedback from the environment, especially for complex, multi-step tasks where existing agents underperform.
AI.method: The authors decompose the standard LLM agent pipeline into two cooperating modules: (1) a goal-conditioned descriptor agent that interprets observations into structured goals or descriptions, and (2) an action selection agent that maps these descriptions to environment actions. They then run an LLM-driven evolutionary optimization loop over the prompts of both modules. A behavior analyzer examines completed episodes to attribute successes and failures to specific parts of the prompts, and a mutator proposes targeted prompt edits. Candidate prompts are validated via environment rollouts on BabyAI tasks from the BALROG benchmark, and environment returns guide which prompt variants survive and are further refined.
AI.result: Across all five BabyAI tasks in the BALROG benchmark, the automated prompt optimization steadily improves performance over baseline prompts, for both plain and guided initializations. Notably, on the challenging PutNext task—where the baseline RobustCoTAgent achieves 0% success—the proposed framework raises success rates up to 72.5% while using the same underlying LLM, demonstrating that prompt optimization alone can unlock substantial latent capability.
AI.conclusion: Automated prompt optimization within a multi-agent decomposition can substantially enhance LLM agent performance in interactive environments, obviating the need for model fine-tuning or heavy human prompt engineering. By using environment feedback and LLM-based analysis to iteratively refine prompts for different submodules, the framework offers a scalable pathway to more robust, high-performing LLM agents across diverse tasks.

title: DecoSearch: Complexity-Aware Routing and Plan-Level Repair for Text-to-SQL
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in translating natural language to SQL, yet existing methods still falter on complex queries requiring multi-step, data-aware reasoning. We introduce DecoSearch, a training-free framework that addresses this by routing each query to the appropriate level of reasoning effort. A lightweight Schema Selector first prunes the full database schema to the relevant tables and columns. An LLM Judger then decides whether the question requires decomposition: straightforward questions follow a direct generation path and complex ones are escalated to a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of atomic sub-questions, each solved by a targeted SQL generation step. A RAG component grounds the decomposer with semantically similar training examples, and a Topology Refiner restructures the reasoning plan when execution failures signal a flawed decomposition rather than a fixable SQL error. DecoSearch achieves 70.53% execution accuracy on BIRD and 88.31% on Spider with a DeepSeek backbone, surpassing all training-free baselines while consuming an order of magnitude fewer tokens than competing methods. It also functions as a model-agnostic wrapper, consistently improving fine-tuned SQL generation backbones without any modification to the pipeline.

AI.tldr: DecoSearch is a training-free, model-agnostic framework that dynamically allocates reasoning effort for text-to-SQL, using schema pruning, query difficulty judgment, DAG-style decomposition, RAG, and execution-aware refinement to significantly boost accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs are strong at text-to-SQL, they often fail on complex, multi-step, or data-dependent queries. Existing methods either overuse heavy decomposition—wasting tokens on easy questions—or under-reason on hard ones, leading to execution errors. There is a need for a training-free, plug-and-play approach that can selectively apply deeper reasoning only when necessary while remaining model-agnostic and token-efficient.
AI.method: DecoSearch introduces a multi-stage, training-free pipeline. First, a lightweight Schema Selector trims the database schema to likely relevant tables and columns. Then an LLM Judger classifies whether the input question is simple or complex. Simple questions go through a direct SQL generation path. Complex questions are decomposed into a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of atomic sub-questions, each answered via targeted SQL generation. A RAG module supplies semantically similar examples to guide decomposition and SQL generation. During execution, if queries fail in a way suggesting a flawed reasoning plan, a Topology Refiner adjusts the DAG structure rather than merely patching SQL syntax. The framework can wrap around both base and fine-tuned LLM backbones without additional training.
AI.result: On the BIRD benchmark, DecoSearch attains 70.53% execution accuracy, and on Spider, 88.31%, using a DeepSeek LLM backbone. These results surpass all other training-free baselines, while using roughly an order of magnitude fewer tokens than competing approaches. When applied as a wrapper to various fine-tuned SQL generation models, DecoSearch consistently improves their performance without changing their internal architectures or requiring retraining.
AI.conclusion: Routing queries to an appropriate level of reasoning effort—via schema pruning, complexity-aware decomposition into a DAG of sub-questions, RAG grounding, and execution-driven refinement—substantially improves both accuracy and efficiency in text-to-SQL. DecoSearch demonstrates that a training-free, model-agnostic orchestration layer can yield state-of-the-art performance among training-free methods and reliably enhance specialized backbones, highlighting a practical path to more robust and scalable natural language to SQL systems.

title: GameCraft-Bench: Can Agents Build Playable Games End-to-End in a Real Game Engine?
summary: Game generation is an emerging application of coding agents, requiring models to transform natural-language specifications into playable interactive systems. Unlike traditional coding tasks, game generation takes place within a game engine, where scripts, scenes, assets, rendering, and runtime interactions must jointly produce coherent gameplay. We formalize end-to-end game generation as the problem of producing a complete game artifact that realizes a specification through observable player-game interaction in a target environment. We argue that evaluating this setting requires three desiderata: Engine Grounding, Artifact Completeness, and Interactive Verification. We propose an interaction-grounded evaluation framework that assesses executable gameplay through replayed demonstrations and rubric-guided multimodal judging. We instantiate this framework as GameCraft-Bench, a benchmark comprising 140 Godot tasks across 15 game families. Evaluations of frontier coding agents show that end-to-end game generation remains highly challenging: the strongest agent achieves only 41.46%, and most agents score below 40%. Further analysis reveals that while agents often implement recognizable mechanics, they struggle to deliver complete games with sufficient content, functional visual feedback, and coherent presentation. See https://tongxuluo.github.io/gamecraft-bench-website for demos, code, and data.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GameCraft-Bench, an interaction-grounded benchmark for evaluating end-to-end game generation by coding agents within the Godot engine, and shows current agents perform poorly, highlighting the difficulty of producing complete, playable games from natural-language specs.
AI.motivation: Existing coding benchmarks mostly test static programming tasks, not the complex, end-to-end process of generating fully playable games inside real game engines. Game generation requires integrating scripts, scenes, assets, rendering, and interactions so that a game actually functions and matches a natural-language specification. There is no standard way to evaluate whether models can produce such complete, interactive artifacts. The authors want to formalize this problem and create a principled evaluation framework and benchmark that reflect the real challenges of game creation.
AI.method: The authors formalize end-to-end game generation as producing a complete game artifact that satisfies a natural-language specification through observable player-game interactions in a target engine environment. They define three key evaluation desiderata: Engine Grounding (working within a real engine), Artifact Completeness (full, coherent game products, not isolated code snippets), and Interactive Verification (judging behavior via gameplay interaction). They propose an interaction-grounded evaluation framework that uses replayed gameplay demonstrations combined with rubric-guided multimodal judging to assess whether generated games meet task goals. They instantiate this framework as GameCraft-Bench: a benchmark of 140 game-making tasks implemented in the Godot engine, covering 15 families of game types. Frontier coding agents are run on these tasks and evaluated using the proposed framework.
AI.result: On GameCraft-Bench, current frontier coding agents achieve relatively low performance, indicating that fully generating games from language descriptions is still very challenging. The best-performing agent reaches 41.46% on the benchmark, while most others score below 40%. Qualitative and quantitative analyses show that agents can often implement core game mechanics that resemble the requested gameplay but fail to produce fully usable games, lacking enough content, reliable visual feedback, and coherent overall presentation.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end game generation in real game engines is a substantially harder problem than conventional code generation benchmarks suggest. The proposed interaction-grounded framework and GameCraft-Bench benchmark expose key gaps: current agents struggle with artifact completeness, polish, and interactive correctness, even when basic mechanics are in place. GameCraft-Bench provides a standardized, engine-grounded way to measure progress and will be useful for developing future coding agents that can construct fully functional, coherent games from natural-language specifications.

title: WallZero: Mastering the Game of WallGo with Strategic Analysis
comment: Accepted by the Computers and Games conference (CG 2026)
summary: WallGo is a recently introduced strategic board game popularized by the 2025 Netflix series The Devil's Plan. Although played on a small 7 x 7 board, its combination of stone movement and wall placement yields high game-tree complexity and intricate strategic interactions. Despite its growing popularity, WallGo remains underexplored. This paper presents WallZero, an AlphaZero-based agent for the two-player WallGo setting. We introduce tailored action and feature designs to improve playing performance significantly. In the evaluation, WallZero defeats two professional Go players who participated in this study, securing on average 1.98x more territory per game. Beyond its strength, we use WallZero to assess game fairness and identify key strategies for mastering WallGo. Interestingly, our results show that the opening used in the Netflix series yields a more balanced game. Our code is available at https://rlg.iis.sinica.edu.tw/papers/wallzero.

AI.tldr: WallZero is an AlphaZero-style AI that plays the strategic board game WallGo at a superhuman level and is used to study the game’s fairness and key strategies.
AI.motivation: WallGo, despite its high complexity and growing popularity (notably via the Netflix series The Devil's Plan), has been largely unexplored in AI research and game analysis. There is a need for a strong AI agent both to push playing strength and to analyze the game’s properties, such as fairness and effective strategies.
AI.method: The authors design WallZero, an AlphaZero-based reinforcement-learning agent specifically adapted to WallGo. They create customized action representations for stone movement and wall placement, and tailored feature encodings for the game state, then train the agent via self-play. They evaluate its strength against professional Go players and use the trained agent to run large-scale analysis of openings and game outcomes.
AI.result: WallZero achieves strong performance, beating two professional Go players in controlled matches and obtaining on average 1.98 times more territory per game. Using WallZero as an analysis tool, the authors examine game fairness and strategic elements, including opening choices, and produce empirical data about which openings lead to more balanced outcomes.
AI.conclusion: An AlphaZero-style approach, carefully adapted to WallGo’s unique action and state structure, yields a superhuman agent that both outperforms expert humans and serves as a powerful instrument for understanding the game. The study indicates that commonly known openings, such as the one featured in the Netflix series, may in fact be relatively balanced, and the released code enables further research on WallGo and related games.

title: Dynamic Rollout Editing for Reducing Overthinking in RL-Trained Reasoning Models
summary: Long-form chain-of-thought reasoning can improve LLM performance on complex tasks, but models often continue generating unnecessary reasoning after a correct answer has emerged. We refer to this behavior as overthinking. We study this phenomenon from the perspective of GRPO-style reinforcement learning (RL) post-training, framing it as a training-time credit-assignment problem rather than merely a decoding-time stopping problem. In rollouts sampled at the onset of GRPO training, we observe that successful trajectories can exhibit a slightly higher degree of overthinking than unsuccessful trajectories for the same prompts. This early imbalance provides a starting point for an undesirable feedback loop: because GRPO assigns sequence-level credit, it cannot distinguish the solution-reaching prefix from the unnecessary continuation that lengthens a successful trajectory. Both receive positive update signal, allowing the initial imbalance to grow into more severe overthinking during training. To address this issue, we introduce Dynamic Rollout Editing (DRE), a training-time intervention for successful trajectories that continue thinking after answer emergence. DRE preserves the accepted verified prefix, edits the remaining thinking, and prefers the edited trajectory within the same RL group, weakening the preference signal for unnecessary thinking without penalizing the reasoning needed to reach the answer. Experiments across diverse tasks show the effectiveness of DRE.

AI.tldr: The paper studies and mitigates "overthinking" in LLMs trained with GRPO-style reinforcement learning, where models keep reasoning after already finding the correct answer, and proposes Dynamic Rollout Editing (DRE) to reduce unnecessary reasoning while preserving performance.
AI.motivation: Long chain-of-thought improves LLM performance but causes inefficiency and potential error due to overlong reasoning. In GRPO-style RL, sequence-level credit assignment unintentionally rewards both useful reasoning and unnecessary continuation, amplifying overthinking during training. The authors aim to understand this phenomenon as a training-time issue and to fix it without harming beneficial reasoning.
AI.method: The authors analyze GRPO rollouts and show that early in training, successful trajectories tend to be slightly longer than unsuccessful ones. Because GRPO gives sequence-level rewards, the entire successful sequence (including unnecessary post-answer reasoning) receives positive credit, forming a feedback loop that increases overthinking. They propose Dynamic Rollout Editing (DRE), which detects when a correct answer has appeared in a successful trajectory, keeps the verified prefix up to and including the answer, edits or truncates the remaining reasoning, and then prefers this edited trajectory within the same RL group so that the RL objective no longer strongly rewards unnecessary continuation.
AI.result: Using DRE in GRPO-style RL reduces overthinking—shortening reasoning after answer emergence—across a variety of complex reasoning tasks while maintaining or improving task accuracy. The experiments demonstrate that DRE breaks the feedback loop that previously favored longer trajectories and that models trained with DRE are more efficient at inference time.
AI.conclusion: Overthinking in GRPO-trained LLMs is primarily a training-time credit-assignment failure rather than only a decoding-time stopping issue. Sequence-level GRPO rewards unintentionally encourage longer trajectories when they correlate with success. Dynamic Rollout Editing resolves this by locally editing successful rollouts to remove or downweight unnecessary continuation while preserving necessary reasoning, leading to more efficient models without degrading performance.

title: A homotopy-type-theoretic generalization of neurosymbolic inference
summary: A wide range of neurosymbolic (NeSy) systems compute one functional: a belief-weighted sum of a logical quantity over a space of $σ$-structures, of which weighted model counting, fuzzy logic, and probabilistic logic are special cases. This account is built on sets, and a set deliberately forgets two things that are important for NeSy: when two $σ$-structures are the same up to a symmetry of the theory, and how many distinct proofs witness a query. Replacing the underlying sets by types, in the sense of homotopy type theory, preserves this information, and turns this functional into a belief-weighted homotopy cardinality, a notion of size that counts each object in inverse proportion to its symmetries. We develop the framework from scratch for NeSy systems, prove a conservativity theorem that recovers the classical functional when symmetries are trivial, and show that the symmetry our framework exposes is exactly the one behind reasoning shortcuts. The payoff is concrete: the shortcut-aware concept posterior that recent methods reach by ensembling or expressive density estimation is the only symmetry-invariant point of the confusion-set simplex, computable in closed form by averaging a single model over the symmetry group. On MNIST reasoning-shortcut benchmarks this single-model wrapper is better calibrated than a diversity-trained ensemble, while leaving label accuracy and identifiable concepts untouched. Code is freely available at https://github.com/bio-ontology-research-group/hott-nesy.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a homotopy type theory–based neurosymbolic framework that generalizes weighted model counting and related logics by replacing sets of structures with types, enabling symmetry-aware, proof-count–sensitive reasoning and yielding closed-form, shortcut-aware concept posteriors that outperform ensembles on MNIST shortcut benchmarks while remaining conservative in the symmetry-free case.
AI.motivation: Classical neurosymbolic systems (e.g., weighted model counting, fuzzy and probabilistic logic) operate over sets of σ-structures and thus ignore two crucial aspects: (1) symmetries between structures that are effectively the same for the theory, and (2) how many distinct proofs support a query. These omissions matter for real-world NeSy tasks where reasoning shortcuts, symmetries, and multiple explanations affect uncertainty and calibration. The authors seek a principled, mathematically grounded way to incorporate symmetries and proof multiplicities into NeSy inference without losing compatibility with existing methods.
AI.method: The authors recast the central NeSy functional—a belief-weighted sum of a logical quantity over σ-structures—within homotopy type theory by replacing underlying sets with types. They define a belief-weighted homotopy cardinality, which measures the size of a space by counting each object inversely to the size of its symmetry group. They construct this framework from first principles for NeSy systems and prove a conservativity theorem showing that when symmetries are trivial, their functional collapses to the classical one. They then identify the symmetry captured by their framework as exactly the symmetry underlying reasoning shortcuts and derive a closed-form expression for a shortcut-aware concept posterior as the unique symmetry-invariant point in the confusion-set simplex, computable via averaging a single model over the relevant symmetry group. Empirically, they implement this as a single-model wrapper and evaluate it on MNIST reasoning-shortcut benchmarks.
AI.result: The main theoretical results include: (1) a formalization of the belief-weighted homotopy cardinality for NeSy systems; (2) a conservativity theorem that recovers standard weighted-sum functionals when the symmetry group is trivial; and (3) a characterization of the shortcut-aware concept posterior as the unique symmetry-invariant point of the confusion-set simplex, obtainable by group-averaging a single model. Experimentally, on MNIST benchmarks involving reasoning shortcuts, the proposed single-model symmetry-averaging wrapper yields better probabilistic calibration than diversity-trained ensembles, while maintaining label accuracy and preserving identifiable concepts. They release an implementation at the provided GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: By lifting neurosymbolic reasoning from sets to homotopy types, the paper introduces a belief-weighted homotopy cardinality that naturally incorporates symmetries and proof multiplicities. This unifies and generalizes existing NeSy formalisms while remaining conservative when no symmetries are present. The framework reveals that shortcut-aware reasoning corresponds to enforcing symmetry invariance, enabling a simple yet powerful single-model group-averaging procedure that matches or surpasses ensemble-based approaches in calibration without sacrificing accuracy or concept identifiability. This positions homotopy type theory as a practical and principled foundation for symmetry-aware neurosymbolic AI.

title: ChLogic: Evaluating Robustness of Logical Reasoning in Chinese Expressions
summary: Large language models perform increasingly well on standardized logical reasoning benchmarks, but whether this ability remains robust beyond English is unclear. We introduce ChLogic, an English--Chinese aligned benchmark that tests whether models preserve logical reasoning performance when the same latent logical structure is expressed in English and diverse Chinese surface realizations. Built from formal logical templates, the benchmark contains three data sets: (i) the General aligned set, derived from 60 General Propositions across nine template families; (ii) the Difficult aligned set, derived from 40 Difficult Problems; and (iii) the Chinese-only set, covering 15 language-specific phenomenon types. Each aligned item pairs one English reference expression with five Chinese realizations. Experiments on Qwen3, Ministral, and GLM models reveal a persistent English--Chinese performance gap. Back-translation from standard Chinese into English often improves performance on the General aligned set, but produces mixed effects on the Difficult aligned set, where Qwen3-32B and GLM-5.1 perform worse after translation. These results indicate that Chinese surface realization, translation artifacts, and model-specific behavior jointly affect multilingual logical reasoning. Overall, ChLogic provides a useful stress test for the robustness of multilingual reasoning.

AI.tldr: ChLogic is a bilingual English–Chinese logical reasoning benchmark showing that current large language models reason less reliably in Chinese than in English, even when the underlying logic is the same.
AI.motivation: Although large language models achieve strong scores on logical reasoning benchmarks, these are mostly in English. It is unclear whether their reasoning ability is robust across languages, especially when the same logical structure is expressed in different surface forms. The paper aims to systematically test whether models maintain logical consistency between English and Chinese and to understand how language, translation, and model architecture affect multilingual reasoning performance.
AI.method: The authors construct ChLogic, an English–Chinese aligned benchmark built from formal logical templates. It includes: (i) a General aligned set from 60 general logical propositions across 9 template families; (ii) a Difficult aligned set from 40 challenging logical problems; and (iii) a Chinese-only set targeting 15 Chinese-specific linguistic phenomena. For each aligned logical item, they create one English version and five diverse Chinese surface realizations. They then evaluate several LLM families (Qwen3, Ministral, GLM) on all sets, compare English vs Chinese accuracy, and test a back-translation setup where Chinese items are first translated into English before being solved by the models.
AI.result: Models exhibit a consistent performance gap, doing better on English than on Chinese, even though the underlying logical structures are equivalent. Back-translation of Chinese prompts into English often improves performance on the General aligned set, suggesting that models reason more reliably in English. However, for the more complex Difficult aligned set, back-translation has mixed or negative effects—e.g., Qwen3-32B and GLM-5.1 sometimes perform worse after translation. The Chinese-only phenomena further reveal sensitivity to particular surface realizations unique to Chinese.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current LLMs’ logical reasoning is not robust across languages: performance depends not only on the abstract logical form but also heavily on the surface language, translation artifacts, and model-specific behaviors. ChLogic serves as a targeted stress test for multilingual logical reasoning and highlights the need to improve cross-lingual robustness, better handle Chinese-specific constructions, and reduce reliance on English-centric reasoning capabilities.

title: FlowRAG: Synergizing Explicit Reasoning via Frequency-Aware Multi-Granularity Graph Flow
summary: Graph-based retrieval-augmented generation (GraphRAG) is effective for knowledge-intensive and multi-hop query tasks; however, many existing methods primarily seed entity-based graphs and rely on implicit semantic relevance propagation. This often (i) under-retrieves when user queries are abstract and semantically sparse at the entity level, and (ii) suffers from brittle multi-hop reasoning, where noisy activations can derail entity-to-entity transitions and corrupt the inferred relation chain, yielding unreliable conclusions. To this end, we propose \texttt{FlowRAG}, a semantic-aware retrieval framework that improves both semantic recall and explicit reasoning. Specifically, \texttt{FlowRAG} constructs a quad-level heterogeneous graph over passages, summaries, sentences, and entities, where summary nodes serve as a coarse semantic hub. At retrieval time, a dual-granularity activation module combines summary--query alignment with sentence-level matching to activate relevant entities under paraphrase and abstraction robustly. We then introduce a frequency-aware weighted flow module that routes relevance through entity--passage links weighted by within-passage term frequency, pruning noisy connections and extracting high-confidence reasoning paths as an explicit logic skeleton for generation. Extensive experiments show that \texttt{FlowRAG} obtains state-of-the-art performance on complex reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: FlowRAG is a graph-based RAG framework that improves semantic recall and multi-hop reasoning by using a quad-level heterogeneous graph and frequency-weighted relevance flow to derive explicit reasoning paths.
AI.motivation: Existing graph-based RAG methods mostly rely on entity-centric graphs and implicit semantic propagation, which underperform when queries are abstract or semantically sparse at the entity level and are brittle in multi-hop reasoning due to noisy entity transitions that corrupt relation chains and conclusions.
AI.method: FlowRAG builds a heterogeneous graph with four node types—passages, summaries, sentences, and entities—where summaries act as semantic hubs. At retrieval time, a dual-granularity activation module combines summary–query alignment with sentence-level matching to robustly activate relevant entities even under paraphrase or abstraction. A frequency-aware weighted flow module then routes relevance through entity–passage edges weighted by within-passage term frequency, pruning noisy edges and extracting high-confidence reasoning paths that serve as an explicit logical skeleton for generation.
AI.result: Across multiple complex reasoning benchmarks, FlowRAG achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to prior RAG and graph-based RAG baselines, demonstrating better semantic recall and more reliable multi-hop reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating a quad-level heterogeneous graph with semantic-hub summaries, dual-granularity activation, and frequency-weighted flow enables more robust retrieval and explicit reasoning in RAG systems, improving performance on knowledge-intensive, multi-hop tasks.

title: Learning task-specific subspaces via interventional post-training of speech foundation models
comment: Accepted to Interspeech 2026; 6 pages (4 main body), 2 figures
summary: Speech foundation models, pre-trained on large corpora of unlabelled speech data, produce general-purpose representations which are useful across tasks. However, these representations encode information about salient speech variables in a distributed manner, while downstream speech tasks rely on only some of this variability. In this work, we propose a post-training refinement approach using interventional contrastive learning. By leveraging an interventional dataset and multi-part contrastive loss, we learn a transformation from the entangled representation space of speech foundation models into separate content and speaker subspaces. We evaluate the learnt representations on speaker verification and keyword spotting tasks, showing improved out-of-domain speaker verification performance and evidence that speaker and content information are separated across the learned subspaces.

AI.tldr: They refine general speech foundation model representations into disentangled content and speaker subspaces using interventional contrastive learning, improving speaker verification and keyword spotting.
AI.motivation: Speech foundation models yield powerful but highly entangled representations that mix different speech factors (like who is speaking and what is being said). Many downstream tasks only need specific aspects, such as speaker identity or linguistic content, and entanglement can hurt performance and generalization. The authors are motivated to structurally disentangle these factors post hoc, without retraining the large foundation model, to better support specialized speech tasks.
AI.method: Starting from a pre-trained speech foundation model, the authors introduce a post-training refinement step based on interventional contrastive learning. They use an interventional dataset where speaker and content factors can be systematically varied (e.g., same content with different speakers, or same speaker with different content). A multi-part contrastive loss encourages a learned transformation that maps the original, entangled representations into two separate subspaces: one dedicated to content information and one to speaker information. The loss is structured so that representations sharing a factor (speaker or content) are pulled together in the appropriate subspace and pushed apart otherwise, effectively factorizing the latent space.
AI.result: The learned subspaces are evaluated on speaker verification and keyword spotting tasks. The refined representations improve out-of-domain speaker verification performance compared to using the raw foundation model features, indicating better generalization of speaker representations. For keyword spotting, the results demonstrate that content- and speaker-related information is predominantly captured in their respective subspaces, providing empirical evidence of effective separation between speaker and content factors.
AI.conclusion: Post-training refinement via interventional contrastive learning can successfully disentangle speaker and content information in representations from speech foundation models. This factorization yields more task-appropriate subspaces and improves performance, especially in out-of-domain speaker verification, without requiring re-training of the original large-scale model. The approach suggests a general strategy for retrofitting disentangled, interpretable subspaces onto existing foundation model embeddings for specialized downstream speech tasks.

title: StepGuard: Guarding Web Navigation via Single-Step Calibration
summary: Web navigation requires agents to follow natural language goals, interact with web pages, and produce accurate answers. While recent advances leverage vision-language models and reinforcement learning, existing methods still suffer from single-step fragility due to reward misalignment and error propagation. To tackle the reward entanglement, we design Dynamic Dual-Policy Optimization (DDPO), which dynamically switches between a navigation-first mode for exploration and an answer-first mode for question-answering to mitigate reward conflict. To calibrate the single-step error, we propose Confidence-Guided Adaptive Navigation Reflection (CANR), a mechanism that estimates per-step confidence, triggers reflection only when necessary, and uses contrastive rewards to encourage self-correction to calibrate the single-step inaccuracy. With the above as the main components, we finally develop our StepGuard, a new framework of Guarding Web Navigation via Single-Step Calibration. Experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly improves navigation and answer accuracy, setting new state-of-the-art performance on standard web navigation benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces StepGuard, a framework for web navigation that improves both navigation and answer accuracy via single-step calibration using dual policies and confidence-guided reflection.
AI.motivation: Existing web navigation agents using vision-language models and reinforcement learning struggle with reward misalignment between navigation and answering, and are fragile to single-step errors that propagate and degrade final performance. The authors aim to disentangle conflicting objectives and reduce error propagation in multi-step web tasks.
AI.method: They propose Dynamic Dual-Policy Optimization (DDPO), which dynamically switches between a navigation-first policy for exploration and an answer-first policy for question-answering to alleviate reward conflict. They further introduce Confidence-Guided Adaptive Navigation Reflection (CANR), which estimates step-wise confidence, selectively triggers reflection only when needed, and uses contrastive rewards to encourage self-correction of low-confidence or inaccurate steps. These components are integrated into the StepGuard framework for web navigation.
AI.result: On standard web navigation benchmarks, StepGuard achieves significantly higher navigation and answer accuracy than prior methods, establishing new state-of-the-art performance according to the experiments reported in the paper.
AI.conclusion: Carefully separating navigation and answering via dual policies and using confidence-guided, selective reflection with contrastive rewards effectively mitigates reward entanglement and single-step fragility in web navigation, yielding more robust and accurate agents that set new performance standards on established benchmarks.

title: Fine-tuning LLMs for Passive Depression Severity Estimation from AI Mental Health Dialogue
summary: Depression is the leading cause of disability worldwide, and early detection of symptom change is essential for timely intervention. Validated instruments such as the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) support symptom monitoring at scale, but real-world completion rates are low, introducing response bias and systematic missingness. Passive approaches that infer severity from routinely generated data could close this gap. We address this by predicting PHQ-9 total scores directly from transcripts of conversations between users and an AI mental health application, requiring only conversation text and no additional clinical data. We fine-tune a Qwen3.5-27B backbone with a regression head, augment 3,111 ground-truth labels with pseudolabels generated by a reasoning model (Claude Opus) and iteratively trained intermediate models, for a combined dataset of 6,283 users. On a held-out test set of 842 users, our best model achieves MAE = 2.6, RMSE = 4.0, Pearson r = 0.80, and AUC = 0.91 at the PHQ-9 >= 10 clinical threshold. We also find AUC > 0.87 at every severity threshold from PHQ-9 >= 3 to PHQ-9 >= 24, demonstrating that the model captures depression severity across the full clinical spectrum. This work opens the door to passive, continuous symptom monitoring in AI mental health platforms, without requiring users to complete self-report measures.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a model that predicts PHQ-9 depression scores directly from text transcripts of user conversations with an AI mental health app, enabling passive, continuous monitoring without requiring users to fill out questionnaires.
AI.motivation: Clinical depression is widespread and a leading cause of disability. Early detection of symptom changes is critical for timely intervention, but standard tools like the PHQ-9 require users to actively complete questionnaires, which in real-world settings often have low completion rates. This leads to response bias and systematically missing data, undermining large-scale and equitable monitoring. Since many users already interact in text with AI mental health applications, there is an opportunity to infer depression severity passively from these routine conversations, reducing user burden and improving coverage.
AI.method: The authors fine-tune a large language model (Qwen3.5-27B) with an added regression head to predict continuous PHQ-9 total scores from raw conversation transcripts between users and an AI mental health app. They start with 3,111 users who have ground-truth PHQ-9 scores, then augment this dataset using pseudolabeling: a reasoning model (Claude Opus) and iteratively trained intermediate models generate additional PHQ-9 labels, expanding the training set to 6,283 users. The model is trained to minimize error on PHQ-9 score prediction and evaluated on a held-out test set of 842 users using regression metrics (MAE, RMSE, Pearson correlation) and classification metrics (AUC) at multiple PHQ-9 severity thresholds.
AI.result: On the held-out test set, the best model attains a mean absolute error of 2.6 and RMSE of 4.0 in predicting PHQ-9 scores, with a Pearson correlation of 0.80 between predicted and actual scores. As a classifier for clinically significant depression at the PHQ-9 ≥ 10 threshold, it achieves an AUC of 0.91. Across the full spectrum of severity, from PHQ-9 ≥ 3 to ≥ 24, the model maintains AUC values above 0.87 at every threshold, indicating robust discrimination across mild to severe symptom levels.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that a fine-tuned LLM can infer PHQ-9 depression severity scores with high accuracy using only conversation text from an AI mental health application, without explicit questionnaire completion or additional clinical features. This supports the feasibility of passive, continuous, and scalable symptom monitoring in digital mental health platforms, potentially reducing missing data and response bias in routine care and enabling more timely interventions. Further work is likely needed on external validation, fairness, and clinical integration, but the approach provides a strong proof of concept.

title: Structural Preservation and the Logical Expressiveness of Graph Neural Networks
summary:
Bridges between graph neural networks (GNNs) and logical formalisms have been established by fixing architectural choices, such as the types of aggregation, combination, and activation functions. These choices define restricted classes of GNNs for which tight correspondences with logical formalisms can be obtained, by showing that logical formulae can be translated into equivalent GNNs and, conversely, that GNNs can be translated into equivalent formulae.
  In this paper we take a semantic perspective by establishing the logical expressiveness of classes of GNN classifiers that are preserved under structural properties: embeddings (extensions), injective homomorphisms, and homomorphisms. We show that, for each such property, there exists a fragment of graded modal logic characterising the class of GNNs. In particular, preservation under embeddings, injective homomorphisms, and homomorphisms corresponds to existential graded modal logic, its existential-positive fragment, and existential-positive modal logic, respectively. These results characterise the expressiveness of broad classes of GNNs independently of specific architectural choices, but we also show that each of these classes admits a GNN architecture of the same expressiveness.
  Technically, our approach uses a new well-quasi-order result for trees of bounded height, yielding finite representations of unravelling-invariant classes.

AI.tldr: The paper characterizes the logical expressiveness of broad classes of GNN classifiers using fragments of graded modal logic, based on structural preservation properties like embeddings and homomorphisms.
AI.motivation: Existing links between GNNs and logic typically rely on fixing specific architectural choices (aggregation, combination, activations), which yields tight but narrow correspondences. This leaves unclear what can be expressed by more general families of GNNs that are only constrained semantically, for example by invariance under certain structural graph mappings. The authors aim to understand the expressive power of GNN classifiers in a way that is independent of particular architectures, using logical preservation theorems as a guide.
AI.method: The authors study classes of GNN classifiers defined semantically by their preservation under graph morphisms: embeddings (extensions), injective homomorphisms, and general homomorphisms. For each preservation condition, they identify a corresponding fragment of graded modal logic that captures exactly the same expressive power. They prove bidirectional characterizations (every GNN in the class corresponds to a formula in the fragment and vice versa). A key technical tool is a new well-quasi-ordering result for trees of bounded height, which enables finite representations of unravelling-invariant graph classes, and underpins the logical characterization proofs. They also construct explicit GNN architectures that match the expressiveness of each logically defined class, showing the results are not only semantic but realizable by concrete models.
AI.result: The paper establishes that: (1) GNN classifiers whose decision functions are preserved under embeddings correspond exactly to existential graded modal logic; (2) those preserved under injective homomorphisms correspond to the existential-positive fragment of graded modal logic; and (3) those preserved under general homomorphisms correspond to existential-positive (non-graded) modal logic. Furthermore, for each such semantically defined class of GNNs, the authors provide a GNN architecture with the same expressive power as the corresponding logical fragment. They also prove a new well-quasi-ordering theorem for bounded-height trees that enables finite characterizations of unravelling-invariant classes, which is instrumental in the expressiveness results.
AI.conclusion: Logical expressiveness of broad, architecture-independent classes of GNN classifiers can be precisely captured by specific fragments of graded modal logic, determined by the structural preservation properties they satisfy. This yields a semantic map between GNN behavior and modal logic, moving beyond earlier work tied to fixed architectures. The newly introduced well-quasi-ordering result for bounded-height trees provides a general tool to obtain finite descriptions of unravelling-invariant classes, and supports the logical characterizations. The work clarifies which node classification properties can be captured by GNNs satisfying common invariance conditions and shows that there exist concrete GNN architectures that achieve exactly this level of expressiveness.

title: VoidPadding: Let [VOID] Handle Padding in Masked Diffusion Language Models so that [EOS] Can Focus on Semantic Termination
summary: MDLMs generate text by denoising a preallocated masked response canvas, making response-length modeling central to instruction tuning. Existing MDLMs often inherit the autoregressive convention of using repeated \texttt{[EOS]} tokens for padding during instruction tuning, giving \texttt{[EOS]} a dual role as both a semantic terminator and a padding token. We show that this dual role is a root cause of \texttt{[EOS]} overflow under large-block decoding. To decouple these roles, we propose VoidPadding, which introduces \texttt{[VOID]} for padding and reserves \texttt{[EOS]} for termination. During inference, the learned \texttt{[EOS]} signal enables early stopping, while the learned \texttt{[VOID]} signal guides adaptive response canvas expansion. On Dream-7B-Instruct, VoidPadding improves the block-size-averaged four-task mean across mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks by \(+17.84\) points over the original model and \(+6.95\) points over RainbowPadding, while reducing decoding NFE by 55.7\% on average. Code is available at https://github.com/Haru-LCY/VoidPadding.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VoidPadding, a new padding scheme for masked denoising language models (MDLMs) that separates the roles of end-of-sequence and padding tokens to improve decoding quality and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Masked denoising language models generate responses over a preallocated canvas, so accurately handling response length and padding is critical. Existing models reuse the [EOS] token both as a semantic end-of-sequence marker and as a padding token, a convention borrowed from autoregressive models. Under large-block decoding, this dual use leads to '[EOS] overflow', where EOS tokens appear excessively or incorrectly, hurting performance and decoding behavior. The authors aim to fix this structural problem in how MDLMs are tuned and decoded.
AI.method: The authors propose VoidPadding, which introduces a separate [VOID] token that is used exclusively for padding, while reserving [EOS] only for marking semantic termination. During instruction tuning, [VOID] fills unused positions in the response canvas, and [EOS] is used solely to indicate the true end of the generated content. At inference time, the model learns two distinct signals: an [EOS] signal that allows early stopping when a complete answer has been generated, and a [VOID] signal that guides when and how to adaptively expand the response canvas in large-block decoding. They compare VoidPadding against the original padding scheme and RainbowPadding on the Dream-7B-Instruct model across several reasoning and code-generation benchmarks.
AI.result: On the Dream-7B-Instruct model, VoidPadding improves the block-size-averaged four-task mean score across mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks by +17.84 points over the original model and by +6.95 points over RainbowPadding. It also reduces the number of function evaluations (NFE) during decoding by 55.7% on average, indicating substantially higher decoding efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Separating the roles of [EOS] and padding via the proposed VoidPadding scheme eliminates [EOS] overflow in large-block decoding and leads to both higher accuracy and more efficient decoding for MDLMs. The results suggest that careful design of padding and termination tokens is crucial for instruction tuning and deployment of masked denoising language models, and VoidPadding offers a simple, effective modification that yields large performance and efficiency gains.

title: MathVis-Fine: Aligning Visual Supervision with Necessity via Progressive Dependency-Guided Training for Multimodal Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has extended from purely linguistic domains to multimodal scenarios; however, existing approaches often treat visual inputs as homogeneous or auxiliary signals, failing to capture the intricate and sample-specific dependencies between text and images in mathematical problem-solving. This gives rise to two core issues: first, the supervisory signals for visual content are generalized and coarse-grained, lacking adaptation to the actual necessity of visual information in each sample; second, training feedback becomes inaccurate when visual rewards are uniformly applied without distinguishing the complementary relationships among inputs. These limitations hinder models from achieving precise multimodal reasoning. In this work, we propose a framework for modeling fine-grained visual dependencies in mathematical reasoning. We first construct the MathVis-Fine dataset, augmenting fine-grained visual annotations with visual dependency ratings. Building upon this dataset, we introduce a two-stage progressive visual enhancement training paradigm that balances answer correctness rewards and visual grounding rewards according to the intrinsic visual dependency level of each sample, thereby mitigating reward bias and improving supervision accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the MathVis-Fine framework effectively enhances visual perception progressively based on visual dependency, offering a more precise training framework for multimodal mathematical reasoning. We will release the dataset upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MathVis-Fine, a dataset and training framework that model fine-grained visual dependencies in multimodal mathematical reasoning, enabling more accurate and visually grounded chain-of-thought solutions.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal Chain-of-Thought reasoning methods treat images as uniform or secondary inputs, using coarse, one-size-fits-all visual supervision. In math problems, however, the need for visual information varies per sample, and current methods fail to capture sample-specific text–image dependencies. This leads to two main problems: (1) visual supervision that does not match how essential the image actually is for solving a given problem, and (2) reward signals that cannot distinguish whether text and image are complementary or redundant, resulting in inaccurate training feedback and suboptimal multimodal reasoning performance.
AI.method: The authors construct MathVis-Fine, a dataset that enriches mathematical problems with fine-grained visual annotations and explicit per-sample visual dependency ratings. Using this dataset, they propose a two-stage progressive visual enhancement training paradigm. In this paradigm, the model is trained with a reward scheme that jointly considers answer correctness and visual grounding, but the relative weight of these rewards is adapted to the intrinsic visual dependency level of each sample. Thus, samples that critically depend on visual information receive stronger visual grounding supervision, while those that are mostly textual receive lighter visual rewards, reducing reward bias and mismatched supervision.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments, the MathVis-Fine framework is shown to progressively enhance the model’s visual perception as a function of the annotated visual dependency. Models trained under this framework achieve more accurate and better grounded multimodal mathematical reasoning compared to baselines that use uniform or coarse-grained visual supervision.
AI.conclusion: By explicitly modeling fine-grained visual dependencies via MathVis-Fine and a progressive, dependency-aware training pipeline, the paper provides a more precise and effective supervision scheme for multimodal mathematical reasoning. This yields improved visual perception and reasoning quality, and the forthcoming release of the MathVis-Fine dataset is intended to support further research on visually grounded Chain-of-Thought in mathematical domains.

title: When English Isn't the Best Teacher: Source Language Effects in Cross-Lingual In-Context Learning
comment: Accepted at 1st Workshop on Multilinguality in the Era of Large Language Models (MeLLM 2026), co-located with ACL 2026
summary: Cross-lingual transfer in multilingual NLP has been widely explored in supervised fine-tuning contexts, where factors like data availability and linguistic similarity largely determine transfer quality. As the field shifts toward few-shot In-Context Learning (ICL), it is often presumed that insights from fine-tuning carry over unchanged. Yet this assumption has not been rigorously evaluated, leaving open the question of how to choose source languages for cross-lingual ICL. We conduct a broad empirical study of cross-lingual transfer in ICL spanning seven tasks, six models, and a typologically diverse set of languages. We further analyze language confusion, a key obstacle for generative tasks in cross-lingual ICL. Our results show that conventional fine-tuning-based expectations do not consistently apply in the ICL regime and point to alternative heuristics for selecting source languages effectively.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how well cross-lingual transfer works in few-shot in-context learning (ICL) settings and finds that common assumptions from supervised fine-tuning do not always hold.
AI.motivation: Most prior work on cross-lingual transfer focuses on supervised fine-tuning, where factors like data size and language similarity guide source language choice. As NLP moves toward few-shot ICL with large models, many implicitly assume the same principles apply, but this has not been systematically tested. This leaves practitioners uncertain about how to select source languages for cross-lingual ICL.
AI.method: The authors conduct a large-scale empirical study of cross-lingual transfer under in-context learning across seven NLP tasks, six different models, and a typologically diverse set of languages. They also perform an analysis of language confusion phenomena—cases where models mix or misidentify languages—which is particularly problematic for generative tasks in cross-lingual ICL.
AI.result: The experiments show that patterns known from supervised fine-tuning—such as strong dependence on linguistic similarity or data availability—do not consistently predict transfer performance in the ICL setting. The analysis highlights language confusion as a major source of errors in generative cross-lingual tasks. Based on the empirical findings, they identify alternative, better-suited heuristics for choosing source languages in cross-lingual ICL.
AI.conclusion: Insights and heuristics from supervised fine-tuning cannot be blindly transferred to the ICL regime. Effective cross-lingual ICL requires reconsidering how source languages are selected, accounting for ICL-specific phenomena like language confusion and leveraging newly proposed heuristics that better match the behavior of large models under in-context prompting.

title: Learn to Quantify Social Interaction with Constraints for Pedestrian Walking
summary: Long-term human path forecasting in crowds is critical for autonomous moving platforms (like autonomous driving cars and social robots) to avoid collision and make high-quality planning. Although the current research take into account social interactions for prediction, they don't reveal the exact kinds of social interactions happened among people and how the social interactions affect the decision-making process of pedestrians, which further limits its robustness. Social interactions in pedestrian walking are intuitively massive and hard to label and quantify. In this paper, we explore creatively to quantify and interpret how pedestrians interact with others by proposing Learn to Cluster. Our clustering social interactions is probabilistic latent variable generative, learning directly from sequential trajectory observations, scalable to arbitrary number of pedestrians. Learn to cluster is label-free and can be naturally integrated into the training process of the prediction model. The latent variables will then serve as 'labels' to categorize social interactions. Extensive experiments over several trajectory prediction benchmarks demonstrate that our method is able to learn the patterns of social interactions and effectively integrate the patterns to pedestrian trajectory prediction.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a label-free, probabilistic clustering framework that learns types of social interactions from pedestrian trajectories and uses them as latent labels to improve long-term crowd path forecasting.
AI.motivation: Existing pedestrian trajectory prediction methods consider social interactions but treat them in a coarse or opaque way, without explicitly modeling what kinds of interactions occur and how they affect pedestrian decisions. Social interactions are numerous, complex, and hard to manually label, which limits the robustness and interpretability of current models. There is a need for an automatic, scalable, and interpretable way to quantify social interactions directly from data and exploit them for better prediction.
AI.method: The authors introduce "Learn to Cluster," a probabilistic latent-variable generative model that clusters social interaction patterns directly from sequential pedestrian trajectory observations. The model is label-free (unsupervised) and scalable to scenes with an arbitrary number of pedestrians. It is integrated into the training of a trajectory prediction model so that the learned latent variables act as soft labels that categorize different types of social interactions, and these inferred interaction types are used as additional structure for trajectory forecasting.
AI.result: On several standard pedestrian trajectory prediction benchmarks, the method successfully discovers meaningful patterns of social interactions from raw trajectories and incorporates these patterns into the forecasting model. Quantitatively, it improves prediction performance compared with baselines that do not explicitly learn interaction clusters, and qualitatively, it yields interpretable interaction categories.
AI.conclusion: Automatically learning clustered representations of social interactions from trajectories, via a probabilistic latent-variable generative framework, improves both the interpretability and performance of long-term pedestrian path forecasting in crowds and can be integrated seamlessly into existing prediction pipelines without manual labels.

title: Compositional Skill Routing for LLM Agents: Decompose, Retrieve, and Compose
summary: LLM agents increasingly rely on external skills -- reusable tool specifications -- but real-world tasks often require composing multiple skills, not just selecting one. We formalize this as the Compositional Skill Routing problem: given a complex user query and a large skill library, decompose the query into atomic sub-tasks, retrieve the appropriate skill for each sub-task, and compose an executable plan. We present SkillWeaver, a decompose-retrieve-compose framework combining an LLM task decomposer, a bi-encoder skill retriever with FAISS indexing, and a dependency-aware DAG planner. To support evaluation, we introduce CompSkillBench, a benchmark of 300 compositional queries over 2,209 real MCP server skills spanning 24 functional categories, sourced from the public MCP ecosystem. Our experiments reveal that task decomposition quality is the primary bottleneck: standard LLM decomposition reaches only 34.2% category recall at the step level. To address this, we propose Iterative Skill-Aware Decomposition (SAD), a retrieval-augmented feedback loop that iteratively aligns decomposition with available skills. SAD improves decomposition accuracy from 51.0% to 67.7% (+32.7%, Wilcoxon p < 10^-6) in a single iteration; DA-conditioned analysis confirms that correct granularity is the prerequisite for effective retrieval (CatR@1 rises from 34% to 41% when DA=1). SkillWeaver reduces context window consumption by over 99%, and transfer experiments confirm generalization (+35.6% relative DA gain even when target categories are absent from the retrieval pool).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SkillWeaver, a framework that decomposes complex user queries into sub-tasks, retrieves appropriate tools (skills), and plans their composition to solve real-world tasks that require multiple tools.
AI.motivation: LLM agents rely heavily on external tools, but most existing approaches focus on choosing a single tool for a task. Real-world problems, however, usually require multiple tools used in sequence or combination. There is a lack of formalization, methods, and benchmarks for this compositional skill routing scenario, and task decomposition quality appears to be a critical but underexplored bottleneck.
AI.method: The authors formalize the Compositional Skill Routing problem and propose SkillWeaver, which has three main components: (1) an LLM-based task decomposer that breaks a complex query into atomic sub-tasks, (2) a bi-encoder skill retriever with FAISS indexing that selects appropriate skills from a large skill library for each sub-task, and (3) a dependency-aware DAG planner that composes these skills into an executable plan. They also introduce CompSkillBench, a benchmark of 300 compositional queries over 2,209 real MCP server skills in 24 categories. To tackle poor decomposition, they propose Iterative Skill-Aware Decomposition (SAD), a retrieval-augmented feedback loop that refines the decomposition based on the available skills.
AI.result: Baseline LLM decomposition performs poorly, achieving only 34.2% category recall at the step level, identifying task decomposition as the main bottleneck. Using their SAD method, decomposition accuracy improves from 51.0% to 67.7%, a relative gain of 32.7% with strong statistical significance (Wilcoxon p < 10^-6). When the decomposition granularity is correct (DA=1), top-1 category recall (CatR@1) increases from 34% to 41%, showing that decomposition quality directly enables better retrieval. SkillWeaver also reduces context window consumption by more than 99%, and transfer experiments show that the approach generalizes even when target categories are missing from the retrieval pool, achieving a 35.6% relative gain in decomposition accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Compositional Skill Routing is a distinct and challenging problem for LLM agents, where the main difficulty lies in accurately decomposing tasks rather than in retrieval or planning alone. SkillWeaver provides an effective decompose-retrieve-compose framework, and the proposed Skill-Aware Decomposition substantially improves decomposition and retrieval performance. The CompSkillBench benchmark and the demonstrated generalization and efficiency gains suggest that SkillWeaver is a practical step toward scalable tool-using LLM agents capable of handling complex, multi-skill tasks.

title: DiagFlowBench: Evaluating How Language Models Handle Off-Procedure Inputs in Grounded Diagnostic Dialogue
summary: Language models increasingly serve as advisory systems in maintenance operations. To prevent hallucination, recent systems ground these models in procedural documentation to constrain them to approved steps. In practice, however, operator queries frequently stray from this path, requiring models to recognise out-of-scope inputs mid-conversation, a dynamic that current benchmarks rarely prioritise. We introduce DiagFlowBench, a dataset of 50 industrial diagnostic flowcharts from a consumer manufacturer converted into 1,676 multi-turn conversations that contrast compliant with out-of-scope utterances. Evaluating a panel of ten commercial and open-weight models reveals high variability in abstention rates, with models commonly selecting a real but contextually inadequate step rather than fabricating facts. The inherent plausibility and authority of this mapped but wrong advice exposes a challenging vulnerability for grounding systems.

AI.tldr: They build a benchmark, DiagFlowBench, from real industrial diagnostic flowcharts to test whether language models can detect and abstain from answering out-of-scope maintenance questions when grounded in procedures, revealing that models often give plausible but contextually wrong grounded steps instead of hallucinating.
AI.motivation: As language models are used as assistants in industrial maintenance, they are usually grounded in official procedural documents to reduce hallucinations. However, real operators frequently ask questions that fall outside the scope of these procedures during multi-turn interactions. Current benchmarks don’t adequately test whether models can recognize and abstain from answering such out-of-scope queries when they appear mid-conversation. The authors want to systematically study this failure mode because misleading but plausible grounded advice could be a serious safety and reliability risk.
AI.method: The authors collect 50 real diagnostic flowcharts from a consumer manufacturing setting and transform them into 1,676 synthetic but realistic multi-turn dialog scenarios between an operator and an assistant. Each conversation contrasts in-scope (compliant with the flowchart) and out-of-scope utterances, enabling test cases where the model should either follow the procedure or abstain/flag when the user goes beyond it. They then evaluate ten different language models, both commercial and open-weight, measuring how often they abstain appropriately versus incorrectly mapping the user’s request to some flowchart step.
AI.result: Across the ten tested models, there is substantial variation in how often they correctly abstain from answering out-of-scope queries. Rather than classic hallucinations with invented facts, a common failure pattern is that models choose a real step from the diagnostic flowchart that is nevertheless contextually inappropriate for the user’s actual problem. This yields answers that look authoritative and grounded but are operationally wrong.
AI.conclusion: Grounding language models in procedural documentation does not by itself prevent harmful or misleading responses in industrial maintenance. When confronted with out-of-scope queries, models often produce plausible but contextually incorrect grounded advice instead of explicitly abstaining. This behavior represents a distinct and challenging vulnerability for grounded advisory systems, underscoring the need for better mechanisms and benchmarks to detect and handle out-of-scope inputs in multi-turn settings.

title: ConSA: Controllable Sparsity in Hybrid Attention via Learnable Allocation
summary: Hybrid architectures combining full attention (FA) and sliding-window attention (SWA) are a promising paradigm for efficient LLM inference. However, existing methods typically rely on hand-crafted rules or simple post-hoc heuristics for FA/SWA allocation and offer limited analysis of the attention behaviors underlying these designs. We propose Controllable Sparsity in Hybrid Attention (ConSA), a framework that learns optimal FA/SWA assignment under a user-specified sparsity target. ConSA employs L0 regularization to learn binary masks selecting between FA and SWA for each attention unit, while an augmented Lagrangian constraint enforces the target sparsity at either layer or KV-head granularity. We evaluate ConSA on two LLMs at the 0.6B and 1.7B scales. Learned allocations consistently outperform rule-based baselines, with KV-head-wise allocation yielding clear gains over layer-wise allocation. The learned patterns place SWA in the bottom layers and concentrate FA into contiguous middle-layer blocks, diverging from evenly interleaved patterns in rule-based methods. This structure persists across model scales, sparsity levels, and allocation granularities, revealing a fine-grained spectrum of intrinsic attention behaviors that underlies the learned allocation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ConSA, a framework that automatically learns where to use full attention vs. sliding-window attention in hybrid attention architectures, under a controllable sparsity budget.
AI.motivation: Hybrid attention (mixing full and sliding-window attention) is promising for efficient LLM inference, but current FA/SWA allocation is based on fixed rules or simple heuristics, with little understanding of the resulting attention patterns. There is no principled, learnable way to decide where to place expensive full attention versus cheaper sliding-window attention for a given sparsity/efficiency target.
AI.method: ConSA formulates FA/SWA assignment as a learnable binary masking problem across attention units. It uses L0 regularization to encourage sparsity in the use of full attention and learns binary masks that choose between FA and SWA. An augmented Lagrangian constraint is added to precisely enforce a user-specified sparsity target, either at the level of whole layers or at the finer granularity of KV heads. The framework is trained to jointly optimize model performance and the sparsity objective, resulting in data-driven FA/SWA allocations.
AI.result: On 0.6B and 1.7B parameter LLMs, the learned ConSA allocations consistently outperform rule-based hybrid attention baselines at comparable sparsity levels. KV-head-wise allocation achieves better performance than coarser, layer-wise allocation. The learned configurations show systematic patterns—SWA is used mostly in lower layers, while FA is concentrated in continuous blocks in middle layers—rather than the evenly interleaved patterns of hand-crafted schemes.
AI.conclusion: ConSA provides a controllable, learning-based mechanism for assigning full vs. sliding-window attention in hybrid architectures, meeting a specified sparsity budget while improving performance over rule-based strategies. The discovered attention layouts are structured and robust across model sizes, sparsity levels, and allocation granularities, suggesting the presence of intrinsic layer- and head-specific attention roles that can be exploited for more efficient LLM design and inference.

title: PreAct: Computer-Using Agents that Get Faster on Repeated Tasks
summary:
Computer-using agents drive real software through the screen -- clicking and typing -- but they solve every task from scratch: asked to repeat a task, an agent re-reads the screen, re-reasons every tap, and pays the full cost again. We present PreAct, which lets such an agent get faster on tasks it has done before. The first time it succeeds, PreAct compiles the run into a small state-machine program-states that check the screen, transitions that act-and on later runs replays it directly instead of invoking the agent 8.5-13x faster, with no per-step language-model calls. Replay is not blind: at each step PreAct checks that the screen matches what the program expects before acting, and hands control back to the agent the moment something is off.
  PreAct applies the same discipline when deciding what to keep: a freshly compiled program enters the store only if, re-run from a clean state, an independent evaluator confirms it solved the task-catching programs that replay to their last step yet leave the task undone. Across a mobile, a desktop, and a web benchmark, this store-time check separates repeated runs that improve from ones that degrade as faulty programs accumulate, worth 1.75-2.6 tasks per benchmark, the same direction on all three; a fallback that explores afresh when no program fits brings PreAct level with a strong record-and-replay baseline. We also report what did not matter: prompt wording, runtime guardrails, and whether a language model or a plain embedding retriever selects which program to reuse.

AI.tldr: PreAct converts successful runs of computer-using agents into reusable state-machine programs that can be safely replayed, greatly speeding up repeated tasks without extra language-model calls.
AI.motivation: Current screen-based agents (that click and type through real software) must solve each task from scratch every time, re-parsing the UI and re-reasoning at every step. This is inefficient for repeated tasks and wastes computation on language-model calls, yet naively replaying prior trajectories is brittle and unsafe when the screen changes or the agent partially succeeded. The authors aim to build a system that lets such agents get faster on tasks they have already solved while maintaining correctness and robustness.
AI.method: The authors propose PreAct, a system that, after an agent successfully completes a task once, compiles that execution trace into a compact finite-state machine program. States encode expected visual conditions on the screen, and transitions encode the corresponding actions (clicks, types). On subsequent runs, instead of invoking the language model step-by-step, PreAct replays the program directly, but at each transition it checks that the current screen matches the expected state; if a mismatch is detected, control reverts back to the general agent. To avoid storing faulty or partial solutions, PreAct only adds a program to its store if an independent evaluator verifies that, when rerun from a clean initial state, the program fully solves the task. They evaluate PreAct on mobile, desktop, and web benchmarks, also comparing different retrieval strategies and prompt-level choices for selecting which program to reuse.
AI.result: On repeated tasks across mobile, desktop, and web benchmarks, PreAct achieves 8.5–13x faster execution during replay by avoiding per-step language-model calls, while maintaining safety via state checks and agent fallback. The store-time verification check prevents accumulation of bad programs: it enables repeated runs to keep improving instead of degrading performance as faulty programs pile up, yielding an additional 1.75–2.6 completed tasks per benchmark. With an exploration fallback for cases where no stored program fits, PreAct matches the performance of a strong record-and-replay baseline. Experiments also reveal that several factors—prompt wording, runtime safety guardrails, and whether a language model or a simple embedding-based retriever is used to select programs—have negligible impact on overall performance.
AI.conclusion: PreAct shows that converting successful agent runs into verified, screen-checked state-machine programs can substantially accelerate repeated tasks for computer-using agents without sacrificing correctness. The approach provides large speedups, avoids per-step language-model costs, and remains robust by detecting UI mismatches and falling back to full agent reasoning. Verifying programs at storage time is critical to long-term reliability, while many design details like prompts and retrieval sophistication matter less than expected. This suggests that principled compilation and verification of interaction traces is a promising direction for scalable, efficient tool-using agents operating over real software interfaces.

title: Security and Privacy Prompts in the Wild: What Users Ask LLMs and How LLMs Respond
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are widely used to fulfill users' information needs; users ask LLMs about the weather, pose educational questions, and consult them for legal assistance. One particularly understudied area is digital security and privacy (S&P), where users may seek LLMs' help on how to secure their online accounts or protect their computers from cyber attacks. To the best of our knowledge, no prior study has collected or analyzed the S&P questions users ask LLMs; prior research on LLM response quality relied on expert-authored S&P misconceptions or FAQs rather than user queries.
