Drawing from WildChat, a dataset of 3.2M user-LLM conversations collected in the wild, our study identifies 14,727 S&P prompts and categorizes them into nine categories covering a wide range of S&P topics. From the S&P prompts, we sampled 450 and performed a thematic analysis to characterize the S&P questions users ask LLMs. Separate from the thematic analysis, we curated 270 advice-seeking S&P prompts, where users ask for recommendations, guidance, or specific S&P information. We measured LLM response quality and consistency when posing the prompt to LLMs 10 times. We found that commercial LLMs outperform open-weight models (GPT 5.5 provided "good enough" responses on 98% of prompts; Llama 4 on 47%). However, among prompts that received high-quality responses on average, commercial models sometimes produce contradictory responses across runs, risking confusing or misleading users.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how real users ask large language models (LLMs) for digital security and privacy (S&P) help, builds a taxonomy of such queries from a large real-world chat dataset, and evaluates how well different LLMs answer and how consistent they are.
AI.motivation: People increasingly rely on LLMs for many tasks, including sensitive ones like securing online accounts or protecting against cyber attacks, but prior research has not examined what real users actually ask about security and privacy or how well LLMs respond. Existing work typically uses expert-crafted questions or misconceptions, which may not reflect authentic user needs or language. Understanding real user queries and LLM performance on them is crucial to assess safety, reliability, and potential harms from incorrect or inconsistent S&P advice.
AI.method: The authors start from WildChat, a dataset of 3.2 million real-world user–LLM conversations. They automatically identify 14,727 prompts related to digital security and privacy and classify them into nine topical categories. From these, they sample 450 prompts and conduct a qualitative thematic analysis to characterize the kinds of S&P questions users ask. Separately, they select a subset of 270 advice-seeking prompts (requests for recommendations, guidance, or specific S&P information). They query multiple LLMs with each of these 270 prompts 10 times, then evaluate response quality and cross-run consistency, comparing commercial LLMs with open-weight models.
AI.result: The study finds that security and privacy questions posed to LLMs span a wide spectrum of topics, captured in nine categories. When answering the 270 advice-seeking prompts, commercial LLMs substantially outperform open-weight models in judged response quality. For instance, the best commercial model (referred to as GPT 5.5) produced "good enough" answers for 98% of prompts, while a leading open-weight model (Llama 4) did so for only 47%. However, even for prompts where average answer quality is high, commercial models sometimes give contradictory responses across the 10 runs, revealing notable inconsistency in their advice.
AI.conclusion: Real-world users frequently seek digital security and privacy help from LLMs, and their queries are diverse and often advice-focused. Commercial LLMs generally provide higher-quality S&P advice than open-weight models, but their answers can be inconsistent across repeated queries, potentially confusing or misleading users. These findings highlight both the promise and the risks of using LLMs as security and privacy advisors and suggest a need for mechanisms to improve consistency, transparency, and safety in LLM-generated S&P guidance.

title: How Inference Compute Shapes Frontier LLM Evaluation
summary: AI evaluations are shifting toward harder tasks that benefit from longer trajectories involving tool use and iterative problem solving. As a result, performance is increasingly sensitive to the amount and allocation of compute available at test time ("inference compute"). Yet many evaluations still report performance at a single restrictive budget, meaning that low scores may reflect the evaluation setup rather than the model's underlying capability. To test this, we evaluate up to 12 frontier language models on seven challenging benchmarks spanning software engineering, mathematics, medicine, and cybersecurity. We use a controlled setup combining three simple inference-scaling interventions: larger token budgets, context compaction, and repeated submission attempts, guided either by the model itself or by minimal correctness feedback. We find three main results. First, larger token budgets substantially improve performance on benchmarks across multiple domains, including cybersecurity, FrontierMath, Humanity's Last Exam, and TerminalBench. Second, fixed-budget evaluations can increasingly understate frontier capability as models advance. Newer models reach higher performance at large budgets, where they unlock harder tasks and solve them more reliably. Third, benchmarks differ in which inference-scaling methods help most: repeated submission broadly improves performance, but the value of larger token budgets, external feedback, and parallel attempts varies by benchmark. Overall, our results show that benchmark scores are protocol-dependent. We therefore argue that evaluations should report capability as a function of inference-time compute, specify protocol choices explicitly, and compare model generations over a large shared compute range at matched budgets, especially in safety- or policy-relevant settings.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how increasing inference-time compute (e.g., more tokens, retries, and feedback) changes benchmark performance of frontier language models, showing that many current evaluations underestimate models’ true capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of frontier language models often use a single, tight inference-time compute budget. On complex, tool-using, multi-step tasks, this can make models appear weaker than they actually are, because they are not given enough room to reason, iterate, or correct mistakes. The authors want to understand how sensitive benchmark scores are to inference-time compute and protocol choices, and to provide guidance for more reliable and comparable evaluations, especially where safety and policy decisions might depend on them.
AI.method: The authors evaluate up to 12 state-of-the-art language models on seven challenging benchmarks across software engineering, mathematics, medicine, and cybersecurity. They use a controlled setup that varies three families of inference-scaling interventions: (1) larger token budgets (allowing longer reasoning trajectories), (2) context compaction (improving how context is used to fit within budgets), and (3) repeated submission attempts. The repeated attempts can be guided either by the model’s own assessment or by minimal external correctness feedback. They then measure how scores on each benchmark change as a function of these compute and protocol adjustments, comparing older and newer model generations.
AI.result: They find that: (1) Increasing token budgets yields substantial performance gains across multiple hard benchmarks such as cybersecurity tasks, FrontierMath, Humanity’s Last Exam, and TerminalBench. (2) As models get stronger, evaluations that use a single fixed, low compute budget increasingly understate their capabilities: newer models in particular achieve much better performance when allowed larger budgets, where they can tackle harder items and solve them more consistently. (3) Different benchmarks benefit from different inference-scaling strategies: repeated submission tends to help broadly, but the relative gains from more tokens, external feedback, and running multiple attempts in parallel vary significantly by task and domain.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark performance of frontier language models is highly dependent on inference-time compute and evaluation protocol. Therefore, evaluation reports should present capabilities as a function of inference-time compute, clearly specify all protocol choices, and compare models over a shared range of matched compute budgets. This is especially important in safety- and policy-relevant contexts, where under- or over-estimating model capability due to arbitrary inference constraints could misinform decisions.

title: HistoRAG: Embedding Historical Methodology in Retrieval-Augmented Generation Through Critical Technical Practice
comment: 25 pages, 6 figures. Companion preprint to a Journal of Digital History notebook article (under review)
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is the prevailing architecture for grounding language model outputs in external evidence, yet its dominant evaluation paradigms and default configurations remain oriented toward factual question-answering. For interpretive disciplines such as historical studies, RAG embeds assumptions that conflict with scholarly practice. We introduce HistoRAG, a framework that translates historiographical principles into concrete architectural interventions. Separated retrieval and generation decouples source discovery from interpretation, temporal windowing enforces balanced source representation across the research period as a methodological requirement of historical inquiry, and LLM-as-judge evaluation makes relevance judgments transparent and contestable. We evaluate these interventions using SPIEGELragged, applied to 102,189 articles from Der Spiegel (1950-1979). Each intervention addresses a measurable deficiency in standard RAG: era-specific vocabulary retrieves zero chunks from the 1950s when using 1970s terminology, evidence of the temporal skew that motivates windowing; vector similarity and LLM-assessed relevance correlate only weakly (Spearman rho = 0.275), motivating post-retrieval evaluation; and keyword-based and semantic retrieval surface largely disjoint source pools, motivating an architecture in which both operate as complementary retrieval layers under a shared LLM evaluation filter. We also introduce the concept of Zwischentexte (intermediate texts that function as interpretive proposals rather than findings) as a framework for responsible integration of LLM-generated text into scholarly practice. The architecture offers a model for how domain-specific epistemological commitments can be translated into RAG design decisions, and may transfer to other interpretive disciplines working with large corpora.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes HistoRAG, a modified RAG architecture tailored for historical scholarship that separates retrieval from interpretation, enforces temporally balanced evidence, and uses LLMs as transparent relevance judges, demonstrated on a large Der Spiegel corpus.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG systems and their evaluations are optimized for factual QA and implicitly assume that retrieval and generation serve straightforward fact-finding. In historical research, however, interpretive reasoning, temporal balance in evidence, and contestable judgments about relevance are central. Existing RAG setups can miss sources due to temporal vocabulary shifts, conflate retrieval with interpretation, and rely on opaque similarity scores that do not align with human notions of historical relevance. There is a need to embed historiographical principles directly into RAG design so that LLM-based workflows respect scholarly methods rather than distort them.
AI.method: The authors design HistoRAG, a RAG framework that encodes historiographical norms into three architectural interventions: (1) separated retrieval and generation, where source discovery is handled independently from interpretive generation; (2) temporal windowing, which constrains retrieval so that sources are sampled across defined time slices to ensure balanced coverage of a historical period; and (3) LLM-as-judge post-retrieval evaluation, where an LLM explicitly assesses the relevance of retrieved documents, making these judgments transparent and contestable. They implement and test these ideas on SPIEGELragged, a system applied to 102,189 Der Spiegel articles from 1950–1979, and analyze retrieval behavior across different methods (keyword vs semantic/vector search) and time periods.
AI.result: The experiments show clear shortcomings of default RAG in a historical setting and how each intervention mitigates them. Using 1970s vocabulary to query the 1950s yields zero retrieved chunks from that decade, demonstrating temporal vocabulary drift and motivating temporal windowing to prevent such skew. Vector similarity scores and LLM-based relevance assessments exhibit only weak correlation (Spearman rho = 0.275), indicating that similarity search alone is a poor proxy for historically meaningful relevance and justifying an LLM-as-judge layer. Furthermore, keyword and semantic retrieval return largely disjoint sets of documents, suggesting that treating them as complementary retrieval channels under a shared LLM relevance filter improves coverage of pertinent sources.
AI.conclusion: HistoRAG operationalizes historiographical principles within a RAG architecture, showing that domain-specific epistemological commitments can be concretely encoded in system design. By decoupling retrieval from interpretation, enforcing temporally balanced evidence via windowing, and subjecting retrieved material to explicit LLM relevance judgments, the framework aligns LLM-supported research more closely with historical practice. The notion of Zwischentexte conceptualizes LLM outputs as intermediate interpretive proposals rather than definitive findings, offering a responsible way to integrate such models into scholarly workflows. The authors argue that their approach can inform RAG designs for other interpretive disciplines working with large textual corpora, not just history.

title: Small Initialization Matters for Large Language Models
summary: Large language models provide a tractable system for asking how intelligence itself emerges, rather than only how LLMs can be engineered. Although progress is usually attributed to scale, data and architecture, we show that parameter initialization is a gene-like determinant of training and, in particular, of model capacity. Reducing the initialization scale consistently improves pretraining, with the largest gains on reasoning-demanding tasks. We identify two widely used empirical settings that restrain the advantage of small initialization, and show how relaxing them restores favorable scaling. We further uncover a critical initialization that balances the reasoning and training. Mechanistically, small initialization drives a distinct developmental trajectory: parameters first condense into low-complexity structures and later expand into richer representations, giving concrete form to the idea that compression is intelligence. Token-level analyses show that the gains concentrate on non-trivial, context-constrained predictions rather than all tokens uniformly. These results motivate a simple $γ$-initialization rule: expose initialization rage as an explicit knob and use small initialization by default, an almost cost-free intervention that improves pretraining and strengthens reasoning across model scales.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that the scale of parameter initialization in large language models is a key, gene-like factor governing training dynamics and reasoning capacity, and proposes a small-variance γ-initialization rule that reliably improves pretraining, especially on reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Most work attributes LLM capability gains to scaling model size, data, and architectural innovations, but the role of parameter initialization is underexplored. The authors want to understand how different initializations influence the emergence of intelligence-like behavior, provide a mechanistic account of training dynamics, and identify simple, low-cost interventions that improve reasoning performance without changing architecture or compute budgets.
AI.method: They systematically vary the scale of parameter initialization in large language models and pretrain them under otherwise comparable conditions. They evaluate performance across a range of tasks, with special focus on reasoning-intensive benchmarks. They analyze how two common empirical practices limit the advantages of small initialization and experiment with relaxing these constraints. Mechanistically, they study the developmental trajectory of learned representations—how parameter structure and token-level predictions evolve during training—to connect initialization scale to compression and representational complexity. They then codify their findings into a γ-initialization rule that makes initialization scale an explicit hyperparameter.
AI.result: Smaller initialization scales consistently improve pretraining performance, with particularly strong gains on reasoning-demanding tasks. When two standard empirical setups are relaxed, the benefits of small initialization become much more pronounced, restoring favorable scaling behavior. They identify a critical initialization regime that trades off well between reasoning performance and overall trainability. Mechanistic analysis shows that with small initialization, model parameters first converge to low-complexity, compressed structures and then gradually expand into more complex representations. Token-level analysis reveals that performance gains are concentrated on difficult, context-dependent tokens rather than being uniformly distributed across all predictions.
AI.conclusion: Parameter initialization is a major, gene-like determinant of LLM capacity and training dynamics, not just a minor implementation detail. Using smaller initialization scales yields better pretraining and stronger reasoning across scales by inducing a developmental trajectory from compressed to rich representations. The authors propose γ-initialization: explicitly exposing and defaulting to small initialization scale as a simple, nearly cost-free way to improve LLM training and reasoning performance, and they link this behavior to the idea that compression underlies intelligence.

title: Unintended Effects of Geographic Conditioning in Large Language Models
comment: To appear at the Second Workshop on Customizable NLP (CustomNLP4U) at ACL 2026
summary: Modern conversational AI systems frequently rely on user metadata to localize responses, yet the unintended regional biases introduced by this hidden context remain poorly understood. In this work, we evaluate location leakage: the phenomenon where a model generates geographic references despite receiving a geographically neutral user prompt. Across both creative writing and open-ended Q&A prompts, even state-of-the-art LLMs systematically favor region-specific outputs when exposed to location metadata, with leakage spiking by up to 793 times above baseline (e.g., from 0.04% to 31.7% for Llama 3.1-8B, and 21.3% and 8.8% for Qwen3-8B and Claude Sonnet 4.6, respectively). Our analysis further shows a novel structural conditioning effect: replacing the injected location with the placeholder "Unknown" still elevates leakage by up to 72 times above baseline, demonstrating that the user profile frame itself, independent of any geographic content, acts as a generative conditioning signal.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how conversational AI models unintentionally reveal or rely on user location metadata, causing region-specific responses even when prompts are geographically neutral.
AI.motivation: Modern LLM-based conversational systems often receive hidden user metadata (like location) to localize responses, but this can introduce unintended regional bias or privacy leakage. The extent, mechanisms, and risks of such location-driven behavior have not been systematically measured.
AI.method: The authors run controlled experiments with several state-of-the-art LLMs on creative writing and open-ended Q&A tasks. They compare outputs under geographically neutral prompts with and without injected location metadata in the hidden user profile. They measure how often models produce geographic references (location leakage), and they test a structural conditioning effect by replacing the location with the placeholder "Unknown" while keeping the user profile frame, to see if the profile structure alone conditions generation.
AI.result: They find that all tested LLMs systematically produce more region-specific content when exposed to location metadata, with leakage rates increasing dramatically—up to 793x over baseline (e.g., 0.04% to 31.7% for Llama 3.1-8B, 21.3% for Qwen3-8B, and 8.8% for Claude Sonnet 4.6). Moreover, even when explicit location content is replaced by the placeholder "Unknown," leakage still rises up to 72x compared to the no-profile baseline, indicating that the presence of a profile structure itself conditions the model’s behavior.
AI.conclusion: Location metadata and even the structural presence of a user profile can strongly bias LLM outputs toward region-specific content, causing hidden location leakage from seemingly neutral prompts. This reveals a previously underappreciated privacy and fairness risk and suggests that both explicit metadata and profile framing must be carefully managed when deploying conversational AI systems.

title: MoCo-AIS: A Contrastive Learning Framework for Similarity Computation of Vessel Trajectories
summary: Trajectory similarity is a fundamental task in analyzing mobility patterns, essential for applications such as route pattern extraction, mobility prediction, and anomaly detection. Traditional distance-based measures for computing similarity incur high computational cost, driving the adoption of lightweight learning-based approaches. Supervised methods rely on extensive labels derived from traditional distance measures and often reproduce these metrics, which limits generalization. While self-supervised learning addresses this issue through contrastive learning, it lacks a unified framework, making it difficult to compare deep learning (DL) models for consistent trajectory representation. Accordingly, this paper presents MoCo-AIS, a unified framework for learning vessel trajectory embeddings based on the Momentum Contrast (MoCo) paradigm, which formulates similarity learning through positive and negative trajectory pairs. Within this framework, we evaluate a diverse set of leading DL models on large-scale, real-world vessel-tracking AIS datasets that capture diverse navigation behaviors and operating conditions. Results demonstrate that our framework significantly improves similarity learning over existing baselines, while providing a benchmarking platform for evaluating trajectory representation models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MoCo-AIS, a unified momentum-contrast-based self-supervised framework for learning vessel trajectory embeddings, enabling efficient and generalizable trajectory similarity measurement and benchmarking of deep models on large-scale AIS data.
AI.motivation: Trajectory similarity is crucial for tasks like route pattern mining, prediction, and anomaly detection, but traditional distance-based measures are computationally expensive. Supervised deep models need large label sets derived from these same costly metrics, so they tend to just mimic them and generalize poorly. Existing self-supervised/contrastive approaches alleviate label dependence but lack a unified, comparable framework, complicating fair evaluation of trajectory representation models, especially in the maritime AIS domain.
AI.method: The authors build MoCo-AIS, adapting the Momentum Contrast (MoCo) self-supervised learning paradigm to vessel trajectory data. They construct positive and negative pairs of trajectories and train an encoder (various DL backbones) to produce embeddings where positives are close and negatives are far in feature space. The framework is model-agnostic and supports multiple leading deep learning architectures. They train and evaluate these within the same pipeline on large-scale real-world AIS vessel-tracking datasets covering diverse navigation behaviors and conditions.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments on large AIS datasets, MoCo-AIS yields significantly better trajectory similarity learning compared to existing baselines, as measured by downstream tasks and similarity-related metrics. The unified framework also reveals how different DL architectures perform under identical training and evaluation conditions, showing clear gains in representation quality and robustness.
AI.conclusion: MoCo-AIS successfully provides both (1) a more effective self-supervised mechanism for learning vessel trajectory embeddings that improve similarity estimation and related downstream applications, and (2) a standardized benchmarking platform to fairly compare trajectory representation models. This addresses limitations of label-heavy supervised methods and fragmented self-supervised approaches, supporting more scalable and generalizable trajectory analytics in maritime domains.

title: Learning from the Self-future: On-policy Self-distillation for dLLMs
summary: On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) has proven effective for post-training large language models (LLMs), yet its application to diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) remains unexplored. Existing OPSD methods are inherently autoregressive-centric. They inject privileged information via left-to-right prefix conditioning with token-level divergence supervision, a design that fundamentally conflicts with the arbitraryorder generation of dLLMs. We introduce d-OPSD, the first OPSD framework tailored for dLLMs. Our approach makes two core contributions. First, we reframe self-teacher construction by using self-generated answers as suffix conditioning, enabling the student model to learn from "self future-experience" rather than privileged prefixes. Second, we shift supervision from token-level to step-level, aligning training with the iterative denoising process of dLLMs. Experiments across four reasoning benchmarks show that d-OPSD consistently outperforms RLVR and SFT baselines with superior sample efficiency, requiring only around 10% of the optimization steps by RLVR and opening a promising pathway for dLLM posttraining. The code is available at https://github.com/xingzhejun/d-OPSD.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes d-OPSD, the first on-policy self-distillation framework designed specifically for diffusion large language models (dLLMs), improving post-training efficiency and performance on reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: On-policy self-distillation is effective for post-training autoregressive LLMs but has not been adapted to diffusion LLMs, whose arbitrary-order generation and iterative denoising conflict with existing autoregressive-centric OPSD designs. There is a need for a post-training method that respects the unique generation process of dLLMs while achieving strong reasoning performance with high sample efficiency.
AI.method: The authors design d-OPSD, an OPSD framework tailored to dLLMs, with two main innovations: (1) self-teacher construction via using the model’s own generated answers as suffix conditioning, so the student learns from its “future experience” instead of privileged left-to-right prefixes; and (2) a shift from token-level to step-level supervision that directly matches the iterative denoising steps of diffusion generation, bringing the learning signal in line with the diffusion process.
AI.result: Across four reasoning benchmarks, d-OPSD consistently outperforms RLVR and standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) baselines, achieving better performance while using only about 10% of the optimization steps required by RLVR, demonstrating substantially improved sample and optimization efficiency for dLLM post-training.
AI.conclusion: d-OPSD successfully adapts on-policy self-distillation to diffusion LLMs by aligning both conditioning and supervision with the diffusion generation process. This yields superior reasoning capabilities and sample efficiency compared to existing post-training methods, indicating that OPSD can be an effective and practical pathway for improving dLLMs and motivating further work on diffusion-aware training algorithms.

title: STAR: SpatioTemporal Adaptive Reward Allocation for Text-to-Image RL Post-Training
summary: Existing RL post-training methods for text-to-image generation usually convert the final-image reward into a single scalar advantage and apply it with the same strength to the entire generative trajectory. However, text-to-image generation naturally has temporal and spatial structure: different denoising steps are responsible for different generation stages, and the content that truly determines text alignment often appears only in part of the image. This granularity mismatch makes it difficult for policy updates to focus on the generative components that actually affect the reward. To address this issue, we propose \textbf{SpatioTemporal Adaptive Reward (STAR) Allocation} for RL post-training of text-to-image diffusion and flow models. STAR uses text-image attention inside the generative model and starts from the core content that the user truly cares about in the prompt. It constructs spatial allocation maps that dynamically vary across denoising steps and rollouts, and allocates the same group-relative advantage to more relevant latent regions with almost no additional computational overhead. STAR then applies stronger policy updates to these regions through a spatially resolved policy objective. We use Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium as the base model and evaluate on three tasks: GenEval, OCR text rendering, and PickScore. Experimental results show that STAR improves compositional semantic alignment, text rendering, and preference optimization without changing the external reward source, achieving $\mathbf{0.9759}$, $\mathbf{0.9757}$, and $\mathbf{23.60}$ on GenEval, OCR, and PickScore, respectively.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes STAR, a SpatioTemporal Adaptive Reward allocation method for RL post-training of text-to-image diffusion/flow models, which allocates rewards to spatial regions and denoising steps based on text-image attention, improving text alignment and user preference metrics without changing external rewards.
AI.motivation: Current RL post-training for text-to-image models applies a single scalar reward uniformly across the whole generative trajectory, ignoring the temporal (different denoising stages) and spatial (different image regions) structure of generation. This mismatch prevents the policy from focusing updates on the parts of the generation that actually determine text alignment or user satisfaction. The authors aim to exploit the inherent spatiotemporal structure of diffusion/flow models to better utilize existing reward signals and more effectively improve compositional alignment, text rendering, and preference satisfaction.
AI.method: The authors introduce SpatioTemporal Adaptive Reward (STAR) Allocation for RL post-training. STAR leverages internal text-image attention maps from the generative model to identify core content in the prompt that users care most about. For each denoising step and trajectory, it builds spatial reward allocation maps over the latent space that highlight regions most relevant to the conditioned text. Instead of a uniform scalar advantage, it assigns higher group-relative advantage to these key regions, with negligible additional compute. A spatially resolved policy objective then applies proportionally stronger policy gradients to the emphasized regions throughout the trajectory, integrating seamlessly with diffusion and flow RL fine-tuning pipelines.
AI.result: Using Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium as the base model, STAR is evaluated on GenEval (compositional semantic alignment), OCR text rendering, and PickScore (preference-based evaluation). The method attains scores of 0.9759 on GenEval, 0.9757 on OCR, and 23.60 on PickScore. These results indicate consistent improvements in compositional alignment, quality of rendered text, and overall preference optimization compared to baselines that use the same external reward but no spatiotemporal reward allocation.
AI.conclusion: SpatioTemporal Adaptive Reward (STAR) effectively resolves the mismatch between coarse scalar rewards and the fine-grained spatiotemporal structure of text-to-image generation. By reallocating the same external rewards across space and time using model-internal attention, STAR yields stronger and more targeted policy updates, leading to better compositional semantic alignment, improved text rendering, and higher preference scores, all with minimal computational overhead and without modifying the reward source itself.

title: RubricsTree: Scalable and Evolving Open-Ended Evaluation of Personal Health Agents across Health Memory and Medical Skills
summary: The LLM-empowered personal health agents with user health (sensor) metrics have offered a promising pathway to alleviate global disparities in healthcare access. However, large-scale clinical deployment remains constrained by an open-ended evaluation bottleneck: physician annotation is reliable but costly and unscalable, while LLM-as-a-judge evaluators are scalable but subjective, inconsistent, and sometimes clinically misaligned. We introduce RubricsTree, a scalable evaluation framework with an expert-aligned hierarchical taxonomy of over 100 atomic, clinically-verifiable Boolean rubrics, evolving from the insights of 4,000 real user queries through an iterative human-in-the-loop curation protocol with an expertise panel led by an experienced physician. A context-aware adaptive router activates only the relevant auto-weighted rubric subset per query, providing the throughput needed for scalable evaluation with expert-aligned quality. Through a systematic meta-evaluation, we show that RubricsTree (i) substantially exceeds a strong large-scale evaluation baseline in expert alignment on challenging open-ended queries; (ii) reliably penalizes contextually degraded responses; and (iii) when used as structured instructions, text feedback, or training rewards for performance optimization, yields up to ~66% relative gains on HealthBench for Gemini, GPT, and Qwen model families. RubricsTree thus provides a scalable, auditable, and evolving evaluation infrastructure required for the continuous optimization of product-level personal healthcare AI.

AI.tldr: RubricsTree is a scalable, rubric-based evaluation framework for LLM health agents that aligns closely with clinical experts and improves model performance when used for feedback and training.
AI.motivation: LLM-based personal health agents could expand healthcare access, but current evaluation methods are problematic: physician review is accurate but expensive and unscalable; LLM-as-a-judge approaches are scalable but can be subjective, inconsistent, and clinically unsafe. There is a need for an evaluation framework that is scalable, objective, clinically grounded, and suitable for continuous optimization of real-world health agents.
AI.method: The authors design RubricsTree, a hierarchical taxonomy of 100+ atomic, clinically-verifiable Boolean rubrics that decompose evaluation into fine-grained checks. These rubrics are derived and refined from ~4,000 real user health queries using an iterative human-in-the-loop process led by an expert physician panel. A context-aware adaptive routing mechanism selects and auto-weights only the relevant subset of rubrics for each query, making evaluation efficient and tailored. They then perform systematic meta-evaluation comparing RubricsTree’s judgments against expert physicians and baseline large-scale evaluation frameworks, and they use RubricsTree as a source of structured instructions, textual feedback, and training rewards to optimize different LLM families on HealthBench.
AI.result: RubricsTree achieves markedly higher alignment with expert physician judgments than a strong baseline on difficult, open-ended health queries. It consistently penalizes answers whose quality degrades when context is perturbed, indicating sensitivity to clinically meaningful errors. When used to guide optimization of several LLM families (Gemini, GPT, Qwen) via rubrics-as-instructions, feedback, or reward signals, models obtain up to ~66% relative performance improvement on the HealthBench benchmark.
AI.conclusion: RubricsTree offers a scalable, auditable, and clinically grounded evaluation infrastructure for LLM-based personal health agents. By breaking evaluation into expert-designed Boolean rubrics and adaptively routing per query, it resolves some of the subjectivity and scalability issues of previous evaluation approaches and can directly drive significant performance gains. This framework is positioned as a foundation for continuous, expert-aligned improvement of product-level healthcare AI systems.

title: LLM Consumer Behavior Theory: Foundations of a Novel Research Field
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents that make consumption decisions on behalf of users. This shift raises fundamental questions for consumer theory, which has traditionally modeled humans as the primary decision-makers. In this paper, we introduce LLM Consumer Behavior Theory, a new field of study concerned with analyzing consumer behavior in agentic markets. Drawing on classical and behavioral economics alongside recent advances in Natural Language Processing, we formalize how human preferences are reflected and acted upon by LLM-based agents, and how agent-level decisions aggregate into market demand. We unify previously fragmented literature on LLM decision-making, human behavior simulation, and preference elicitation under a common economic lens, highlighting where assumptions, such as rationality and heterogeneity, may fail in agentic markets. Rather than providing empirical validation, this paper outlines the scope of LLM consumer behavior and identifies open research questions related to alignment, preference representation, and market dynamics.

AI.tldr: Introduces a conceptual framework (LLM Consumer Behavior Theory) for understanding how large language model agents make consumption decisions for humans and how this affects market demand.
AI.motivation: As LLMs start acting as autonomous purchasing/decision agents for users, traditional consumer theory—which assumes human decision-makers—no longer fully applies. There is a need for an updated theory to understand how LLM agents interpret human preferences, make choices, and influence markets.
AI.method: Conceptual/theoretical work: the authors synthesize classical and behavioral consumer theory with recent NLP research on LLM decision-making, human behavior simulation, and preference elicitation. They formalize how human preferences get encoded and executed by LLM agents and how these agents’ decisions aggregate into demand, while examining standard economic assumptions (rationality, heterogeneity) in this new context.
AI.result: A unified theoretical framework—LLM Consumer Behavior Theory—that organizes scattered findings about LLM-based decision-making under an economic lens, clarifies where standard assumptions may break down in agentic markets, and structures the space of issues around LLM-mediated consumption.
AI.conclusion: The paper defines the scope of a new research area focused on consumer behavior in markets populated by LLM agents, and it identifies open questions about aligning LLM agents with human preferences, accurately representing those preferences, and understanding the resulting market dynamics, without yet providing empirical tests.

title: Analyzing and Encoding the Al-Mawrid Arabic-English Dictionary with the ISO Language Markup Framework and TEI Lex-0
comment: 44 pages, 58 figures, 12 tables. Submitted to Language Resources and Evaluation, under review since Aug 2025, round 3
summary: This paper presents a robust methodology for the systematic digitization and encoding of the Al-Mawrid Arabic-English dictionary, transforming it from a legacy print resource into a standardized computational lexicon. Addressing a significant gap in Arabic lexical infrastructure, the study adopts a dual-standard framing that aligns the ISO Lexical Markup Framework (LMF) with the Text Encoding Initiative TEI Lex-0 guidelines. By applying an editorial view to the dictionary's macro- and microstructure, the research resolves the structural ambiguities and punctuation inconsistencies typical of 20th-century bilingual dictionaries. The methodology is grounded in an empirical analysis of the dictionary's lexical knowledge density. Drawing on a representative sample (the letter Ayn, comprising 4.6% of the total volume), the study provides scientific weight to the encoding process, demonstrating a structural parsing accuracy of 91%. Quantitative evaluation of the information extraction rules reveals high performance, with 85% precision and 98% recall for synonyms, and 88% precision for other morpho-semantic features. Beyond technical description, the paper provides a critical comparison with existing Arabic lexical resources and discusses the limitations of TEI Lex-0 when modelling specific Arabic phenomena, such as implicit "open set" semantic relations and scattered morphological cues. Furthermore, the study explores the potential for Linguistic Linked Open Data (LLOD) integration by establishing a scalable prefix-based referencing system that facilitates the resource's inclusion in the semantic web. The result is an interoperable, machine-tractable resource that provides a reproducible workflow for the retro-digitization of complex legacy bilingual lexicons within the Arabic NLP and Digital Humanities communities.

AI.tldr: A methodology to convert the Al-Mawrid Arabic-English print dictionary into a standardized, machine-readable lexicon using LMF and TEI Lex-0, achieving high parsing and extraction accuracy and enabling integration into the semantic web.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of robust, standardized Arabic lexical resources suitable for NLP and digital humanities, and legacy print dictionaries like Al-Mawrid are structurally inconsistent and hard to exploit computationally. The authors want to systematically retro-digitize such resources in a way that is accurate, interoperable, and reusable.
AI.method: They treat Al-Mawrid from an editorial/structural perspective, analyzing its macro- and microstructure and its lexical knowledge density. Using a representative sample (entries under the letter Ayn), they design and apply encoding and information-extraction rules mapped to a dual standard: ISO LMF and TEI Lex-0. They then quantify the structural parsing accuracy and the precision/recall of extracted lexical features (e.g., synonyms and morpho-semantic information). They also design a prefix-based referencing system to support LLOD/semantic web integration and critically examine how well TEI Lex-0 can model specific Arabic linguistic phenomena.
AI.result: The approach yields a structurally parsed and encoded version of the Al-Mawrid dictionary with 91% structural parsing accuracy. Information extraction performs strongly: 85% precision and 98% recall for synonyms, and 88% precision for other morpho-semantic features. The resulting resource is interoperable, aligned with LMF and TEI Lex-0, and equipped with a scalable referencing system suitable for LLOD integration. The authors also identify concrete limitations of TEI Lex-0 for Arabic-specific features like implicit open-set relations and dispersed morphological information.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that legacy Arabic bilingual dictionaries can be reliably converted into standardized, computational lexicons through a reproducible workflow grounded in editorial analysis and dual-standard encoding. This produces a machine-tractable, interoperable lexical resource for Arabic NLP and digital humanities, exposes gaps in current TEI Lex-0 modelling for Arabic, and shows a clear path toward integrating such resources into the Linguistic Linked Open Data ecosystem.

title: LegalHalluLens: Typed Hallucination Auditing and Calibrated Multi-Agent Debate for Trustworthy Legal AI
comment: 15 pages, 5 figures; Published at the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond (AIWILD) at ICML 2026
summary: AI systems deployed in legal workflows hallucinate at rates that aggregate metrics report at ~52%, but this average conceals where errors concentrate and in which direction they run, leaving compliance officers without an actionable signal for trustworthy deployment. We present LegalHalluLens, an auditing framework with three components: typed hallucination profiles across four legally-motivated claim categories (numeric, temporal, obligation/entitlement, factual) over CUAD (Hendrycks et al., 2021); a Risk Direction Index (RDI) that reduces omission-versus-invention bias to a single deployment-comparable scalar; and a typed debate pipeline calibrated to both magnitudes and directions. Across 510 contracts and 249,252 clause-level instances we measure a within-model gap of approximately 38-40 pp between obligation/numeric and temporal claims that aggregate reporting hides, and show that two systems with matched 52% rates can carry opposite RDIs. The debate pipeline reduces fabricated detections by 45% with per-category gains tracking the diagnosis, matching commercial APIs with a substantially smaller backbone (4B active parameters). Typed profiles and RDI surface failure modes that aggregate metrics hide; we further show these diagnostics serve as calibration inputs for multi-agent debate pipelines, where Skeptic challenges and asymmetric gates targeted at measured failure modes outperform generically-tuned debate. The framework supports direction-aware procurement, accountability, and agent design for legal AI deployed in the wild.

AI.tldr: They introduce LegalHalluLens, a framework to analyze and reduce hallucinations of AI systems in legal workflows, focusing on where and how errors occur rather than just aggregate rates.
AI.motivation: Aggregate hallucination metrics (e.g., a 52% hallucination rate) hide where errors are concentrated across different types of legal claims and whether models tend to omit or fabricate information, leaving compliance officers without actionable insight for safe deployment.
AI.method: They build an auditing framework with: (1) typed hallucination profiles over four legal claim categories (numeric, temporal, obligation/entitlement, factual) using the CUAD contract dataset; (2) a Risk Direction Index (RDI) that summarizes whether a system tends toward omissions or inventions; and (3) a typed, multi-agent debate pipeline calibrated to error magnitudes and directions, with specialized skeptic agents and asymmetric gating targeted at measured failure modes.
AI.result: Analyzing 510 contracts and ~249k clause-level instances, they find a 38–40 percentage point within-model gap between obligation/numeric and temporal claim performance that aggregate metrics obscure. Two models with the same overall 52% hallucination rate can have opposite RDIs, implying very different risk profiles. Their typed debate pipeline cuts fabricated detections by 45%, with improvements aligned to the diagnosed failure modes, and achieves performance comparable to commercial APIs despite using a smaller 4B-parameter backbone.
AI.conclusion: Typed hallucination profiles and the Risk Direction Index reveal critical, hidden failure modes of legal AI systems and provide actionable diagnostics. These diagnostics can then be used to calibrate multi-agent debate pipelines, where targeted skeptic agents and gates outperform generic debate setups, enabling more trustworthy, direction-aware deployment, procurement, and accountability for legal AI in real-world settings.

title: Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization: Teacher in Prompts, Not Gradients
summary: Knowledge distillation transfers a teacher's competence to a small student but is brittle in the small-student regime: forcing the student to imitate logits from a much larger teacher concentrates it on the teacher's sharpest modes, hurting generalization on benchmark families beyond the training corpus. Reinforcement learning (RL) avoids logit imitation by training on the student's own rollouts. However, on questions where every rollout fails-yielding zero advantage and being silently discarded-injecting a stronger teacher's response into the policy gradient breaks the on-policy assumption and induces drift. We introduce Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization (ZPPO), inspired by Vygotsky's zone of proximal development, which keeps the teacher inside the prompt rather than the policy gradient. On hard questions, ZPPO constructs two reformulated prompts: a Binary Candidate-included Question (BCQ) pairs one correct teacher response with one incorrect student response as anonymized candidates the student must discriminate, and a Negative Candidate-included Question (NCQ) aggregates the student's wrong rollouts into a single prompt to surface their shared failure modes. A prompt replay buffer recirculates each hard question until it either graduates-the student's mean rollout accuracy on it reaches half- or is FIFO-evicted under finite capacity, amplifying BCQ and NCQ inside the student's current zone of proximal development. On the Qwen3.5 family at four student scales (0.8B-9B) with a 27B teacher, post-trained as vision-language models and evaluated on a 31-benchmark suite (16 VLM, 10 LLM, 5 Video), ZPPO outperforms off/on-policy distillation and GRPO, with the largest gains at the smallest scale.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ZPPO, a new reinforcement-learning-based distillation method that uses teacher guidance only in prompts, not in gradients, to better train small vision-language models.
AI.motivation: Standard knowledge distillation makes small students overfit to a large teacher’s sharp logit modes, harming generalization, especially for small models. RL-based approaches avoid this but fail on very hard questions where all student rollouts are wrong, and naively injecting teacher answers into the gradient breaks on-policy RL and causes policy drift. The authors seek a way to leverage a strong teacher to help students, particularly small ones, without these failures and with better generalization across many benchmarks.
AI.method: They introduce Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization (ZPPO). Instead of putting teacher outputs into the policy gradient, ZPPO keeps teacher guidance within prompts. For hard questions on which the student currently fails, ZPPO creates: (1) Binary Candidate-included Questions (BCQ), where one correct teacher answer and one incorrect student answer are anonymized as two candidates the student must choose between; and (2) Negative Candidate-included Questions (NCQ), which bundle several incorrect student rollouts to highlight common failure modes. A prompt replay buffer stores such hard questions and their BCQ/NCQ forms, and replays them until the student’s average accuracy on that question reaches at least 50%, or the question is dropped via FIFO when capacity is exceeded. This focuses training on items within the student’s “zone of proximal development.” The optimization backbone is PPO-like RL, but with these specialized prompts and replay.
AI.result: Using the Qwen3.5 model family as students (0.8B–9B parameters) and a 27B-parameter teacher, all post-trained as vision-language models, the authors evaluate on 31 benchmarks (16 VLM, 10 pure language, 5 video). Across all four student scales, ZPPO consistently outperforms both off-policy and on-policy distillation baselines as well as GRPO, with the biggest improvements observed for the smallest student models.
AI.conclusion: Placing teacher guidance in structured prompts instead of gradients, and focusing training on questions within the student’s current capability range via BCQ/NCQ and a replay buffer, yields more effective and stable distillation—particularly for small vision-language models. ZPPO mitigates overfitting to teacher logits and on-policy violations while improving generalization across diverse VLM, LLM, and video benchmarks.

title: ProvenanceGuard: Source-Aware Factuality Verification for MCP-Based LLM Agents
summary:
Tool-using LLM agents increasingly use the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to answer from heterogeneous evidence sources, including search, APIs, databases, clinical records, and formulary tools. Standard factuality metrics usually test whether an answer is supported by pooled evidence, missing a provenance-sensitive failure mode: a claim may be supported somewhere while being attributed to the wrong source. We call this cross-source conflation.
  We introduce ProvenanceGuard, a source-aware verifier for MCP-grounded answers. It consumes captured MCP traces with stable tool IDs, source IDs, and raw outputs; decomposes answers into atomic claims; routes claims to source-specific evidence; checks support with NLI and a token-alignment proxy; compares stated attribution with the routed source; and returns per-claim verdicts plus an answer-level allow/block decision. Blocked answers can be repaired with retrieval-augmented answer revision and re-verified.
  We evaluate on 281 medical-domain MCP-agent traces. A 266-trace adjudicated subset yields 2,325 LLM-assisted claim labels split by trace; 361 held-out labels are human-verified. On the 40-trace held-out split, ProvenanceGuard achieves block F1 0.802 and source accuracy 0.858 over 260 source-eligible claims, outperforming source-blind baselines that do not emit claim-to-source IDs. On a harder multi-source benchmark it reaches block F1 0.846, while source-plus-relation accuracy drops to 0.229, showing that exact source ownership remains difficult with semantically close sources. Repair-and-reverify resolves all blocked answers in the full trace set, often via conservative fallback. In 50 controlled clinical conflation probes, ProvenanceGuard detects all injected attribution swaps with no retained wrong attribution. These results show that source attribution is an independent axis for factuality verification in MCP-based agents.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ProvenanceGuard, a verifier that checks not only whether tool-using LLM agents’ answers are supported by evidence, but also whether each claim is correctly attributed to the specific source that supports it, addressing cross-source conflation in MCP-based agents.
AI.motivation: Tool-using LLM agents now aggregate evidence from many heterogeneous sources via the Model Context Protocol (MCP). Existing factuality metrics and verifiers usually pool all retrieved evidence and only check if an answer is supported somewhere in that pool. This misses a critical failure mode where the model cites or relies on the wrong source (cross-source conflation), which is especially problematic in domains like medicine where source provenance (e.g., a specific guideline, formulary, or patient record) is crucial. There is a need for an automated, source-aware verification system that can detect and prevent such conflations in MCP-grounded answers.
AI.method: The authors design ProvenanceGuard, a source-aware verifier that operates on recorded MCP traces containing stable IDs for tools and sources plus their raw outputs. The system (1) decomposes an LLM agent’s answer into atomic claims, (2) routes each claim to the relevant source-specific evidence using the MCP trace, (3) checks whether each claim is supported by its routed source using a combination of natural language inference (NLI) and a token-alignment based proxy, (4) compares the answer’s stated attribution to the actual supporting source in the trace, and (5) outputs per-claim decisions (including source correctness) and an overall allow/block decision for the answer. If an answer is blocked, a retrieval-augmented repair step rewrites the answer to fix attribution and content issues, after which it is re-verified.
AI.result: On 281 medical-domain MCP-agent traces, with a 266-trace adjudicated subset yielding 2,325 labeled claims and 361 human-verified labels, ProvenanceGuard achieves strong performance. On a 40-trace held-out split and 260 source-eligible claims, it gets a block F1 of 0.802 and a source accuracy of 0.858, outperforming source-blind baselines that do not output explicit claim-to-source mappings. On a more challenging multi-source benchmark, it attains block F1 of 0.846 but much lower source-plus-relation accuracy of 0.229, highlighting the difficulty of exact source ownership when sources are semantically similar. The repair-and-reverify mechanism successfully fixes all blocked answers across the full trace set, often by reverting to more conservative responses. In 50 controlled clinical probes that inject deliberate attribution swaps, ProvenanceGuard detects all such cross-source conflations with no remaining incorrect attributions.
AI.conclusion: ProvenanceGuard demonstrates that source attribution can and should be treated as a separate dimension of factuality in tool-using, MCP-based LLM agents. By verifying both claim correctness and correct source ownership, the system detects cross-source conflation that traditional pooled-evidence checkers miss, and can drive automated repair of problematic answers. While resolving fine-grained source ownership among closely related sources remains challenging, the results indicate that provenance-aware verification substantially improves the reliability and safety of multi-tool, multi-source LLM agents, particularly in sensitive domains like medicine.

title: Darshana Graph: A Parallel Commentary Corpus for Comparative Indian Philosophy, with Stylometric and Exploratory Graph Analyses
comment: 12 pages, 1 figure. Open Source Code available at https://github.com/joyboseroy/darshana-graph and dataset at https://huggingface.co/datasets/joyboseroy/darshana-graph
summary: We introduce Darshana Graph, a corpus of over 125,000 text records spanning classical Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain philosophical traditions, drawn from public-domain and openly licensed translations of sources including the Bhagavad Gita, Brahma Sutras, principal Upanishads, the Pali Canon, and core Jain texts. Its distinctive contribution lies in a structurally unique subset of roughly 8,500 Hindu and Jain records in which the same root verse or sutra is aligned across eighteen historical commentators representing five schools of Vedanta and other darshanas, enabling direct comparison of how independent interpretive traditions read identical source material. To our knowledge, no publicly available resource provides comparable cross-commentator alignment at this scale. We present two analyses built on this corpus. First, a transparent stylometric comparison requiring no machine learning measures argumentative style through scriptural citation density, explicit refutation rate, and sentence complexity. It finds a moderate negative correlation between citation density and refutation rate, a marked increase in refutation rate across three commentators in a related doctrinal lineage, and measurable genre-level differences within the Pali Canon itself. Second, we describe a constrained large language model pipeline that extracts typed philosophical relationships between concepts using a predefined relation vocabulary and deterministic post-hoc validation. The resulting graph surfaces cross-school disagreement patterns while also revealing important extraction limitations, including cases where an independent embedding-based analysis disagrees with the graph-derived findings. We release the full corpus, extracted relationship graph, and all source code.

AI.tldr: Darshana Graph is a large corpus and relationship graph built from classical Indian philosophical texts and aligned commentaries, used to study stylistic and conceptual patterns across traditions.
AI.motivation: To provide an open, large-scale, structurally aligned corpus of classical Indian philosophical texts that enables quantitative comparison of interpretive traditions and supports automated extraction of philosophical relationships, filling the gap of lacking cross-commentator, cross-school resources.
AI.method: They construct a corpus of 125k+ text records from public-domain translations of Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain sources, with a special aligned subset of 8,500 records where the same root verse/sutra is linked across 18 commentators from multiple Vedanta and other schools. They then (1) run a transparent, non-ML stylometric analysis measuring citation density, refutation rate, and sentence complexity; and (2) build a constrained LLM-based pipeline with a predefined relation schema plus deterministic validation to extract typed conceptual relations and build a relationship graph, comparing it with embedding-based analysis.
AI.result: They obtain a cross-commentator aligned corpus and an extracted relation graph. Stylometric analysis reveals a moderate negative correlation between citation density and refutation rate, a clear increase in refutation rate in a specific doctrinal lineage, and genre-level stylistic differences within the Pali Canon. The LLM pipeline surfaces cross-school disagreement patterns but also exposes extraction limitations where its results diverge from an independent embedding-based analysis.
AI.conclusion: Darshana Graph offers a novel, large-scale, publicly released resource for studying classical Indian philosophy computationally. It enables both stylometric and conceptual analyses across schools and commentators, but also highlights current limitations of LLM-based relationship extraction, encouraging cautious use and further methodological refinement.

title: PseudoBench: Measuring How Agentic Auto-Research Fuels Pseudoscience
summary: As Large Language Model based agents enter autonomous scientific research, their ability to resist pseudoscience becomes increasingly important. Otherwise, such systems may rapidly generate plausible yet misleading studies that contaminate academic literature and erode trust in science. We present PseudoBench, an adversarial benchmark for evaluating whether agentic auto-research systems can identify and resist pseudoscientific narratives. PseudoBench contains 200 curated pseudoscientific claim-evidence pairs across five domains and evaluates agents through an end-to-end research pipeline from experiments to writing. Testing seven state-of-the-art agents, we find that current systems readily produce persuasive reports that align with pseudoscientific premises with near-zero refusal rates and the highest resistance of only 27.4%. Stronger agents risk packaging pseudoscience in more sophisticated scientific language, increasing its apparent credibility. These findings reveal an alarming capacity to fuel pseudoscience, calling for scientific alignment before widespread deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PseudoBench, a benchmark to test whether autonomous LLM-based research agents can recognize and resist pseudoscientific narratives, finding that current agents frequently generate convincing pseudoscientific reports with very low refusal rates.
AI.motivation: As LLM-based autonomous agents begin to conduct scientific research tasks, there is a serious risk they may uncritically adopt and amplify pseudoscientific claims, generating plausible-looking but misleading studies that could contaminate the scientific record and erode public trust. There is currently a lack of systematic tools to stress-test these agents’ robustness against pseudoscience.
AI.method: The authors construct PseudoBench, an adversarial benchmark consisting of 200 carefully curated pseudoscientific claim–evidence pairs spanning five different domains. They then evaluate seven state-of-the-art agentic auto-research systems on an end-to-end pipeline—from planning and running experiments to writing up reports—measuring how often agents resist versus endorse the pseudoscientific narratives.
AI.result: Across the benchmark, all tested agents frequently produced persuasive research reports that accepted and supported the embedded pseudoscientific premises. Refusal rates (i.e., explicit rejection of pseudoscience) were close to zero for most systems, and even the best-performing agent showed only 27.4% resistance to pseudoscientific narratives. More capable agents tended to wrap the pseudoscientific content in more sophisticated and credible-sounding scientific language.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM-based autonomous research agents exhibit a pronounced vulnerability to pseudoscientific narratives and can inadvertently generate credible-looking but misleading scientific outputs. This creates a substantial risk of amplifying pseudoscience if such systems are widely deployed without additional safeguards. The authors argue that “scientific alignment” mechanisms—explicitly aligning agents with scientific standards and skepticism—are urgently needed before broad real-world use.

title: ReproRepo: Scaling Reproducibility Audits with GitHub Repository Issues
summary: Reproducing research results from papers and released code is central to scientific progress. Existing works have introduced benchmarks to evaluate whether LLM agents can assist with reproducibility, but they are difficult to scale due to their reliance on substantial manual effort for data curation and evaluation. We introduce ReproRepo, a scalable framework for reproducibility evaluation that leverages human-raised GitHub issues as naturally occurring supervision on realistic reproduction blockers. We instantiate ReproRepo on 1,149 recent machine learning papers from major conferences and evaluate four frontier model-agent configurations. Our results show that LLM agents, even without executing code, can identify many real-world reproducibility problems from paper-repository pairs: the best agent in our study, namely Codex with GPT-5.5, surfaces at least one semantically related human-reported blocker for ~90% of papers in the study. Further analysis shows that agents are particularly effective for surfacing visible failures and identifying the right semantic region, but may still be insufficient in exact localization. ReproRepo can serve as a reusable, scalable framework for future evaluations of LLM agents on real-world reproducibility auditing. Our code is released at https://github.com/LithiumDA/ReproRepo.

AI.tldr: ReproRepo is a scalable benchmark/framework that uses real GitHub issues to test how well LLM agents can detect real-world reproducibility problems in ML papers and their code, showing that strong agents can flag semantically related issues for about 90% of papers.
AI.motivation: Reproducing results from ML papers is vital but difficult. Existing LLM benchmarks for helping with reproducibility require heavy manual curation of tasks and labels, which limits their scale and realism. The authors want a scalable, realistic way to evaluate LLM agents on actual reproducibility issues that researchers face in practice.
AI.method: They build ReproRepo, a framework that mines human-raised GitHub issues from repositories associated with ML papers and treats them as natural supervision signals about real reproduction blockers. They instantiate it on 1,149 recent ML papers from major conferences, linking each paper with its code repo and associated issues. Then they evaluate four configurations of frontier LLM agents (e.g., Codex with GPT-5.5) that analyze paper–repository pairs without executing code, and measure whether the agents can surface issues that are semantically aligned with human-reported blockers.
AI.result: Across the 1,149 ML papers, the best-performing agent (Codex with GPT-5.5) is able to surface at least one semantically related issue corresponding to a human-reported reproducibility blocker for roughly 90% of the papers. The analysis shows the agents are good at detecting visible failures and narrowing down the semantically relevant parts of the repo or paper, but less precise at pinpointing the exact location or root cause.
AI.conclusion: ReproRepo offers a reusable, scalable, and realistic evaluation framework for testing LLM agents on reproducibility auditing of ML research. Current LLM agents can already detect many real-world reproducibility problems without running code, but they still struggle with exact localization of the underlying issues. The framework can support future work on improving and benchmarking LLM-based reproducibility tools.

title: Agentic AI-based Framework for Mitigating Premature Diagnostic Handoff and Silent Hallucination in Healthcare Applications
summary:
Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) and multi-agent systems have driven the rise of Agentic AI, showing promise for medical reasoning. However, open-ended conversational agents remain prone to two critical failure modes: premature diagnostic handoff and silent clinical hallucinations that may go undetected before reaching the patient. In this work, we propose a multi-agent framework that addresses both issues by replacing ``LLM-as-a-judge'' routing with deterministic orchestration constraints. The framework incorporates two safety mechanisms. First, a neuro-symbolic state-tracking gate enforces completeness of the OLDCARTS clinical protocol (Onset, Location, Duration, Character, Aggravating/Alleviating factors, Radiation, Timing, and Severity) by blocking diagnostic transitions until all required dimensions are collected. Second, an epistemic uncertainty quantification (UQ) gate computes semantic entropy (H) across K=5 independent diagnostic samples to identify and intercept divergent outputs before delivery.
  We evaluate the system using simulated patient agents powered by the llama-3.1-70b-instruct model on 150 test cases. The full architecture achieves 49.3% diagnostic precision, representing an absolute improvement of 11.3 percentage points over an unconstrained baseline. Additionally, we observe a statistically significant negative correlation (r = -0.181, p < 0.05) between OLDCARTS completeness (σ) and semantic entropy (H), suggesting that structured information gathering is associated with reduced diagnostic uncertainty.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a safety-focused multi-agent framework for LLM-based medical diagnosis that enforces structured history-taking and filters uncertain diagnoses, yielding higher diagnostic precision.
AI.motivation: LLM-based conversational medical agents can make two dangerous mistakes: stopping data collection too early and producing confident but incorrect diagnoses that are hard to detect. Existing "LLM-as-a-judge" routing is brittle and non-deterministic, so the authors aim to design a more reliable, structured orchestration mechanism that mitigates these risks in agentic medical reasoning.
AI.method: They build a multi-agent system with deterministic orchestration constraints and two safety gates. (1) A neuro-symbolic state-tracking gate checks compliance with the OLDCARTS clinical history protocol and blocks any diagnostic step until all required symptom dimensions are gathered. (2) An epistemic uncertainty quantification gate samples five independent diagnostic outputs from the model, computes semantic entropy over them, and intercepts highly divergent (high-entropy) diagnoses before they can be delivered. The system is tested using simulated patients implemented with llama-3.1-70b-instruct across 150 clinical cases.
AI.result: The full architecture reaches 49.3% diagnostic precision, an 11.3 percentage-point absolute gain over an unconstrained baseline. They also find a statistically significant negative correlation (r = -0.181, p < 0.05) between OLDCARTS completeness and semantic entropy, indicating that more complete structured information gathering tends to reduce diagnostic uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic, protocol-enforcing orchestration combined with semantic-entropy-based uncertainty gating can substantially improve the reliability of LLM-driven medical diagnostic agents. Structured clinical questioning (OLDCARTS) appears to reduce epistemic uncertainty, supporting the use of explicit clinical protocols and UQ mechanisms in safe Agentic AI for healthcare.

title: Variable-Width Transformers
summary: Scaling model size, specifically depth and width, has driven significant progress in transformer-based language models. However, most architectures maintain a constant width across all layers, allocating a fixed parameter and computation budget evenly despite different layers potentially playing distinct computational roles. In this work, we empirically investigate nonuniform capacity allocation across network depth by proposing a $\times$-shaped > <former architecture. This design maintains wider early and late layers while narrowing the middle layers, utilizing a parameter-free residual resizing mechanism. Across decoder-only language models ranging from 200M to 2B parameters (dense) and 3B parameters (MoE), our > <former consistently outperforms parameter-matched uniform baselines on language modeling loss. By reducing the average layer width, this architecture also requires fewer overall FLOPs (22% reduction under fitted loss-matched scaling curves) and smaller KV cache memory and I/O cost (15% reduction). In analysis, we show that this bottleneck structure results in qualitatively different representations in residual streams. Overall, our results demonstrate that nonuniform width allocation can result in more resource-optimal scaling of language models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a nonuniform-width (×-shaped) Transformer that widens early and late layers and narrows middle layers, improving loss, FLOPs, and KV cache efficiency over standard uniform-width models.
AI.motivation: Standard Transformer language models keep the same width across all layers, implicitly assuming each layer should get an equal parameter and compute budget. Yet different depths likely play different computational roles, so constant width may be suboptimal for performance and efficiency. The authors aim to test whether redistributing capacity across depth can yield better performance–compute tradeoffs.
AI.method: They introduce a ×-shaped Transformer architecture where the network is wide at shallow and deep layers and narrow in the middle, implemented via a parameter-free residual resizing mechanism to change the residual stream width between layers. They train decoder-only LMs with this architecture from 200M to 2B parameters (dense) and a 3B MoE, and compare them against parameter-matched standard uniform-width baselines on language modeling tasks. They also fit scaling curves to compare loss at matched compute and analyze residual stream representations to understand representational differences induced by the bottleneck.
AI.result: Across all tested model sizes (200M–2B dense and 3B MoE), the ×-shaped Transformer consistently achieves lower language modeling loss than uniform-width baselines at matched parameter counts. Due to reduced average layer width, the models require fewer total FLOPs (about 22% reduction along loss-matched scaling curves) and yield roughly 15% smaller KV cache memory and I/O costs. Representation analysis shows qualitatively different residual stream behavior in the bottlenecked middle layers versus wide ends.
AI.conclusion: Nonuniform width allocation across depth—specifically widening early and late layers while narrowing the middle—can make Transformer language models more parameter- and compute-efficient than standard constant-width designs. Bottlenecked, ×-shaped architectures offer better loss–compute tradeoffs and more resource-optimal scaling, suggesting that future scaling strategies should consider depth-wise capacity allocation instead of uniform width.

title: A Unified Framework for Context-Aware and Relation-Aware Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation
comment: Accepted at The ACM Web Conference 2026 (WWW '26)
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has emerged as a paradigm for enhancing large language models (LLMs) with external knowledge, yet existing graph-based methods face a fundamental limitation: entity-centric and chunk-centric approaches operate on representations anchored to original text without true knowledge fusion. While entity-centric methods connect logically related content and chunk-centric methods preserve context, both retrieve information separately through similarity search, missing emergent understanding from their synthesis. In this paper, we propose HyGRAG, a hierarchical graph RAG framework that transcends source documents by addressing three core challenges: constructing summaries that genuinely integrate contextual and relational information, leveraging these synthesized representations to access emergent knowledge during retrieval, and efficiently updating hierarchical structures for dynamic corpora. Specifically, we design hierarchical index structures over hybrid graphs with both chunk and entity nodes, then iteratively cluster them and generate LLM-based summaries. Then, we design context and relation-aware retrieval that searches across all abstraction levels while expanding through community membership. Moreover, we enable dynamic knowledge update through attachment-based algorithms with only local re-summarization. Experimental results show that HyGRAG improves the average accuracy of multi-hop reasoning tasks by 9.7%, while maintaining reasonable efficiency.

AI.tldr: HyGRAG is a hierarchical graph-based RAG framework that fuses chunk and entity information into multi-level summaries to better support multi-hop reasoning, with efficient updates for dynamic corpora.
AI.motivation: Existing graph-based RAG methods are limited because they remain anchored to original text, either focusing on entities (good for logical connections but weak on context) or on chunks (good for local context but weak on cross-chunk relations). Both rely on independent similarity search and fail to capture emergent knowledge that arises when information is synthesized across documents. There is also a need to keep such structures up to date efficiently as the underlying corpus changes.
AI.method: Construct a hybrid graph where both text chunks and entities are nodes, then build hierarchical index structures over this graph. Iteratively cluster nodes at each level and use an LLM to generate summaries that integrate both contextual and relational information, creating increasingly abstract representations. During retrieval, perform context- and relation-aware search across all abstraction levels, expanding via graph community membership to capture relevant but indirectly connected information. For dynamic corpora, use attachment-based update algorithms that modify only affected local regions of the hierarchy and re-summarize them instead of rebuilding the entire structure.
AI.result: On multi-hop reasoning benchmarks, HyGRAG achieves a 9.7% average accuracy improvement over baselines while keeping retrieval and update efficiency within practical bounds.
AI.conclusion: By hierarchically summarizing a hybrid chunk-entity graph and using multi-level, relation-aware retrieval with efficient local updates, HyGRAG overcomes the limitations of prior entity-centric and chunk-centric graph RAG methods and better exploits emergent knowledge, significantly improving multi-hop reasoning performance at reasonable cost.

title: IsabeLLM: Automated Theorem Proving Applied to Formally Verifying Consensus
summary: Advances in Artificial Intelligence (AI) have led AI for Theorem Proving to become a promising means of formally verifying computer systems.
