Whilst formal verification is traditionally reserved for safety-critical systems due to the required amount of expertise and effort, AI can help to automate a large amount of this workload and make it far more accessible. Blockchain-based systems are becoming increasingly popular and are frequently targeted by malicious actors, often resulting in huge financial losses, highlighting the need to better verify these systems and mitigate vulnerabilities. Arguably the most important component of these systems is the consensus protocol, which allows nodes to agree on decisions in a potentially adversarial environment. In this paper, we improve upon IsabeLLM, the automated theorem proving tool in Isabelle. Namely, we implement a Retrieval-Augmented Generation framework, Error tracing and counterexample generation for improved context supplied to the Large Language Model. Compatibility with the latest version of Isabelle and Sledgehammer is also implemented for improved efficiency. We compare the performance of the two versions of IsabeLLM in their ability to complete the verification of Bitcoin's Proof of Work consensus.

AI.tldr: The paper enhances an AI-assisted theorem proving tool (IsabeLLM) in Isabelle and evaluates it by verifying Bitcoin’s Proof-of-Work consensus protocol.
AI.motivation: Formal verification is powerful but costly and requires deep expertise, so it has mostly been applied only to safety-critical systems. AI can reduce the manual burden and make theorem proving more accessible. At the same time, blockchain systems, especially their consensus protocols, increasingly suffer from high-impact vulnerabilities and financial losses, motivating stronger formal verification. The authors focus on improving AI support in Isabelle so that complex protocols like Bitcoin’s PoW consensus can be more efficiently and reliably verified.
AI.method: The authors extend the IsabeLLM tool in Isabelle with several capabilities: (1) a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework to better supply relevant formal context to the large language model; (2) error tracing and counterexample generation to detect and diagnose failed proof attempts; and (3) compatibility with the latest Isabelle and Sledgehammer versions to leverage improved underlying automation. They then compare the original and enhanced IsabeLLM on the task of completing an existing formal verification of Bitcoin’s Proof-of-Work consensus protocol.
AI.result: The improved IsabeLLM shows better performance than the original version on the verification of Bitcoin’s Proof-of-Work consensus, though the abstract does not quantify gains. The added RAG, error tracing, counterexamples, and updated toolchain compatibility collectively enhance the tool’s ability to assist in finishing the formal proof.
AI.conclusion: By upgrading IsabeLLM with retrieval-augmented context provision, diagnostic tools, and modern Isabelle/Sledgehammer support, AI-assisted theorem proving becomes more effective for verifying complex blockchain consensus protocols. This suggests that AI for theorem proving can help bring formal verification to broader, non-traditional domains such as cryptocurrencies by lowering the required expertise and effort.

title: Trust the Right Teacher: Quality-Aware Self-Distillation for GUI Grounding
summary: Graphical user interface (GUI) grounding requires vision-language models (VLMs) to identify small target elements in high-resolution screenshots and predict precise screen coordinates. On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) is a promising post-training approach for this coordinate-sensitive task, since it provides dense token-level teacher signals beyond hard coordinate labels. However, naive OPSD is not well suited to GUI grounding: OPSD evaluates the teacher on student-generated prefixes, the quality of coordinate-token teacher signals can degrade when the prefix has already deviated from the target coordinate, leading to unreliable teacher signal. To mitigate this, We propose quality-aware self-distillation for VLM-based GUI grounding, which improves coordinate-token teacher-signal quality through soft correctness-aware gating and teacher-probability scaling. The soft correctness-aware gate checks whether the teacher's current coordinate-token prediction can still be completed into the ground-truth box under the student-generated prefix. If not, the corresponding teacher signal is down-weighted. Teacher-probability scaling then uses the teacher's confidence as a lightweight factor to further calibrate the strength of the gated supervision. A key empirical finding is that neither component alone improves overall performance, whereas combining them consistently improves performance. This suggests that the two mechanisms play complementary roles: correctness-aware gating suppresses unreliable coordinate-token supervision, while teacher-probability scaling calibrates the strength of the remaining signals. Experiments across six GUI grounding benchmarks show that our method consistently improves the base model and outperforms strong baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a quality-aware self-distillation method to improve GUI grounding in vision-language models by refining how teacher signals supervise coordinate predictions.
AI.motivation: GUI grounding requires accurate prediction of small UI elements and their precise coordinates in high-resolution screenshots. Existing on-policy self-distillation can provide dense supervision signals, but its effectiveness drops when the model’s predicted coordinate tokens deviate from the ground truth, leading to noisy and unreliable teacher guidance for this coordinate-sensitive task.
AI.method: The authors introduce quality-aware self-distillation for VLM-based GUI grounding. It adds (1) a soft correctness-aware gating mechanism that checks whether the teacher’s current coordinate-token prediction can still be completed into the true bounding box given the student’s prefix, and down-weights signals that fail this check; and (2) teacher-probability scaling, which uses the teacher’s confidence scores to further scale the strength of the surviving supervision signals. Distillation is thus guided by both structural correctness and probabilistic confidence of coordinate predictions.
AI.result: Across six GUI grounding benchmarks, the proposed method consistently improves performance over the base VLM and surpasses strong OPSD and other baseline methods, showing better coordinate prediction accuracy and robustness.
AI.conclusion: Combining correctness-aware gating with probability-based scaling yields higher-quality coordinate-token teacher signals for GUI grounding. The two mechanisms are complementary—gating filters out unreliable supervision while scaling properly weights the remaining signals—leading to consistent performance gains on multiple benchmarks.

title: First Proof Second Batch
summary:
To assess the ability of current AI systems to correctly solve research-level mathematics problems, we tested several AI systems on a set of ten problems in a broad range of mathematical fields; these problems arose naturally in the research process of the contributors. This document includes the problems, our methodology, and the results of our testing. We provide links to supplementary documents including the human solutions, the AI-generated solutions, and the referee reports and logs for the AI-generated solutions.
  The ten problems were contributed by the following mathematicians: (1) Dariusz Kalociński and Theodore A. Slaman, (2) Richard Schwartz, (3) Aleksa Milojevic and Benny Sudakov, (4) Larry Guth, (5) Oleg Butkovsky, Jonathan Mattingly, and Lorenzo Zambotti, (6) Joshua Evan Greene and Duncan McCoy, (7) Sucharit Sarkar, (8) Sam Payne and Jidong (Jayden) Wang, (9) Sylvie Corteel and John Lentfer, (10) Srivatsav Kunnawalkam Elayavalli.

AI.tldr: Evaluation of current AI systems on ten authentic research-level math problems from diverse fields.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to empirically measure how well modern AI systems can tackle genuine, unsimplified research problems in mathematics, as opposed to standard benchmark or textbook-style questions.
AI.method: The authors collected ten problems that naturally arose in ongoing mathematical research from ten sets of contributors in different subfields. They then posed these problems to several AI systems, recorded the AI-generated solutions, and obtained referee reports and logs to rigorously assess correctness and quality.
AI.result: The study produced a dataset of AI solutions and human referee assessments, organized by problem, along with documentation of the testing methodology. Specific performance statistics are not given in the abstract but can be found in supplementary materials.
AI.conclusion: Current AI systems were systematically evaluated on real research-level math questions, providing evidence and detailed artifacts (solutions, reports, logs) that can be used to understand their present capabilities and limitations and to guide future improvements.

title: Knowledge Reutilization in Meta-Reinforcement Learning
summary: Meta-reinforcement learning enables fast adaptation by extracting shared structure from related tasks, but existing end-to-end methods often couple task inference with embodiment-specific control. This coupling can obscure non-parametric task semantics, reduce sample efficiency, and limit cross-agent reuse. We propose a meta-knowledge reutilization framework that learns task-level knowledge on a dynamics-simplified agent and transfers it to heterogeneous agents. The framework uses a Bayesian non-parametric prior to organize latent task modes and a high-level policy to generate task-level magnitude guidance. To bridge reusable task knowledge with different embodiments, we introduce a semantic-magnitude interface and a lightweight temporal adaptor, which convert frozen meta-knowledge into temporally aligned subgoals for embodiment-specific low-level controllers. Experiments on multiple locomotion agents show that our framework reduces final-step tracking error by 94.75% -- 99.79% compared with recent state-of-the-art baselines and achieves comparable deployment performance with about 23.8% of their interaction data.

AI.tldr: A framework for meta-reinforcement learning that separates reusable task knowledge from embodiment-specific control, enabling efficient transfer of task-level meta-knowledge across heterogeneous agents.
AI.motivation: Existing meta-RL methods often entangle task inference with specific robot embodiments, which hides clear task semantics, hurts sample efficiency, and prevents reusing knowledge across different agents. The authors want a way to learn task-level meta-knowledge once, on a simple agent, and then reuse it on many different, more complex embodiments.
AI.method: They introduce a meta-knowledge reutilization framework where meta-training is done on a dynamics-simplified agent. A Bayesian non-parametric prior is used to discover and organize latent task modes, and a high-level policy outputs task-level magnitude guidance. To link this reusable knowledge to various agents, they define a semantic-magnitude interface plus a lightweight temporal adaptor that convert the frozen meta-knowledge into temporally aligned subgoals, which are then executed by embodiment-specific low-level controllers.
AI.result: On multiple locomotion agents, the method dramatically improves performance: it reduces final-step tracking error by roughly 95%–100% relative to strong state-of-the-art baselines, and matches their deployment performance while using only about 23.8% of their interaction data.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling task-level meta-knowledge from embodiment-specific control via a semantic-magnitude interface and temporal adaptor allows learned meta-knowledge to be reused across heterogeneous agents, yielding much higher sample efficiency and lower tracking error in meta-RL locomotion tasks.

title: Your AI Travel Agent Would Book You a Bullfight: An Agentic Benchmark for Implicit Animal Welfare in Frontier AI Models
summary: AI agents are moving from advisors to actors, booking travel, planning menus, and running procurement on behalf of users. Existing benchmarks for AI and animal welfare evaluate model text responses to question-answer prompts, leaving open whether the welfare reasoning surfaced in those responses transfers to agentic deployment where the model must take actions with tools. We introduce TAC (Travel Agent Compassion), the first agentic benchmark measuring whether AI agents avoid options involving animal exploitation when acting on behalf of users. TAC presents an AI agent with twelve hand-authored travel booking scenarios across six categories of animal exploitation, augmented to forty-eight samples to control for price, rating, and position confounds. We evaluate seven frontier models from four labs. Every model scores below the chance level of sixty-four percent, with the best performer (Claude Opus 4.7) at fifty-three percent. A single welfare-aware sentence in the system prompt yields gains of forty-seven to sixty-three percentage points in Claude and GPT-5.5, twenty-six points in GPT-5.2, and under twelve points in DeepSeek and Gemini. An auxiliary Inspect Scout audit of 288 base-condition transcripts from the top two performers, using Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite as judge, flags zero transcripts for evaluation awareness, suggesting the below-chance rates do not stem from the models recognising the evaluation. We discuss implications for category-level variation across cultural domains, the limits of text-response welfare benchmarks, and the EU General-Purpose AI Code of Practice systemic risk framework.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TAC (Travel Agent Compassion), the first benchmark to test whether AI agents, when acting through tools in booking travel, avoid animal-exploitative options. It finds that current frontier models perform below chance, but simple welfare-aware prompt cues can substantially raise performance for some models.
AI.motivation: As AI agents increasingly act autonomously (e.g., booking travel, planning menus, handling procurement), it is unclear whether their apparent concern for animal welfare in text-based Q&A evaluations actually carries over to real tool-using behavior. Existing benchmarks only test written answers, not actions. The authors aim to close this gap and assess whether agentic deployment respects animal welfare when agents choose among concrete options.
AI.method: The authors build TAC (Travel Agent Compassion), an agentic benchmark consisting of 12 manually created travel booking scenarios spanning six types of animal exploitation. These are augmented to 48 samples that control for confounds like price, ratings, and item position. AI agents must choose options via tools rather than just answer questions. Seven frontier models from four labs are evaluated. The authors also test the effect of adding a single welfare-aware sentence in the system prompt and run an Inspect Scout-style audit on 288 transcripts judged by Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite to check for evaluation awareness.
AI.result: All seven models perform below the random-choice baseline of 64% avoidance of exploitative options; the best model, Claude Opus 4.7, reaches only 53% in the base condition. Adding a single welfare-focused sentence to the system prompt yields large gains for some models: Claude and GPT-5.5 improve by 47–63 percentage points, GPT-5.2 by 26 points, and DeepSeek and Gemini by less than 12 points. The audit detects no signs that models are gaming the benchmark via evaluation awareness.
AI.conclusion: Agentic AI systems currently fail, often worse than chance, to avoid animal-exploitative options in realistic travel-booking scenarios, indicating that welfare reasoning shown in text Q&A does not reliably translate into action. Simple prompt-level interventions can substantially improve some models’ welfare behavior, but effects vary by model family. The findings highlight weaknesses of existing text-only welfare benchmarks, suggest category and cultural domain variation in model behavior, and bear on systemic risk discussions in frameworks such as the EU General-Purpose AI Code of Practice.

title: Memory as a Wasting Asset: Pricing Flash Endurance for Embodied Agents, and the Limits of Doing So
summary:
A robot's flash endurance is a non-renewable stock: every persisted write spends one of a few thousand program/erase cycles and never refills, yet no fielded robot memory system prices which memories are worth an erase cycle. We treat embodied memory as depreciating capital and price that stock with a single endurance shadow price $η$, which makes cost-minimizing placement across a RAM / on-board NVM / cloud hierarchy a threshold in a wear-augmented per-byte index. The index is cost-optimal whatever the sign of the value-write association $χ$; only when $χ> 0$ does the optimum turn non-monotone, sending a robot's most valuable memories off its flash.
  The pivot is thus empirical, and we measure $χ$ on real robot logs at a pre-specified gate: its sign is a property of the deployment regime -- positive on recurrent long-horizon manipulation ($\hatχ \approx +1.0 \times 10^{-3}$, replicated at full power), null on a shorter-horizon suite, and negative on non-recurrent teleoperation. Two boundaries scope the result. The endurance budget is dormant on premium 3,000-P/E TLC at datasheet prices and binding on the commodity QLC/eMMC ($\sim$1,000 P/E) that cheaper edge robots run. And where it binds, a learned wear-aware controller only ties price-based routing on task value, because realized value is tier-invariant across RAM, NVM, and cloud: the rent governs device lifetime and cost, not task performance. Whether wear-aware placement improves task value remains open -- $χ$ is measured against a value proxy, and the non-monotone optimum, while proven, is not yet observed in data.

AI.tldr: The paper models flash memory endurance in robots as a consumable capital resource and derives a cost-optimal policy for placing data across RAM, on-board non-volatile memory, and cloud, based on a single shadow price of wear and an empirically measured relation between data value and write frequency.
AI.motivation: Robot flash memories have limited program/erase (P/E) cycles, but existing systems treat all writes as free and do not account for the long-term cost of wearing out on-board storage. As robots increasingly use cheap, low-endurance flash and store large logs or learned models, there is a need to treat memory endurance as a scarce resource and to design placement policies that minimize total lifecycle cost without hurting task performance.
AI.method: The authors model flash endurance as depreciating capital and introduce a single endurance shadow price η that internalizes the cost of consuming program/erase cycles. They formulate a wear-augmented per-byte index that incorporates this shadow price to guide cost-minimizing data placement across RAM, on-board NVM, and cloud tiers. They analyze the index’s optimality under different signs of the empirical correlation χ between data value and write frequency, proving that the optimal placement becomes non-monotone (pushing the most valuable data off flash) when χ>0. They then estimate χ from real robot logs under different deployment regimes and evaluate when the endurance constraint is binding for different flash technologies (TLC vs QLC/eMMC), comparing a learned wear-aware controller against simple price-based routing.
AI.result: The analysis shows that the proposed index yields cost-optimal placement across the memory hierarchy for any sign of χ, with a qualitatively different, non-monotone structure when χ>0. Empirical measurements on real robot logs indicate χ is positive and small but statistically clear for recurrent, long-horizon manipulation (around +1.0×10^-3 and replicated), approximately zero for shorter-horizon tasks, and negative for non-recurrent teleoperation. The endurance constraint is essentially slack for higher-end 3,000-P/E TLC devices at their datasheet prices, but becomes binding for cheaper QLC/eMMC devices with about 1,000 P/E cycles, which are common in low-cost edge robots. In the binding regime, a learned wear-aware controller performs similarly to price-based routing in terms of realized task value, because value does not depend on which tier stores the data, but it affects device lifetime and cost via wear distribution.
AI.conclusion: Endurance in robot flash memory can and should be modeled as a priced, depreciating resource, and a single shadow price η is sufficient to derive a simple, threshold-based index for optimal data placement across RAM, on-board NVM, and cloud. The empirical sign and magnitude of the value–write correlation χ depend strongly on the deployment regime, which determines whether the theoretically possible non-monotone optimal placement (evicting highest-value data from flash) is relevant. For premium, high-endurance flash the endurance budget is effectively non-binding, while for commodity low-endurance flash it is important for cost and device lifetime, though current data show no task-performance gain from wear-aware placement. Whether wear-aware policies can improve realized task value, and whether the predicted non-monotone optimum appears in richer deployments, remain open questions.

title: WEQA: Wearable hEalth Question Answering with Query-Adaptive Agentic Reasoning
summary: Language models are remarkably capable at medical question answering, in some cases surpassing the accuracy of general physicians. However, answering questions about wearable health data remains challenging and understudied, as these ubiquitous sensors produce continuous, high-dimensional, and longitudinal data, which is non-trivial to align with text-centric distributions in LLM pretraining. The diversity of sensor modalities and user intents cannot be effectively handled by a fixed reasoning workflow or a single pretrained foundation model. To address these challenges, we propose WEQA, a query-adaptive agent framework that unifies LLM reasoning with specialized wearable analytical and modeling tools. An LLM controller is employed to synthesize execution plans and dynamically route each query to the appropriate combination of sensor analysis and pretrained models, and perform grounded response auditing with external knowledge. We also curate a benchmark spanning four open wearable datasets comprising analytic and predictive tasks in three different health domains. Experiments show that our framework is 24% more accurate than LLM and agentic baselines, and a blinded study with 12 medical experts and 8 users shows substantial gains in usefulness and clinical soundness.

AI.tldr: WEQA is a query-adaptive LLM-agent framework that combines large language models with specialized wearable data tools to better answer medical questions based on wearable sensor data, outperforming standard LLM and agent baselines and being judged more clinically useful by experts and users.
AI.motivation: Existing language models can answer many medical questions but struggle with wearable health data, which is continuous, high-dimensional, longitudinal, and comes from diverse sensors and user goals. Fixed reasoning pipelines or a single foundation model cannot flexibly handle this diversity or align sensor data with text-based LLM pretraining, motivating a framework that can adaptively integrate sensor analytics, predictive models, and medical knowledge for each query.
AI.method: The authors introduce WEQA, a query-adaptive agent framework. An LLM acts as a controller that, for each user query, plans an execution workflow, chooses and orchestrates appropriate wearable data analysis tools and pretrained models, and conducts grounded auditing of responses against external knowledge sources. They also build a benchmark using four open wearable datasets across three health domains, containing both analytic and predictive tasks, to systematically evaluate the framework.
AI.result: On the curated benchmark, WEQA achieves 24% higher accuracy than strong LLM-only and agentic baselines. In a blinded evaluation with 12 medical experts and 8 lay users, WEQA’s answers are rated substantially higher in usefulness and clinical soundness compared to the baselines.
AI.conclusion: A query-adaptive LLM-agent framework that integrates wearable analytics and specialized models can significantly improve medical question answering over wearable data, both in quantitative accuracy and in expert-perceived clinical quality, demonstrating a promising direction for deploying LLMs in wearable health applications.

title: Learning Cardiac Electrophysiology Digital Twins Through Agentic Discovery of Hybrid Structure
summary: Building personalized cardiac electrophysiology (EP) digital twins requires identifying the appropriate model structure for each patient, not merely fitting parameters. Traditional methods rely on experts to manually prescribe hybrid physics-neural architectures, which requires deep domain expertise and does not transfer across patients. Recent works have applied large language models (LLMs) to generate or act as hybrid models. However, despite their promising generalization capacity, these LLM-based methods lack the structural priors needed for stable cardiac simulations. Hence, we propose LEADS, a framework that formulates cardiac EP domain knowledge as a structured action space and utilizes an LLM agent to discover hybrid models. The agent follows an iterative reasoning-and-action loop to select, combine, and refine hybrid models, whilst gradient descent handles parameter fitting. The proposed LEADS designs every candidate model towards physically grounded, interpretable, and numerically stable, while allowing open-ended architectural discovery. We validate LEADS on synthetic data with three ground-truth reaction models and on real cardiac EP data, demonstrating that it outperforms both human-designed hybrid models and other LLM-based hybrid modeling.

AI.tldr: They build an LLM-driven framework (LEADS) that automatically designs stable, interpretable hybrid physics–ML models for patient-specific cardiac electrophysiology and beats existing human- and LLM-designed baselines.
AI.motivation: Personalized cardiac EP digital twins need not just parameter fitting but the right model structure per patient. Manually crafting suitable hybrid physics–ML models is expertise-heavy, time-consuming, and not easily transferable across patients. Existing LLM-based hybrid modeling lacks strong physical/structural priors, leading to unstable or unphysical cardiac simulations. There is a need for an automated way to discover patient-specific hybrid models that respect cardiac EP domain knowledge and remain numerically stable while retaining the flexibility and generalization of LLMs.
AI.method: They introduce LEADS, which encodes cardiac EP domain knowledge into a structured action space that constrains and guides model design. An LLM agent operates in this space in an iterative reasoning-and-action loop: it proposes, combines, and refines candidate hybrid model architectures, while gradient descent is used separately to fit the model parameters. The action space and design rules ensure that all candidate models are physically grounded, interpretable, and numerically stable, yet still allow open-ended architectural exploration.
AI.result: On synthetic datasets built from three known ground-truth reaction models and on real cardiac EP datasets, LEADS discovers hybrid models that achieve better performance than expert-crafted hybrid physics–ML models and prior LLM-based hybrid modeling approaches. The improvements hold across both synthetic and real-world evaluation settings.
AI.conclusion: Encoding domain knowledge as a structured action space and using an LLM agent to search this space is an effective strategy for discovering personalized, stable, and interpretable hybrid models in cardiac electrophysiology. LEADS demonstrates that automated, LLM-driven model structure discovery can surpass manual expert designs and less constrained LLM-based approaches, suggesting a promising paradigm for building digital twins in cardiac EP and potentially other scientific domains.

title: DRFLOW: A Deep Research Benchmark for Personalized Workflow Prediction
summary: Deep research (DR) systems are increasingly used for complex information-seeking tasks, but existing works mainly focus on generating reports and summaries. In contrast, many enterprise tasks instead require an agent to identify concrete workflows which is a sequence of action-steps. For example, rather than summarizing budgeting policies, an agent should be able to determine the steps needed to answer a question such as: "How do I request new headcount given a fixed budget?". Therefore, we introduce DRFLOW, a benchmark for evaluating personalized workflows predicted by agents from heterogeneous sources. Each task requires the agent to identify relevant evidence from scattered sources, then use that evidence to predict the correct action-step sequence for the user's task. DRFLOW contains 100 tasks across five domains, with 1,246 reference workflow steps grounded in more than 3,900 sources. We define seven diagnostic metrics covering factual grounding, step recovery, structural ordering, condition resolution, and personalization. We further present DRFLOW-Agent (DRFA), a workflow-oriented reference agent to predict personalized workflow. We show that although DRFA improves over strong baseline agents (upto 10.02% average F1 score), there is substantial room for improvement remains across these workflow metrics, indicating that predicting complete and correct personalized workflows remains a challenging frontier for deep research.

AI.tldr: Introduces DRFLOW, a benchmark and agent for predicting personalized, step-by-step workflows for complex enterprise-like tasks from heterogeneous information sources.
AI.motivation: Most deep research systems focus on generating summaries or reports, but many real-world enterprise tasks require concrete, actionable workflows (ordered steps) rather than prose summaries. Existing benchmarks and agents do not adequately test or support this need, especially when information is scattered across many heterogeneous sources and when workflows must be personalized to the user’s situation.
AI.method: The authors build DRFLOW, a benchmark of 100 tasks spanning five domains, each requiring an agent to: (1) search across heterogeneous, scattered sources; (2) identify relevant evidence; and (3) synthesize that evidence into a personalized sequence of action-steps (a workflow). The dataset includes 1,246 reference workflow steps grounded in over 3,900 sources. They design seven diagnostic evaluation metrics targeting factual grounding, step coverage/recovery, ordering/structure of steps, handling of conditional logic, and personalization. They also implement DRFLOW-Agent (DRFA), a workflow-oriented reference agent, and compare it to strong baseline agents using these metrics.
AI.result: DRFLOW establishes a challenging benchmark for workflow prediction from heterogeneous sources. DRFLOW-Agent outperforms strong baseline agents by up to 10.02% average F1 on the proposed metrics, showing that workflow-oriented design helps. However, performance remains far from perfect across all seven metrics, revealing significant gaps in current agents’ ability to produce complete, correctly ordered, well-grounded, and personalized workflows.
AI.conclusion: Predicting accurate, complete, and personalized workflows for complex information-seeking tasks is significantly harder than generating summaries and remains an open challenge. DRFLOW and DRFLOW-Agent provide a first systematic benchmark and reference approach for this problem, with diagnostic metrics that highlight where agents fail (grounding, step recovery, ordering, conditional handling, personalization). The results suggest substantial headroom for future research on deep research agents that can translate scattered information into executable, user-tailored workflows.

title: The Stanford EDGAR Filings Dataset: Reconstructing U.S. Corporate and Financial Disclosures into Layout-Faithful and Token-Efficient Pretraining Data
comment: Preprint. Includes appendix, tables, and figures
summary: As high-quality public web corpora become increasingly exhausted, clean long-context documents have become a scarce and expensive source of training data for large language models (LLMs). Existing long-context corpora are often proprietary and costly to acquire, synthetically generated, or concentrated in narrow domains such as programming. We introduce the Stanford EDGAR Filings Dataset (SEFD), an open reconstruction of SEC filings into layout-faithful MultiMarkdown for financial language modeling and evaluation. SEFD makes audited financial statements, risk disclosures, ownership reports, accounting notes, and market-moving event filings usable as long-context pretraining data and as a basis for financial reasoning, forecasting, compliance, and document understanding. The resulting corpus is token-efficient, model-ready, and has less than 0.1% overlap with Common Crawl-derived corpora. We release SEFD-v1, a 152B-token initial public snapshot, and provide corpus-level analyses of a larger 18.5M-filing archive estimated at 550B tokens. We further introduce two SEFD-derived benchmarks: EDGAR-Forecast, which evaluates filing-grounded numerical forecasting after model knowledge cutoffs, and EDGAR-OCR, which evaluates transcription of complex financial tables.

AI.tldr: Introduces SEFD, an open, large-scale, layout-faithful SEC filings corpus and two derived benchmarks for long-context financial modeling and evaluation.
AI.motivation: High-quality, clean, long-context public web text suitable for LLM training is running out. Existing long-context corpora are often proprietary, synthetic, or domain-narrow (e.g., code), leaving a gap for open, real-world, document-structured financial text to support pretraining and evaluation, especially for long-context reasoning in finance.
AI.method: Reconstruct SEC EDGAR filings into layout-faithful MultiMarkdown, preserving document structure such as tables and sections, to create a token-efficient, model-ready corpus. Analyze overlap with existing Common Crawl-derived corpora, and perform corpus-level analysis of a larger archive. Build two benchmarks from the corpus: EDGAR-Forecast for filing-grounded numerical forecasting after the model’s knowledge cutoff, and EDGAR-OCR for transcription of complex financial tables.
AI.result: Released SEFD-v1 with 152B tokens and analyzed a broader 18.5M-filing archive estimated at 550B tokens, with less than 0.1% overlap with Common Crawl-derived corpora. Constructed and released two benchmarks, EDGAR-Forecast and EDGAR-OCR, to evaluate financial forecasting and table transcription capabilities of LLMs on long-context filings.
AI.conclusion: SEFD provides an open, large-scale, low-overlap, layout-faithful financial text corpus that is directly usable for long-context pretraining and evaluation. The associated benchmarks enable systematic assessment of LLMs’ financial reasoning, forecasting beyond their training horizon, and complex table understanding, addressing a major gap in public long-context training resources.

title: Fixed-Point Reasoners: Stable and Adaptive Deep Looped Transformers
summary: Looped architectures provide an inductive bias toward learning step-by-step procedures for tasks that require compositional reasoning. The number of effective layers reached by looping determines the quality of the solution these models find. Like deep architectures, looped architectures are prone to a signal propagation problem induced by depth as the halting decision is postponed. In this paper, we address this signal propagation issue using pre-norm layers and residual scaling. Building on these architectural modifications, we propose FPRM, a Transformer-based Fixed-Point Reasoning Model that uses fixed-point convergence as an end-to-end halting mechanism in a looped architecture. We show that fixed-point halting allows FPRM to adapt its compute to task difficulty. FPRM is effective on common reasoning benchmarks, namely Sudoku, Maze, state-tracking, and ARC-AGI.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FPRM, a looped Transformer that uses fixed-point convergence as a halting mechanism, enabling adaptive computation depth for compositional reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Looped neural architectures are well-suited for compositional, step-by-step reasoning, but as they iterate more (becoming effectively deeper), they suffer from signal propagation problems similar to very deep networks. Moreover, existing halting mechanisms can be difficult to train or control. The authors want an architecture that can perform variable-depth, algorithmic-style reasoning while maintaining stable training and using a principled, end-to-end halting rule.
AI.method: They modify looped Transformer architectures with pre-normalization and residual scaling to mitigate depth-related signal degradation. On top of this, they define FPRM (Fixed-Point Reasoning Model), a looped Transformer whose recurrent updates are run until they reach a fixed point, i.e., the representation stops changing significantly. This fixed-point condition serves as an automatic halting criterion, letting the model determine how many loop iterations (effective layers) are needed per input.
AI.result: FPRM can dynamically adjust its computational depth depending on task difficulty. Empirically, it performs well on several compositional reasoning benchmarks such as Sudoku, Maze solving, state-tracking tasks, and ARC-AGI, showing competitive or improved performance relative to baselines while using adaptive compute.
AI.conclusion: By combining architectural changes for stable signal propagation (pre-norm and residual scaling) with a fixed-point convergence-based halting mechanism, FPRM enables looped Transformers to carry out step-by-step, compositional reasoning with adaptive depth. This approach addresses training difficulties in deep/looped models and yields strong results on a range of reasoning benchmarks, suggesting fixed-point halting as a promising principle for adaptive computation in neural networks.

title: EvolveNav: Proactive Preflection and Self-Evolving Memory for Zero-Shot Object Goal Navigation
summary: Zero-Shot Object-Goal Navigation (ZS-OGN) requires embodied agents to explore and locate target objects without any prior training. To this end, recent methods leverage foundation models. But they typically rely on static priors and lack adaptation, which leads to repeated errors and costly trial and error. In this paper, we propose a self-evolving ZS-OGN framework that enables continuous test-time improvement. Specifically, we build an agentic rule memory by extracting actionable knowledge from past trajectories. Then, we propose a retrieval strategy based on upper confidence bound, selecting effective rules by balancing semantic relevance and historical success. In addition, we introduce a memory-guided preflection module that forecasts potential outcomes before action, reducing inefficient exploration. Extensive experiments show that our method outperforms existing zero-shot baselines, achieving a 10.1\% improvement in success rate with fewer unnecessary steps.

AI.tldr: They propose a zero-shot object-goal navigation method that continually improves at test time by learning and reusing rules from past trajectories, leading to higher success with fewer steps.
AI.motivation: Existing zero-shot object-goal navigation methods use large foundation models but rely on static priors. These methods cannot adapt from their own experience during testing, so they keep making similar mistakes and require a lot of trial-and-error exploration. A framework that can continually learn from test-time trajectories and reuse this experience to guide future navigation is needed.
AI.method: They design a self-evolving ZS-OGN framework with three core components: (1) an agentic rule memory that stores actionable knowledge distilled from previous navigation trajectories; (2) a rule retrieval strategy based on an upper confidence bound (UCB), which selects rules by trading off semantic relevance to the current task and past empirical success; and (3) a memory-guided preflection module that predicts the likely outcomes of candidate actions using the retrieved rules, allowing the agent to avoid unpromising explorations before executing actions.
AI.result: In extensive experiments on zero-shot object-goal navigation benchmarks, their approach surpasses existing zero-shot baselines, yielding a 10.1% higher success rate while also reducing unnecessary steps, indicating both better effectiveness and efficiency of navigation.
AI.conclusion: Continual test-time adaptation via rule memory, UCB-based retrieval, and preflection allows zero-shot object-goal navigation agents to improve performance on the fly, outperforming static-foundation-model baselines in both success rate and navigation efficiency.
